2. Construct a template: Cut a cardboard or other material template to match the calculated average dimensions obtained in step 1.

3. Index the map using the template:
- Select the general area on the map that corresponds to the first photo and align it with the photo.
- Place the template over this area and adjust it until it covers as much of the area as possible without exceeding its boundaries.
- Draw lines around the edges of the template on the map to outline the approximate coverage of the photo.

4. Repeat for all photos: Continue using the same template to index each subsequent photo, ensuring consistent placement and alignment.

Notes:
• This method is suitable when exact area coverage is not as critical as general location and approximate size.
• It is efficient for indexing a large number of aerial photographs.
index map,template method,map reading,aerial photo navigation
Define a Map
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
A map is a graphic representation of a portion of the earth’s surface drawn to scale, as seen from above. It uses colors, symbols, and labels to represent features found on the ground. The ideal representation would be realized if...
A map is a graphic representation of a portion of the earth’s surface drawn to scale, as seen from above. It uses colors, symbols, and labels to represent features found on the ground. The ideal representation would be realized if every feature of the area being mapped could be shown in true shape. Obviously this is impossible, and an attempt to plot each feature true-to-scale would result in a product impossible to read even with the aid of a magnifying glass.
map definition, map reading, navigation basics
Determine Azimuths Using a Protractor
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Purpose: To determine the grid azimuth (in degrees or mils) from point A to point B on a map using a protractor.

Materials Required: Standard 1:50,000 scale military map with two known points plotted, protractor, straightedge, and...
Purpose: To determine the grid azimuth (in degrees or mils) from point A to point B on a map using a protractor.

Materials Required: Standard 1:50,000 scale military map with two known points plotted, protractor, straightedge, and pencil.

### Performance Steps:

1. Understand Azimuths:
- An azimuth is the direction from one point to another on a map or ground, measured in degrees or mils clockwise from north.
- In degrees: 0° (or 360°) = North, 90° = East, 180° = South, 270° = West.
- In mils: 0 mils (or 6400 mils) = North, 1600 mils = East, 3200 mils = South, 4800 mils = West.

2. Use the Protractor:
- The Army uses two types of protractors: square (GTA 5-2-12) and semicircular.
- Square protractor has an inner degree scale and outer mil scale.
- Semicircular protractor may have both scales, with the outer row used for azimuths to the East and the inner row for azimuths to the West.

3. Plot Points on Map:
- Plot two points (A and B) on the map using a pencil.

4. Draw Azimuth Line:
- Use a straightedge to draw a straight line from point A to point B.

5. Align Protractor:
- Place the index of the protractor at the point where the azimuth line crosses a vertical (North-South) grid line on the map.

6. Read Azimuth:
- Ensure that the 0-degree or 0-mil mark is toward the top (North) of the map.
- Start reading from the 0-degree or 0-mil mark and move clockwise to where the azimuth line crosses the scale(s).
- Each tick mark on the degree scale = 1°, each tick mark on the mil scale = 20 mils.

7. Record Azimuth:
- Read the value at the point where the azimuth line intersects the protractor scale(s). Example: 65° or 1,150 mils.

8. Adjust for Semicircular Protractor:
- If using a semicircular protractor and point B is to the West of point A, reverse the protractor so that the 0-degree mark points toward the bottom (South) of the map.

### Notes:
• The line drawn between the two points must be long enough to cross the scale(s). If it’s too short, extend the line beyond point B until it crosses the scale(s).
• Ensure that the 0-degree or 0-mil mark is always aligned toward North on the map.

### Evaluation Measures:
• Determine the correct azimuth within ±1 degree or ±20 mils.
azimuth, protractor, map reading, navigation, military map, grid azimuth, degrees, mils
Determine Elevation Using Contour Lines on a Map
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Steps to Determine Elevation:

1. Locate the point on the map that you want to determine the elevation for (it may already be plotted or given as an eight-digit coordinate).
2. Determine the contour interval of the map from the marginal...
Steps to Determine Elevation:

1. Locate the point on the map that you want to determine the elevation for (it may already be plotted or given as an eight-digit coordinate).
2. Determine the contour interval of the map from the marginal information.
3. Find the index contour line nearest to the point for which the elevation is being sought.
4. Count the number of contour lines that must be crossed to go from the numbered line to the point, and note the direction to the point.
- If the point is on a contour line, its elevation is equal to that of the contour line.
- For points between contour lines:
- Points less than one-fourth of the distance between two lines are considered to have the same elevation as the nearest line.
- Points one-fourth to three-fourths of the distance from the lower line are considered to be at an elevation half the contour interval above the lower line.
5. Estimate the elevation of a hilltop: Add half the contour interval to the elevation of the highest contour line around the hill.
6. Estimate the elevation of a depression: Subtract half the contour interval from the elevation of the lowest contour line surrounding the depression.
7. If the map does not show sufficient detail, use supplementary contour lines, which are treated exactly like solid contour lines.
elevation,map reading,determine elevation,navigation
Determine Grid Square Coordinates on a Military Map
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Determine Grid Square Coordinates on a Military Map

Overview:
A military map uses vertical and horizontal lines to form grid squares, each representing 1,000 meters. These grid squares are numbered along the outside edge of the map. To...
### Determine Grid Square Coordinates on a Military Map

Overview:
A military map uses vertical and horizontal lines to form grid squares, each representing 1,000 meters. These grid squares are numbered along the outside edge of the map. To locate a specific point within a grid square, additional digits can be added for greater precision.

### Steps:
1. Identify Grid Square:
- Look at the map and find the vertical (top to bottom) and horizontal (left to right) lines that form grid squares.
- Each grid square is 1,000 meters on each side and has a unique number.
- To determine your general location, read the numbers along the left and bottom edges of the map. For example, if you are in grid square 1181, that means you are within the area defined by vertical line 11 and horizontal line 81.

2. Determine Precision:
- A 4-digit coordinate (e.g., 1996) identifies a location to the nearest 1,000 meters.
- A 6-digit coordinate (e.g., 192961) identifies a location to the nearest 100 meters.

3. Refine Grid Square Location:
- To get more precise coordinates, imagine that each grid square is divided into smaller squares by adding 10 imaginary lines inside it (5 running north-south and 5 east-west), creating 100 smaller squares of 100 meters on each side.
- Estimate where the point lies within the grid square:
- If you are halfway between vertical line 11 and 12, add 5 to the first half, making it 115.
- If you are 3/10 of the way between horizontal line 81 and 82, add 3 to the second half, making it 813.
- Your final 6-digit coordinate would be 115813.

4. Use Coordinate Scale for Accuracy:
- The most accurate way to determine coordinates is by using a coordinate scale, protractor (GTA 5-2-12), or plotting scale.
- Ensure the correct scale is used (either 1:25,000 or 1:50,000 meters) depending on the map.

5. Place Coordinate Scale:
- Locate the grid square where your point of interest is located (e.g., point A).
- Align the coordinate scale so that its zeros are in the lower-left corner (southwest) of the grid square containing the point.
- Slide the scale to the right, keeping it aligned with the bottom horizontal grid line until the point is under the vertical (right-hand) scale.

6. Read Coordinates:
- The 100-meter mark on the bottom scale nearest to the vertical grid line gives the third digit of the coordinate.
- The 100-meter mark on the vertical scale nearest to the point gives the sixth digit of the coordinate.
- Combine these digits with the original grid square number for a full six-digit coordinate (e.g., 115813).

### Notes:
• Always ensure that you are using the correct scale and device (protractor or plotting scale) based on the map's scale.
• If you are exactly on a grid line, use 0 for the additional digit (e.g., if you're exactly on horizontal line 81, the second half of your coordinate is 810).
military map, grid square, coordinate scale, protractor, plotting scale, navigation, location precision
Determine Road Distance on a Map
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Purpose: To determine the road distance in miles, meters, or yards using a map.

### Performance Steps
1. Use this step to determine the road distance in miles, meters, or yards as appropriate.
2. If the total distance measured on the paper...
Purpose: To determine the road distance in miles, meters, or yards using a map.

### Performance Steps
1. Use this step to determine the road distance in miles, meters, or yards as appropriate.
2. If the total distance measured on the paper is less than or equal to 1 inch:
- Use steps 2a(3) and (4).
3. If the total distance measured on the paper is more than 1 inch:
- Use steps 2b(3) through (7).

### Evaluation Preparation
• Setup: In the field or classroom, give the Soldier a 1:50,000-scale military map, a strip of paper with a straight edge, and a pencil. On the map, plot a straight-line distance of 3,000 to 4,000 meters. Mark this distance points A and B. On a road or trail, plot a curved-line distance of 3,000 to 4,000 meters with at least two changes of direction. Mark this distance points C and D.
• Brief Soldier: Tell the Soldier to determine the straight-line distance between points A and B with no more than 5 percent error, and the curved-line distance between points C and D with no more than 10 percent error. The Soldier must either write down the correct answers or state them when asked by the scorer.

### Performance Measures
| Performance Measure | GO | NO GO |
|---------------------|----|-------|
| Identified the scale of the map. | —— | —— |
| Converted the straight-line map distance to miles, meters, or yards using the map's bar scale, with no more than 5 percent error. | —— | —— |
| Converted the road map distance to miles, meters, or yards using the map's bar scale, with no more than 10 percent error. | —— | —— |

### Evaluation Guidance
• Refer to chapter 1, paragraph 1-9e, (1) and (2).
map reading, distance measurement, road distance, military map, scale conversion
Determine Slope Steepness Using Contour Lines
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Contour line spacing indicates slope steepness:
• Widely spaced contour lines indicate a gentle slope.
• Closely spaced contour lines indicate a steep slope.

Additional observations:
• If contour lines are widely spaced at...
Contour line spacing indicates slope steepness:
• Widely spaced contour lines indicate a gentle slope.
• Closely spaced contour lines indicate a steep slope.

Additional observations:
• If contour lines are widely spaced at the top of a hill, it indicates a flat hilltop.
• If contour lines are closely spaced near the top of a hill, it suggests a pointed or sharp hill.

These observations help in understanding terrain features and planning routes accordingly.
slope, map reading, contour lines, navigation
Determine Your Location Using Map and Compass (Resection)
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Objective: Determine your location on a map using a compass and known positions.

Steps:
1. Determine the G-M angle of the map you are using.
2. Locate two known positions on the ground and mark them on your map (A).
3. **Using your...
Objective: Determine your location on a map using a compass and known positions.

Steps:
1. Determine the G-M angle of the map you are using.
2. Locate two known positions on the ground and mark them on your map (A).
3. Using your compass, measure the magnetic azimuth to one of the known locations.
- If it is a westerly G-M angle, subtract the number of degrees in the G-M angle from your magnetic azimuth.
- If it is an easterly G-M angle, add the number of degrees in the G-M angle to your magnetic azimuth.
4. Change this grid azimuth to a back azimuth.
5. Place the protractor on the map, ensuring that the zero-degree indicators on the protractor point to the top of the map (North), and the index point is center of mass on this location.
6. Place a tick mark at the number of degrees you want to plot.
7. Remove the protractor from the map and draw a line on the map from this position on the grid back azimuth you found, in the direction of your unknown position.
8. Repeat steps 3 through 7 for a second (C) and third (D) known position.
9. Where these lines cross is your location.

Notes: The example map is not to scale. The example uses an easterly G-M angle of 10 degrees.
resection,map and compass,navigation,G-M angle,back azimuth
Determine a Location on the Ground by Terrain Association
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Determining a Location on the Ground by Terrain Association

### Conditions:
• In the field during daylight, at an unknown location on the ground.
• Given a standard 1:50,000-scale military map of the area.
• Pencil and paper...
Procedure for Determining a Location on the Ground by Terrain Association

### Conditions:
• In the field during daylight, at an unknown location on the ground.
• Given a standard 1:50,000-scale military map of the area.
• Pencil and paper available.
• Coordinate scale and protractor provided.
• A known point on the ground is identified.

### Standards:
• Determine the six-digit coordinate of your location within 7 minutes with a 100-meter tolerance.

### Performance Steps:
1. Determine the type of terrain feature you are located on.
2. Identify the types of terrain features that surround your location.
3. Orient the map.
4. Determine the four cardinal directions (North, South, East, and West).
5. Determine your location:
- a. Relate the terrain features on the ground to those shown on the map.
- b. After identifying where the terrain features on the ground and those on the map coincide, use the coordinate scale and protractor to determine the coordinates of your location.

### Evaluation Preparation:
• Setup: Select a field site whose terrain features are shown on the map. Provide at the test site: a field table, a 1:50,000-scale military map of the area, pencil, paper, and a coordinate scale.
• Brief Soldier: Instruct the soldier that they must determine a six-digit coordinate of their location within 7 minutes. Tell them one of the cardinal directions (North, South, East, or West).

### Performance Measures:
|Performance Measure|GO|NO GO|
|---|---|---|
|Identified the type of terrain feature where you are located.|——|——|
|Identified the terrain features around your location.|——|——|
|Oriented the map.|——|——|
|Determined the remaining three cardinal directions.|——|——|
|Identified the same terrain features on the map.|——|——|
|Determined the six-digit grid coordinate of your own location (the point selected must be within 100 meters of your location).|——|——|

### Evaluation Guidance:
• Refer to chapter 1, paragraph 1-9e(1) and (2).

### References:
• Required: FM 3-25.26
• Related: STP 21-1-SMCT, 3-234, 071-329-1005
terrain association,map reading,determine location,navigation,military map,coordinate scale,protractor
Determining Direction Using a Map
Standard Source: How To Avoid Getting Lost
Determining Direction Using a Map

1. Understand map orientation: The top of the map is north, the right edge is east, the bottom is south, and the left edge is west.
2. Identify azimuths: The direction from one point to another (either...
Determining Direction Using a Map

1. Understand map orientation: The top of the map is north, the right edge is east, the bottom is south, and the left edge is west.
2. Identify azimuths: The direction from one point to another (either on the map or on the ground) is called an azimuth.
3. Use a compass for accuracy: When traveling cross-country or at night, use a compass to maintain a straight line and avoid walking in circles.
4. Utilize steering points: During the day, use well-defined objects in your direction of travel as reference points (steering points) to help you stay on course.
direction,map reading,navigation,azimuth,compass
Establishing a Magnetic-North Line Using a Compass on a Photograph
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Orient the photograph by inspection to match surrounding ground features.
2. Open a compass and place it flat on the photograph.
3. Rotate the compass until the north arrow aligns with the black fixed index line.
4. Draw a...
Procedure:

1. Orient the photograph by inspection to match surrounding ground features.
2. Open a compass and place it flat on the photograph.
3. Rotate the compass until the north arrow aligns with the black fixed index line.
4. Draw a straight line along the edge of the compass on the photograph. This is the magnetic-north line.

Details:
• This method requires visual confirmation of ground features to ensure proper alignment.
• A compass must be used for accurate magnetic north orientation.
compass navigation,magnetic north,map alignment
Identify Features on Aerial Photographs Using Shade (Tone or Texture)
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Shade (Tone or Texture) is another factor used to identify features on aerial photographs. Most aerial photography uses panchromatic film, which registers colors as shades of gray ranging from white to black. The tone depends on the texture of...
Shade (Tone or Texture) is another factor used to identify features on aerial photographs. Most aerial photography uses panchromatic film, which registers colors as shades of gray ranging from white to black. The tone depends on the texture of the feature: a paved highway has a smooth texture and produces an even tone, while a recently plowed field or marsh has a rough texture and results in a grainy tone. Similar features may have different tones depending on sunlight reflection. For example, a river appears light when reflecting sunlight directly toward the camera and dark otherwise.
aerial photography, feature identification, shade, tone, texture, map reading, navigation
Identify Features on Aerial Photographs Using Surrounding Objects
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Surrounding Objects can help identify features that are not easily recognized by themselves. For example, large buildings located beside railroads or railroad sidings are usually factories or warehouses. Schools can be identified by the presence...
Surrounding Objects can help identify features that are not easily recognized by themselves. For example, large buildings located beside railroads or railroad sidings are usually factories or warehouses. Schools can be identified by the presence of baseball or football fields. It may be difficult to distinguish between a water tower next to a railroad station and a silo next to a barn unless surrounding objects such as railroad tracks or cultivated fields are considered.
aerial photography, feature identification, surrounding objects, map reading, navigation
Identify Major Terrain Features on a Map
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
### Identify Major Terrain Features on a Map

Major terrain features include hills, saddles, valleys, ridges, and depressions.

#### Hill
• A hill is an area of high ground. From a hilltop, the ground slopes down in all directions.
• On a map, a...
### Identify Major Terrain Features on a Map

Major terrain features include hills, saddles, valleys, ridges, and depressions.

#### Hill
• A hill is an area of high ground. From a hilltop, the ground slopes down in all directions.
• On a map, a hill is shown by contour lines forming concentric circles.
• The inside of the smallest closed circle represents the hilltop.

#### Saddle
• A saddle is a dip or low point between two areas of higher ground. It may be simply a dip along a level ridge crest.
• If you are in a saddle, there is high ground in two opposite directions and lower ground in the other two directions.
• On a map, a saddle is normally represented as an hourglass shape.

#### Valley
• A valley is a stretched-out groove in the land, usually formed by streams or rivers.
• Three sides of a valley have high ground, while the fourth direction has low ground.
• Water flows from higher to lower ground. The closed end of the contour line (U or V) always points upstream or toward high ground.

#### Ridge
• A ridge is a sloping line of high ground. If you are on the centerline, there is low ground in three directions and high ground in one direction.
• When crossing a ridge at right angles, you climb steeply to the crest and then descend steeply to the base.
• On a map, contour lines forming a ridge tend to be U-shaped or V-shaped, with the closed end pointing away from high ground.

#### Depression
• A depression is a low point in the ground or a sinkhole. It may be surrounded by higher ground on all sides or simply a hole in the ground.
• On maps, depressions are represented by closed contour lines that have tick marks pointing toward low ground.
terrain features,hill,saddle,valley,ridge,depression,map reading,navigation
Identify Major and Minor Terrain Features on a Military Map
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Obtain materials: Use a 1:50,000-scale military map, paper, two pencils, and a grid coordinate scale.
2. Prepare the map: Circle one example of each major terrain feature (e.g., hill, ridge, valley, saddle, depression) and...
Procedure:

1. Obtain materials: Use a 1:50,000-scale military map, paper, two pencils, and a grid coordinate scale.
2. Prepare the map: Circle one example of each major terrain feature (e.g., hill, ridge, valley, saddle, depression) and one example of each minor terrain feature (e.g., draw, spur, cliff) on the map.
3. Randomly number the circled terrain features 1 through 8.
4. Provide materials: Give each Soldier duplicate sets of the map, paper, and two pencils.
5. Instruct Soldiers: Tell them to number their paper 1 through 8 and write down the terrain feature that corresponds to each circled area on the map.
6. Demonstrate identification: During instruction, use the fist and hand to demonstrate how to identify terrain features and what they would look like on the ground.
7. Evaluate performance: Ensure Soldiers correctly identify all major and minor terrain features based on their written responses.

Notes:
• Major terrain features include hills, ridges, valleys, saddles, and depressions.
• Minor terrain features include draws, spurs, and cliffs.
• Use the grid coordinate scale to locate points accurately if needed.
terrain features,military map,map reading,navigation skills,identification
Identify Map Symbols Representing Physical Features
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Identify Map Symbols Representing Physical Features

Steps:
1. Identify each object by its shape on the map. For example:
- A black, solid square represents a building or a house.
- A round or irregular blue item is a lake or...
### Identify Map Symbols Representing Physical Features

Steps:
1. Identify each object by its shape on the map. For example:
- A black, solid square represents a building or a house.
- A round or irregular blue item is a lake or pond.
2. Use logic and color to identify each map feature. For example:
- Blue represents water.
- If you see a symbol that is blue and has clumps of grass, this would be a swamp.
3. Understand the size of the symbol:
- The size of the symbol shows the relative size of the object.
- Most symbols are enlarged 6 to 10 times so that you can see them under dim light.
4. Use the legend:
- The legend should identify most of the symbols used on the map.
map symbols,map features,topographic map identification,legend use,physical feature recognition
Identify Map Symbols for Orienteering Control Points
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
To identify map symbols for orienteering control points:

1. Refer to Figure E-9 in FM 3-25.26, which contains typical topographic and cultural symbols used for orienteering.
2. The map cutouts are selected from DMA maps and include a variety of...
To identify map symbols for orienteering control points:

1. Refer to Figure E-9 in FM 3-25.26, which contains typical topographic and cultural symbols used for orienteering.
2. The map cutouts are selected from DMA maps and include a variety of features such as trails, fences, roads, streams, and other landmarks.
3. Use these symbols to locate and identify control points during orienteering exercises.

Note: These symbols are essential for accurate navigation and should be studied thoroughly before field use.
map symbols, orienteering control points, topographic symbols, cultural symbols, DMA maps
Identify Marginal Information on a Topographic Map
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Top of the map sheet:
- Top left corner: Identify the geographic location covered by the map and its scale.
- Top center: Identify the name of the map sheet.
- Top right corner: Identify the map edition,...
Procedure:

1. Top of the map sheet:
- Top left corner: Identify the geographic location covered by the map and its scale.
- Top center: Identify the name of the map sheet.
- Top right corner: Identify the map edition, series, and sheet number.
2. Bottom of the map sheet:
- Lower left corner: Locate the legend, agency that prepared the map, sheet number, and sheet name.
- Bottom center: Identify bar scales in meters, yards, miles, and nautical miles; contour interval; grid reference box; declination diagram; and G-M angle (mils or degrees).
- Lower right corner: Locate the elevation guide, adjoining map sheet diagram, and boundaries box showing any boundaries on the map.

Notes:
• This procedure is essential for understanding the context and scale of a topographic map.
• Use this information to orient yourself and plan routes effectively.
topographic map, marginal information, navigation, map reading, legend, scale
Identify Minor Terrain Features on a Map
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
### Identify Minor Terrain Features on a Map

Minor terrain features include draws, spurs, and cliffs.

#### Draw
• A draw is a stream course that is less developed than a valley. There is essentially no level ground within its confines.
• If you...
### Identify Minor Terrain Features on a Map

Minor terrain features include draws, spurs, and cliffs.

#### Draw
• A draw is a stream course that is less developed than a valley. There is essentially no level ground within its confines.
• If you are standing in a draw, the ground slopes upward in three directions and downward in one direction.
• On maps, draws are represented by U-shaped or V-shaped contour lines, with the closed end pointing toward high ground.

#### spur
• A spur is a short, continuous sloping line of higher ground that juts out from the side of a ridge. It is often formed by two roughly parallel streams cutting down the side of a ridge.
• The ground slopes down in three directions and up in one direction.
• On maps, spurs are represented by U or V-shaped contour lines, with the closed end pointing away from high ground.

#### Cliff
• A cliff is a vertical or near-vertical feature; it represents an abrupt change of the land.
• When a slope is so steep that the contour lines converge into one “carrying” contour, this last contour line has tick marks pointing toward low ground.
• Cliffs are also shown by contour lines very close together, and in some cases, touching each other.
draw,spur,cliff,map reading,navigation,terrain features
Identify Physical Features on a Topographic Map
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Identify symbols by shape:
- A black, solid square represents a building or house.
- A round or irregular blue item is a lake or pond.
2. Use logic and color to identify features:
- Blue represents water. If you...
Procedure:

1. Identify symbols by shape:
- A black, solid square represents a building or house.
- A round or irregular blue item is a lake or pond.
2. Use logic and color to identify features:
- Blue represents water. If you see a symbol that is blue with clumps of grass, this would be a swamp.
3. Interpret the size of symbols:
- Symbols are typically enlarged 6 to 10 times for visibility under dim light.
4. Use the legend:
- The legend should identify most map symbols and features.

Notes:
• This procedure is essential for navigation using topographic maps.
• Ensure that you refer to the legend when identifying unclear symbols.
topographic map, identify features, navigation, map reading, symbols, legend

• PDs are typically shown along the line of departure (LD) for night attacks.
• They appear similar to checkpoints but include the letters 'PD' followed by a letter or number.

Procedure for Identifying Points of Departure (PDs):

• PDs are typically shown along the line of departure (LD) for night attacks.
• They appear similar to checkpoints but include the letters 'PD' followed by a letter or number.
• The arrow on the map points downward from its location.

Purpose: To correctly identify and mark PDs on a map overlay for operational planning.
