4. Expressed direction to the nearest 10 mils and within 60 mils of the actual direction.
determine direction, compass use, map scaling, RALS rule, navigation skills
Determine Direction Using the North Star Method
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Locate the Big Dipper in the night sky.
2. Identify the last two stars in the cup of the Big Dipper (the ones that form the end of the ladle).
3. Draw an imaginary line between these two stars and extend it about five...
Procedure:

1. Locate the Big Dipper in the night sky.
2. Identify the last two stars in the cup of the Big Dipper (the ones that form the end of the ladle).
3. Draw an imaginary line between these two stars and extend it about five times the distance between them.
4. The point where this line ends is approximately where the North Star (Polaris) is located.
5. Face Polaris to determine your direction: you are facing North, with East on your right and West on your left.

Notes:
• This method works best on clear nights when the Big Dipper and Polaris are visible.
• The North Star is not visible in the Southern Hemisphere.
• Use this technique only if you are in the Northern Hemisphere.
north star method,navigation,direction finding,constellation,polaris
Determine Direction Using the Southern Cross Method at Night
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Locate the Southern Cross in the night sky (visible primarily in the Southern Hemisphere).
2. Extend an imaginary line through the long axis of the Southern Cross.
3. Identify the two pointers (Alpha and Beta Centauri)...
Procedure:

1. Locate the Southern Cross in the night sky (visible primarily in the Southern Hemisphere).
2. Extend an imaginary line through the long axis of the Southern Cross.
3. Identify the two pointers (Alpha and Beta Centauri) which are part of the constellation Centaurus.
4. Draw another imaginary line that bisects the two pointers at a right angle (perpendicular to the first line).
5. Where these two lines intersect, draw a vertical line downward toward the horizon; this will indicate south (in the Southern Hemisphere) or north (in the Northern Hemisphere), depending on your location.

Notes:
• This method is most effective in clear skies with minimal light pollution.
• It works best when the Southern Cross is high enough above the horizon to be clearly visible.
southern cross navigation,direction finding at night,stargazing survival,navigation techniques
Determine Direction Using the Watch Method Without a Compass
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
To determine direction using the watch method without a compass:

1. Hold the watch horizontally: Ensure it is level with the ground.
2. Point the hour hand toward the sun: Align the hour hand so that it points directly at the sun's current...
To determine direction using the watch method without a compass:

1. Hold the watch horizontally: Ensure it is level with the ground.
2. Point the hour hand toward the sun: Align the hour hand so that it points directly at the sun's current position in the sky.
3. Find the midpoint between the hour hand and 12 o'clock: This midpoint will point toward true south (in the Northern Hemisphere). If you are in the Southern Hemisphere, this midpoint will point toward true north.

Important Notes:
• This method works best during daylight hours when the sun is visible.
• It assumes a standard analog watch with hour and minute hands.
• Accuracy may vary depending on your location and time of year.
watch method, direction finding, navigation
Determine Direction Within the Target Area Using a Compass and Field Artillery Binoculars
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
### Procedure: Determine Direction Within the Target Area

Objective: To determine the direction to selected points within the target area using an object with a known direction, field artillery binoculars, a compass, and a target in the target...
### Procedure: Determine Direction Within the Target Area

Objective: To determine the direction to selected points within the target area using an object with a known direction, field artillery binoculars, a compass, and a target in the target area.

Equipment Required:
• Field artillery binoculars
• Compass
• Object with a known direction (e.g., a landmark or reference point)
• Target within the target area

**Steps:
1. Identify the object with a known direction that is visible from your position and align it with the compass.
2. Use field artillery binoculars to observe the target in the target area and note its approximate location relative to the object with a known direction.
3. Align the compass with the object of known direction, ensuring that the magnetic needle points north.
4. Determine the azimuth (direction) from your position to the object using the compass scale, which is measured in mils.
5. Estimate the direction to the target by comparing its location relative to the object with a known direction and adjusting the azimuth accordingly.
6. Record the direction to the selected points, expressed to the nearest 10 mils (or within ±60 mils of the actual direction, depending on the required precision).

Notes:
• Ensure that the compass is not near magnetic materials or sources of interference.
• Use the field artillery binoculars for accurate visual identification and estimation of the target's position relative to known landmarks.
• If the object with a known direction is not visible, use other reference points such as celestial bodies (e.g., sun, stars) or topographic features (e.g., rivers, roads).
determine direction,navigation,compass,binoculars,target area,mils
Determine North-South Line Using East-West Line
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
To determine a North-South line using an established East-West line:

1. Identify the East-West line: Ensure you have already drawn a straight line representing East and West.
2. Draw a perpendicular line at any point on the East-West line:...
To determine a North-South line using an established East-West line:

1. Identify the East-West line: Ensure you have already drawn a straight line representing East and West.
2. Draw a perpendicular line at any point on the East-West line: Use a right angle to create a new line that intersects the East-West line at 90 degrees.
3. This new line represents North-South: The direction of this line will be aligned with true north and south.

Important Notes:
• This method is useful for establishing cardinal directions in the absence of a compass.
• Accuracy depends on the precision of your East-West line.
north-south line, navigation, direction finding
Navigate Using the Stars Without a Compass
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Locate north or south using star constellations (e.g., Southern Cross in the Southern Hemisphere or Polaris in the Northern Hemisphere).
2. Draw an imaginary line on the ground to represent the north-south direction.
3. Bisect...
Procedure:

1. Locate north or south using star constellations (e.g., Southern Cross in the Southern Hemisphere or Polaris in the Northern Hemisphere).
2. Draw an imaginary line on the ground to represent the north-south direction.
3. Bisect this line with another line at a right angle (90°) to create an earth compass indicating east and west directions.
4. Identify a bright star or group of stars in the desired direction you wish to travel toward.
5. Face the selected star(s) and begin moving in that direction, keeping them in sight if possible.
6. Be aware that stars move from east to west at 15° per hour, similar to the sun. Adjust your course accordingly as needed.
7. Periodically stop and redraw your earth compass on the ground to verify your direction and ensure you are heading straight toward your destination.

Notes:
• This method is most effective in open, arid regions with a clear view of the sky.
• Avoid using stars that are too close to the horizon as they may be obscured by terrain or lose visibility quickly.
star navigation,navigation without compass,survival navigation,earth compass
Use Field-Expedient Methods to Determine Direction Without a Compass
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Determine direction using the shadow-tip method:
- Place a stick vertically in the ground and mark the tip of its shadow.
- Wait about 15 minutes and mark the new position of the shadow tip.
- Draw a line between the...
Procedure:

1. Determine direction using the shadow-tip method:
- Place a stick vertically in the ground and mark the tip of its shadow.
- Wait about 15 minutes and mark the new position of the shadow tip.
- Draw a line between the two marks; this line runs East-West, with the first mark being West and the second mark being East.
- The direction perpendicular to this line points North-South.

2. Determine direction using the watch method:
- If you are North of the equator: Point the hour hand at the sun; South will be halfway between the hour hand and 12 o'clock.
- If you are South of the equator: Point 12 o'clock at the sun; North will be halfway between the hour hand and 12 o'clock.

3. Determine direction using the North Star method:
- Locate the Big Dipper in the night sky.
- Identify the last two stars in the cup of the Big Dipper and draw an imaginary line between them, extending it five times the distance between those stars.
- The point where this line ends is approximately where Polaris (the North Star) is located. Facing Polaris means you are facing North.

Notes:
• These methods are field-expedient and should be used only when a compass is unavailable.
• Proficiency in all three methods is required for full competency.
• The Southern Cross is the main constellation used as a guide South of the equator, with directions reversed compared to the Northern Hemisphere.
field-expedient navigation,direction finding,no compass,north star,watch method
Using a Pocket Navigator for Direction Finding
Standard Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
To use a pocket navigator for direction finding:

1. Set up the navigator: Place the tiny rod upright on a flat piece of material so that the sun will cast a shadow. Mark the position where the base of the rod sits to ensure it can be returned...
To use a pocket navigator for direction finding:

1. Set up the navigator: Place the tiny rod upright on a flat piece of material so that the sun will cast a shadow. Mark the position where the base of the rod sits to ensure it can be returned to the same spot for later readings.
2. Secure the navigator: Place the pocket navigator flat on level ground and secure it from wind by placing rocks on it. Write down the date and mark the tip of the shadow at this time.
3. Track shadow movement: As the sun moves, observe how the shadow-tip moves. Make repeated shadow-tip markings every 15 minutes. Ensure that you write down the time for each marking.
4. Connect shadow tips: At the end of the day, connect the shadow-tip markings. The result will typically be a curved line. The arch is less pronounced closer to the vernal or autumnal equinoxes (March 20 and September 23).
5. Continue observations if needed: If it's not convenient or the tactical situation does not permit taking a full day’s shadow-tip readings, your observation can be continued on the following day by orienting the pocket navigator on the ground so that the shadow-tip is aligned with a previously plotted point.
6. Determine north-south line: The markings made at the sun's highest point during the day (solar noon) indicate the north–south line. Draw an arrow on the navigator to show the direction of north, using the shortest line from the base of the rod to the shadow-tip curve.
7. Align for use: To use your pocket navigator, hold it so that the shadow-tip is aligned with a plotted point at the specified time (e.g., if it is now 0900, align the shadow-tip with that point). This ensures the navigator is level and oriented to true north.
8. Use for navigation: Once the arrow on the navigator is oriented to true north, you can use it to orient yourself in any desired direction of travel.
pocket navigator,direction finding,sun shadow,shadow tip,navigation tool

Compass Usage (17) -

Identify and Use Lensatic Compass Components
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Understand the parts of the lensatic compass:
- Cover: Protects the floating dial and contains the sighting wire, luminous slots/dots for night navigation.
- Base: Contains the floating dial (with E/W markings),...
Procedure:

1. Understand the parts of the lensatic compass:
- Cover: Protects the floating dial and contains the sighting wire, luminous slots/dots for night navigation.
- Base: Contains the floating dial (with E/W markings), glass with a fixed black index line, bezel ring (120 clicks, 3 degrees per click), and thumb loop.
- Lens: Used to read the dial and contains the rear-sight slot for sighting objects. The rear sight also serves as a lock to clamp the dial when closed.

2. Use the coordinate scale on the compass:
- Newer lensatic compasses have a 1:50,000 scale on the straight edge of the left side.
- Older models may have a 1:25,000 scale (used with 1:50,000 maps but values must be halved).

Important Notes:
• The floating dial has two scales: outer in mils, inner in degrees.
• The bezel ring contains a luminous line used for navigation.
• When using the rear sight, it must be opened more than 45 degrees to allow the dial to float freely.
lensatic compass, compass parts, navigation tools, coordinate scale, map reading
Use Lensatic Compass for Direction Finding
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Hold the compass level and steady in your hand.
2. Open the rear sight more than 45 degrees to allow the dial to float freely.
3. Align the front sight with the target object (e.g., a landmark).
4. **Look through the...
Procedure:

1. Hold the compass level and steady in your hand.
2. Open the rear sight more than 45 degrees to allow the dial to float freely.
3. Align the front sight with the target object (e.g., a landmark).
4. Look through the rear-sight slot until the target is centered in the notch.
5. Read the azimuth from the inner scale (degrees) or outer scale (mils), depending on which is needed for your task.
6. Use the luminous line on the bezel ring to align with the north-seeking arrow during night navigation.

Important Notes:
• The floating dial always points to magnetic north.
• The letters E and W are at 90 degrees and 270 degrees, respectively.
• Ensure that the compass is not near metal objects or electronic devices that may interfere with its accuracy.
direction finding, azimuth reading, magnetic north, lensatic compass use
Use Artillery M2 Compass for Precision Navigation
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Ensure the artillery M2 compass is properly calibrated and free from magnetic interference.
2. Hold the compass level in your hand with the sighting wire aligned with the target object.
3. **Use the magnifying lens to read...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the artillery M2 compass is properly calibrated and free from magnetic interference.
2. Hold the compass level in your hand with the sighting wire aligned with the target object.
3. Use the magnifying lens to read the azimuth more accurately from the dial.
4. Adjust the bezel ring if necessary for precise readings (the M2 compass has a more accurate scale than standard models).
5. Record the measured azimuth in degrees or mils, depending on your mission requirements.

Important Notes:
• The artillery M2 compass is designed for high accuracy and is used primarily by specialized units.
• It should be stored in a protective case when not in use to prevent damage.
• This compass is discussed in detail in Appendix J of the manual.
artillery m2 compass, precision navigation, military compass
Use Wrist/Pocket Compass for Direction Finding
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Hold the wrist/pocket compass level and steady in your hand.
2. Ensure that the north-seeking arrow is free to rotate (not obstructed by any metal objects).
3. **Wait until the needle settles and points toward magnetic...
Procedure:

1. Hold the wrist/pocket compass level and steady in your hand.
2. Ensure that the north-seeking arrow is free to rotate (not obstructed by any metal objects).
3. Wait until the needle settles and points toward magnetic north.
4. Read the direction from the dial in degrees or use the markings on the compass face for approximate directions.

Important Notes:
• The wrist/pocket compass is a small, portable tool with a simple design.
• It contains a north-seeking arrow and a dial in degrees.
• This compass should be used as a backup when more advanced tools are not available.
wrist compass, pocket compass, direction finding
Using a Compass to Find or Follow an Azimuth
High Priority Source: How To Avoid Getting Lost
Using a Compass to Find or Follow an Azimuth

To use your compass effectively, follow these steps:

1. Open the compass: Ensure that the cover forms a straight edge with the base. Move the lens of the compass out of the way.
2. **Hold the...
Using a Compass to Find or Follow an Azimuth

To use your compass effectively, follow these steps:

1. Open the compass: Ensure that the cover forms a straight edge with the base. Move the lens of the compass out of the way.
2. Hold the compass correctly: Place your thumb through the thumb loop. Form a steady base with your third and fourth fingers. Extend your index finger along the side of the compass.
3. Stabilize the compass: Place the thumb of your other hand between the eyepiece and the lens. Extend your index finger along the other side of the compass, and wrap your remaining fingers around the fingers of your other hand.
4. Position the compass: Pull your elbows firmly into your sides to hold the compass between your chin and belt.

To measure an azimuth:
1. Turn your entire body toward the object you want to find or follow.
2. Point the compass cover directly at the object.
3. Look down and read the azimuth from beneath the fixed black index line.
4. This method can be used even at night, especially if your compass has luminous lines and dots for visibility.

Tips for land navigation: Stop occasionally to check the azimuth along which you are moving to avoid going in circles. Move from object to object along your path of travel by shooting an azimuth to each object and then moving to that object. Repeating this process will help keep you on a straight path.
compass, azimuth, navigation, direction, land navigation
Check Compass Accuracy Using a Known Line of Direction
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Purpose: Verify the accuracy of a compass to ensure reliable navigation.

Steps:
1. Use a known line of direction, such as a surveyed azimuth or declination station, for testing.
2. Compare the compass reading with the known...
Purpose: Verify the accuracy of a compass to ensure reliable navigation.

Steps:
1. Use a known line of direction, such as a surveyed azimuth or declination station, for testing.
2. Compare the compass reading with the known direction.
3. If the variation between the compass and the known direction exceeds 3 degrees, the compass should not be used until it is repaired or recalibrated.

Notes: A compass in good working condition is very accurate but must be checked periodically to maintain reliability.
compass accuracy, declination station, surveyed azimuth, compass calibration
Inspect a Compass for Proper Functioning
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Purpose: Ensure the compass is in good working condition before use.

Steps:
1. Check the floating dial, which contains the magnetic needle, to ensure it moves freely and is not stuck.
2. Verify that the sighting wire is straight and...
Purpose: Ensure the compass is in good working condition before use.

Steps:
1. Check the floating dial, which contains the magnetic needle, to ensure it moves freely and is not stuck.
2. Verify that the sighting wire is straight and undamaged.
3. Inspect the glass and crystal parts for any cracks or breaks.
4. Confirm that the numbers on the dial are readable and clearly marked.
5. Ensure that the dial does not stick when moved manually.

Notes: A detailed inspection is required when first obtaining and using a compass.
compass inspection, floating dial, sighting wire, compass maintenance
Maintain Proper Distance from Metal and Electrical Sources to Avoid Compass Interference
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Purpose: Prevent magnetic interference with compass readings by maintaining safe distances from metal and electrical sources.

Safe Distances:
• High-tension power lines: 55 meters
• Field gun, truck, or tank: 18 meters
• **Telegraph...
Purpose: Prevent magnetic interference with compass readings by maintaining safe distances from metal and electrical sources.

Safe Distances:
• High-tension power lines: 55 meters
• Field gun, truck, or tank: 18 meters
• Telegraph or telephone wires and barbed wire: 10 meters
• Machine gun: 2 meters
• Steel helmet or rifle: 1/2 meter

Notes: Nonmagnetic metals and alloys do not affect compass readings. However, magnetic materials can significantly distort the compass needle.
compass interference, metal objects, electrical sources, magnetic field
Measure an Angle of Site or Vertical Angle from the Horizontal Using a Compass
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
To measure an angle of site or vertical angle from the horizontal using a compass:

1. Position the compass: Hold the compass with the left side down (cover to the left) and fold the rear sight holder out parallel to the face of the compass,...
To measure an angle of site or vertical angle from the horizontal using a compass:

1. Position the compass: Hold the compass with the left side down (cover to the left) and fold the rear sight holder out parallel to the face of the compass, with the rear sight perpendicular to its holder. Position the cover so that, when looking through the rear sight and the aperture in the cover, the elevation vial is reflected in the mirror.
2. Sight on the point: Sight on the point to be measured.
3. Center the bubble: Center the bubble in the elevation level vial (reflected in the mirror) with the level lever.
4. Read the angle: Read the angle on the elevation scale opposite the index mark. The section of the scale graduated counterclockwise from 0 to 1200 mils measures plus angles of site. The section of the scale graduated clockwise from 0 to 1200 mils measures minus angles of site.
angle of site, vertical angle, compass usage, navigation, military compass
Protect the Compass When Traveling with It Unfolded
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Purpose: Prevent damage to the compass during travel.

Steps:
1. If traveling with the compass unfolded, ensure that the rear sight is fully folded down onto the bezel ring.
2. This action will lock the floating dial, preventing...
Purpose: Prevent damage to the compass during travel.

Steps:
1. If traveling with the compass unfolded, ensure that the rear sight is fully folded down onto the bezel ring.
2. This action will lock the floating dial, preventing vibration and reducing the risk of damage.
3. It also protects the crystal and rear sight from potential harm during movement or exposure to rough terrain.

Notes: Proper handling ensures that the compass remains functional for extended periods of use.
compass protection, rear sight folding, bezel ring, compass handling
Use a Lensatic Compass for Navigation
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure for Using a Lensatic Compass for Navigation:

1. Understand the components of a lensatic compass: Familiarize yourself with the bezel, cover, baseplate, magnetic needle, and orienting lines.

2. Hold the compass level: Hold the...
Procedure for Using a Lensatic Compass for Navigation:

1. Understand the components of a lensatic compass: Familiarize yourself with the bezel, cover, baseplate, magnetic needle, and orienting lines.

2. Hold the compass level: Hold the compass flat in your hand, ensuring it is not tilted or held against any metal objects that may interfere with the magnetic needle.

3. Orient the map: Place the map on a flat surface and align it with the surrounding terrain using the compass. Ensure the map's north matches the direction of the magnetic needle.

4. Take a bearing:
- Point the direction of travel arrow on the compass baseplate toward your intended destination on the map.
- Rotate the bezel until the orienting lines align with the magnetic needle.

5. Follow the bearing: Hold the compass level and walk in the direction indicated by the direction of travel arrow, keeping the magnetic needle aligned with the orienting lines.

6. Use the mirror for precision: Use the mirror on the lensatic compass to reflect light onto the map or to see the magnetic needle more clearly when navigating in low-light conditions.

7. Check your position periodically: Stop at regular intervals and recheck your bearing using the compass to ensure you are still on course.

8. Use the declination adjustment: If necessary, adjust for magnetic declination (the difference between true north and magnetic north) by rotating the bezel accordingly.
lensatic compass, navigation techniques, map orientation, bearing taking, declination adjustment
Bypassing an Obstacle Using Compass Navigation
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. While moving on an azimuth of 90 degrees, change your azimuth to 180 degrees and travel for 100 meters.
2. Change your azimuth to 90 degrees and travel for 150 meters.
3. Change your azimuth to 360 degrees and travel for 100...
Procedure:

1. While moving on an azimuth of 90 degrees, change your azimuth to 180 degrees and travel for 100 meters.
2. Change your azimuth to 90 degrees and travel for 150 meters.
3. Change your azimuth to 360 degrees and travel for 100 meters.
4. Change your azimuth back to 90 degrees, which will return you to your original azimuth line (Figure 9-5).

During limited visibility:
- To make a 90-degree turn to the right, hold the compass in the centerhold technique and turn until the center of the luminous letter E is under the luminous line. Do not move the bezel ring.
- To make a 90-degree turn to the left, turn until the center of the luminous letter W is under the luminous line. This ensures accurate 90-degree turns without changing the compass setting.
bypass obstacle, compass navigation, right/left turns
Maintain Compass Accuracy by Avoiding Metal and Electrical Interference
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Steps to Maintain Compass Accuracy:

1. Avoid Proximity to Magnetic Objects: Keep the compass away from magnetic materials that can interfere with its function.
2. Follow Recommended Separation Distances:
- **High-tension power...
Steps to Maintain Compass Accuracy:

1. Avoid Proximity to Magnetic Objects: Keep the compass away from magnetic materials that can interfere with its function.
2. Follow Recommended Separation Distances:
- High-tension power lines: 55 meters
- Field gun, truck, or tank: 18 meters
- Telegraph or telephone wires and barbed wire: 10 meters
- Machine gun: 2 meters
- Steel helmet or rifle: ½ meter
3. Use Nonmagnetic Metals and Alloys: These materials do not affect compass readings, so they can be used in proximity to a compass.

Important Notes:
• Metal objects and electrical sources can significantly impact compass performance.
• Maintaining proper separation distances is critical for accurate navigation.
compass,metal_interference,electrical_interference,navigation
Presetting a Compass and Following an Azimuth During Daylight or with Light Source
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Steps for Presetting a Compass and Following an Azimuth:

1. Hold the compass level in the palm of your hand.
2. Rotate it until the desired azimuth falls under the fixed black index line (e.g., 320 degrees), maintaining the azimuth as...
Steps for Presetting a Compass and Following an Azimuth:

1. Hold the compass level in the palm of your hand.
2. Rotate it until the desired azimuth falls under the fixed black index line (e.g., 320 degrees), maintaining the azimuth as prescribed.
3. Turn the bezel ring until the luminous line is aligned with the north-seeking arrow. Once alignment is obtained, the compass is preset.
4. Assume the centerhold technique and turn your body until the north-seeking arrow is aligned with the luminous line on the bezel.
5. Proceed forward in the direction of the front cover’s sighting wire, which is aligned with the fixed black index line containing the desired azimuth.
preset compass, azimuth following, lensatic compass
Presetting a Compass and Following an Azimuth During Limited Visibility (Click Method)
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Steps for Presetting a Compass Using the Click Method:

1. Rotate the bezel ring until the luminous line is over the fixed black index line.
2. Find the desired azimuth and divide it by 3 to determine the number of clicks needed.
- If the...
Steps for Presetting a Compass Using the Click Method:

1. Rotate the bezel ring until the luminous line is over the fixed black index line.
2. Find the desired azimuth and divide it by 3 to determine the number of clicks needed.
- If the desired azimuth is less than 180 degrees, count the number of clicks in a counterclockwise direction.
Example: Desired azimuth = 51° → 51 ÷ 3 = 17 clicks counterclockwise.
- If the desired azimuth is greater than 180 degrees, subtract it from 360 and divide by 3 to get the number of clicks in a clockwise direction.
Example: Desired azimuth = 330° → (360 − 330) ÷ 3 = 10 clicks clockwise.
3. With the compass preset as described, assume the centerhold technique and rotate your body until the north-seeking arrow is aligned with the luminous line on the bezel.
4. Proceed forward in the direction of the front cover’s luminous dots, which are aligned with the fixed black index line containing the azimuth.

Note: If the desired azimuth is not exactly divisible by 3, rounding up or down may be necessary. Rounding up causes an increase in the azimuth value and places the object on the left; rounding down decreases the azimuth value and places the object on the right.
click method, compass preset, limited visibility navigation
Using a Deliberate Offset to Reach an Objective Along a Linear Feature
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Purpose: A deliberate offset is used when the objective lies along or near a linear feature (e.g., road, stream) and there is uncertainty about whether it is on the left or right side.

Procedure:
1. Apply a known magnetic deviation to the...
Purpose: A deliberate offset is used when the objective lies along or near a linear feature (e.g., road, stream) and there is uncertainty about whether it is on the left or right side.

Procedure:
1. Apply a known magnetic deviation to the right or left of an azimuth to ensure that upon reaching the linear feature, you know which direction to go to find the objective.
2. A 10-degree offset is typically sufficient for most tactical uses.
3. For every 1,000 meters traveled, each degree of offset moves the course approximately 18 meters to the right or left.
- Example: If you travel 1,000 meters with a 10-degree offset, the objective will be about 180 meters to the right (see Figure 9-6).
deliberate offset, linear feature navigation, compass deviation
Using the Centerhold Technique with a Lensatic Compass
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Open the compass to its fullest so that the cover forms a straightedge with the base.
2. Move the lens (rear sight) to the rearmost position, allowing the dial to float freely.
3. Place your thumb through the thumb loop, form a...
Procedure:

1. Open the compass to its fullest so that the cover forms a straightedge with the base.
2. Move the lens (rear sight) to the rearmost position, allowing the dial to float freely.
3. Place your thumb through the thumb loop, form a steady base with your third and fourth fingers, and extend your index finger along the side of the compass.
4. Place the thumb of the other hand between the lens (rear sight) and the bezel ring; extend the index finger along the remaining side of the compass, and wrap the remaining fingers around the fingers of the other hand.
5. Pull your elbows firmly into your sides; this will place the compass between your chin and your belt.
6. Turn your entire body toward the object, pointing the compass cover directly at the object.
7. Look down and read the azimuth from beneath the fixed black index line.

Advantages of this technique:
• It is faster and easier to use.
• It can be used under all conditions of visibility.
• It can be used when navigating over any type of terrain.
• It can be used without putting down the rifle; however, the rifle must be slung well back over either shoulder.
• It can be used without removing eyeglasses.
centerhold technique, compass usage, azimuth measurement, lensatic compass

Compass Use (16) -

Avoiding Local Magnetic Attraction
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Keep all iron or steel objects at a safe distance from the compass before use.
2. Small items such as keys can be safely stored in a trouser pocket, but larger items like **wristwatches, steel-framed spectacles, vehicles,...
Procedure:

1. Keep all iron or steel objects at a safe distance from the compass before use.
2. Small items such as keys can be safely stored in a trouser pocket, but larger items like wristwatches, steel-framed spectacles, vehicles, power lines, wire fences, and railway lines should be placed two to three metres away.
3. Be aware that even small quantities of iron or steel can significantly affect compass readings.
4. Ensure the area around the compass is free from magnetic interference for accurate navigation.
local magnetic attraction, iron ore, steel objects, compass interference, navigation safety
Maintain Compass Warmth for Accurate Bearings
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Keep your compass warm to ensure faster and more accurate bearing readings.

2. Avoid taking bearings in areas with metallic objects, such as:
- Metal frames on glasses or rucksacks
- Small arms
- Other metallic...
Procedure:

1. Keep your compass warm to ensure faster and more accurate bearing readings.

2. Avoid taking bearings in areas with metallic objects, such as:
- Metal frames on glasses or rucksacks
- Small arms
- Other metallic items

3. If no aiming marks are visible ahead, use a back bearing to maintain direction. This can be based on:
- A natural feature behind you
- An artificial mark left by you

4. When using a back bearing and the only available aiming mark is poorly defined:
- Keep your eyes constantly on it after taking your bearing.
- Ensure you do not lose sight of it during movement.

5. In poor visibility conditions, rely on close-in aiming marks. As you approach each one, try to identify further marks along the correct bearing if possible.

6. To ensure accuracy in poor visibility:
- Follow a straight-line route to each mark.
- Make frequent compass checks to confirm that the correct bearing is being maintained.

7. Set your compass for night marching conditions when visibility is low.
compass,warmth,back bearing,metallic objects,poor visibility,navigation
Measure a Magnetic Azimuth Using a Compass
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
To measure a magnetic azimuth:

1. Zero the azimuth scale by turning the scale adjuster.
2. Position the compass: Place the cover at an angle of about 45 degrees to the face of the compass so that the scale reflection is viewed in the...
To measure a magnetic azimuth:

1. Zero the azimuth scale by turning the scale adjuster.
2. Position the compass: Place the cover at an angle of about 45 degrees to the face of the compass so that the scale reflection is viewed in the mirror.
3. Adjust sights:
- Raise the front sight and the extended rear sight assembly perpendicular to the face of the compass (see Figures F-2 and F-3). Sight over the tips of the front and rear sights. If the object is above the line of sighting, fold the rear sight toward the eye as needed.
- The instrument is correctly aligned when, with the level centered, the operator sees the tips of the sights and the center of the object at the same time.
- Raise the rear sight approximately perpendicular to the face of the compass. Sight on the object through the opening in the rear sight holder and through the window in the cover. Keep the compass level and raise or lower the eye along the opening in the rear sight holder until the black center line of the window bisects the object and the opening in the rear sight holder.
- Fold the rear sight holder out parallel with the face of the compass with the rear sight perpendicular to its holder. Sight through or over the rear sight and view the object through the window in the cover. If the object sighted is at a lower elevation than the compass, raise the rear sight holder as needed. The compass is correctly sighted when the compass is level and the operator sees the black center line of the window bisecting the rear sight and the object sighted.
4. Hold the compass: Hold it in both hands, at eye level, with the arms braced against the body and the rear sight near the eyes. For precise measurements, rest the compass on a nonmetallic stake or object.
5. Level the instrument: View the circular level in the mirror and move the compass until the bubble is centered. Sight on the object, look in the mirror, and read the azimuth indicated by the black (south) end of the magnetic needle.
magnetic azimuth, compass use, navigation, military compass
Use of Aiming Marks in Navigation
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. When no aiming marks are visible ahead, use a back bearing to maintain direction.

2. The back bearing can be based on:
- A natural feature behind you (e.g., a hill, tree, or rock)
- An artificial mark left by you...
Procedure:

1. When no aiming marks are visible ahead, use a back bearing to maintain direction.

2. The back bearing can be based on:
- A natural feature behind you (e.g., a hill, tree, or rock)
- An artificial mark left by you during the march

3. If the only aiming mark available is poorly defined:
- Keep your eyes constantly on it after taking your bearing.
- Ensure that you do not lose sight of it during movement.

4. In conditions of low visibility, such as snow or fog, use close-in aiming marks and:
- Identify further marks along the correct bearing as you approach each one.
- Follow a straight-line route to ensure accuracy.

5. Make frequent compass checks to confirm that the correct bearing is being maintained.

6. Set your compass for night marching conditions when visibility is low.
aiming marks,back bearing,navigation,visibility,compass use
Use the Center-Hold Method to Determine Magnetic Azimuth
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Open the compass so that the cover forms a straight edge with the base. The lens of the compass is moved out of the way.
2. Place your thumb through the loop, form a steady base with your third and fourth fingers, and extend your...
Procedure:

1. Open the compass so that the cover forms a straight edge with the base. The lens of the compass is moved out of the way.
2. Place your thumb through the loop, form a steady base with your third and fourth fingers, and extend your index finger along the side of the compass.
3. Place the thumb of the other hand between the eyepiece and lens, extend the index finger along the remaining side of the compass, wrap the remaining fingers around the fingers of the other hand, and pull your elbows firmly into your side. This will place the compass between your chin and your belt.
4. To measure an azimuth, turn your entire body toward the object and point the compass cover directly at the object. Look down and read the azimuth from beneath the fixed black index line. This method can be used at night.
5. To keep from going in circles when you are land navigating, stop occasionally to check the azimuth along which you are moving. Also, you can move from object to object along your path by shooting an azimuth to each object and then moving to that object. Repeating this process while you navigate should keep you straight.
compass,azimuth,determine,magnetic,center-hold method,navigation
Alter Direction Using a Compass to Avoid an Obstacle
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
To alter direction using a compass to avoid an obstacle:

1. Use right angles: When encountering an obstacle, change your route by traveling around it in a series of right-angle turns.
2. Maintain original bearing: With an orienteering...
To alter direction using a compass to avoid an obstacle:

1. Use right angles: When encountering an obstacle, change your route by traveling around it in a series of right-angle turns.
2. Maintain original bearing: With an orienteering compass, you can maintain the original magnetic bearing without adjusting the compass setting by using the right angles on the compass plate as guides for direction changes.
3. Follow the compass: After each turn, ensure that the red end of the needle aligns with the north mark on the housing and follow the direction arrow to stay on course.
obstacle avoidance,navigation,compass use
Correcting for Individual Compass Error
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Check compass regularly for individual variation, as each compass may not point exactly to magnetic north.
2. Note any known error on the compass itself for reference.
3. When taking readings, **make allowances for the...
Procedure:

1. Check compass regularly for individual variation, as each compass may not point exactly to magnetic north.
2. Note any known error on the compass itself for reference.
3. When taking readings, make allowances for the individual variation of the compass.
4. Ensure that the compass needle aligns correctly with the markings on the card; if discrepancies are found, seek professional calibration.
compass error, individual variation, compass calibration, magnetic north, navigation
Measure Bearings Using a Compass for Navigation and Map Reading
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. A bearing is the angle measured clockwise from a fixed zero line (which is always north) to a target point.
2. Use a compass to align its needle with true north or grid north, depending on your map's...
Procedure:

1. A bearing is the angle measured clockwise from a fixed zero line (which is always north) to a target point.
2. Use a compass to align its needle with true north or grid north, depending on your map's orientation.
3. Rotate the compass housing until the direction of travel arrow points toward the target feature on the map.
4. Read the bearing from the degree markings on the compass (e.g., 045° for northeast).

Notes:
• Bearings are essential in survival and navigation to determine direction accurately.
• Ensure that your compass is properly calibrated before use.
bearing measurement, compass use, navigation, map reading
Set the Compass to Walk on a Magnetic Bearing
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
To set your compass for walking along a magnetic bearing:

1. Adjust the housing: Rotate the compass housing until the desired magnetic bearing aligns with the index line on the compass plate.
2. Align the needle: Hold the compass flat in...
To set your compass for walking along a magnetic bearing:

1. Adjust the housing: Rotate the compass housing until the desired magnetic bearing aligns with the index line on the compass plate.
2. Align the needle: Hold the compass flat in your hand and rotate your body until the red end of the compass needle points to the north mark on the housing, and is parallel to the meridian lines.
3. Follow the direction arrow: The direction arrow now points along the required magnetic bearing. Walk in this direction.
magnetic bearing,compass setting,navigation
Understand the 32 Points of a Compass and Their Use in Map Reading
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Recognize that there are 32 points on a compass, but only 16 are typically used in map reading.
2. The four cardinal directions (North, East, South, West) divide the circle into four quadrants of **90 degrees...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that there are 32 points on a compass, but only 16 are typically used in map reading.
2. The four cardinal directions (North, East, South, West) divide the circle into four quadrants of 90 degrees each.
3. The remaining twelve intermediate points provide more precise directional references for navigation and map reading.

Notes:
• These 16 points are sufficient for most survival or navigation tasks.
• Use them to align your compass with the map's grid lines (grid north) or true north.
compass points, navigation, map reading, cardinal directions
Understand the Degree System for Compass Accuracy in Navigation
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
Procedure:

1. Recognize that a full circle is divided into 360 degrees, with north at 0° or 360°.
2. Each quadrant of the compass (North, East, South, West) spans 90 degrees.
3. Degrees are subdivided into **minutes and...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that a full circle is divided into 360 degrees, with north at 0° or 360°.
2. Each quadrant of the compass (North, East, South, West) spans 90 degrees.
3. Degrees are subdivided into minutes and seconds:
- One degree = 60 minutes,
- One minute = 60 seconds.
4. In practical navigation, readings to the nearest degree are generally sufficient (e.g., 045° for northeast).

Notes:
• This system is used in survival and map reading scenarios for precise directional measurements.
• Some organizations, such as the Australian Army, use mils instead of degrees (6,400 mils = 360°).
degree system, compass accuracy, navigation, map reading
Use Back Bearings with an Orienteering Compass
Standard Source: Aids to Survival
To use a back bearing with an orienteering compass:

1. Reverse the compass: Turn around so that you are facing approximately in the direction you have come from, and reverse the compass so that the direction arrow points toward you.
2. **Align...
To use a back bearing with an orienteering compass:

1. Reverse the compass: Turn around so that you are facing approximately in the direction you have come from, and reverse the compass so that the direction arrow points toward you.
2. Align the needle: Rotate your body until the red end of the compass needle points to the north mark on the housing and is parallel to the meridian lines.
3. Travel in the new direction: Walk in the direction indicated by the rear of the compass plate.
back bearing,compass use,navigation
Boxing the Compass
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Boxing the Compass

Used to enumerate compass points in order, beginning with north and working around the circle.

• Start at North.
• Proceed in this order:
- North by East
- North-East
- North-East by North
- North-East by East
...
Boxing the Compass

Used to enumerate compass points in order, beginning with north and working around the circle.

• Start at North.
• Proceed in this order:
- North by East
- North-East
- North-East by North
- North-East by East
- East
- East by South
- East-South-East
- East by South
- South
- South by East
- South-East
- South-East by South
- South-West
- South-West by South
- West
- West by North
- West-North-West
- West by North
- North-West
- North-West by West
- North
• Continue this sequence until all points are covered.
boxing the compass, compass directions, navigation
Understand and Account for Compass Errors
Reference Source: Aids to Survival
When using a magnetic compass, be aware of the following potential errors:

• Magnetic variation (declination): The difference between true north and magnetic north, which varies depending on your location on Earth. This must be accounted for...
When using a magnetic compass, be aware of the following potential errors:

• Magnetic variation (declination): The difference between true north and magnetic north, which varies depending on your location on Earth. This must be accounted for when navigating with precision.
• Local magnetic interference: Objects such as metal structures, rocks containing iron ore, or electrical equipment can distort the compass needle’s reading. Avoid using a compass near these sources of interference.
compass errors,magnetic variation,navigation accuracy
Use a Watch as a Compass
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Using a Watch to Determine Direction

A watch can be used to find due south during daylight hours.

• In the morning:
- Point the hour hand toward the sun.
- Due south is halfway between the hour hand and noon (12 o'clock).
• **In the...
Using a Watch to Determine Direction

A watch can be used to find due south during daylight hours.

• In the morning:
- Point the hour hand toward the sun.
- Due south is halfway between the hour hand and noon (12 o'clock).
• In the afternoon:
- Point the hour hand toward the sun.
- Due south is halfway backward from the hour hand to noon.

Example:
• At 8 A.M., point the hour hand at the sun. Halfway between 8 and 12 (noon) is 10 o'clock, which points due south.
• At 4 P.M., point the hour hand at the sun. Halfway backward from 4 to 12 is 2 o'clock, which points due south.

Note: The watch must be set to local time, not daylight saving time.

If the sun is obscured by clouds:
• Find an open space and hold your knife point upright on the watch dial.
• A faint shadow will indicate where the sun actually is (unless clouds are very heavy).
watch compass, navigation without compass, using a watch for direction
Use the M2 Compass for Azimuth and Angle of Site Readings
Reference Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Objective: Learn how to use the M2 compass to measure azimuths and angles of site.

Procedure:
1. Understand the M2 Compass Features: The M2 compass is a rustproof, dustproof magnetic instrument with an azimuth scale from 0 to 6400 mils...
Objective: Learn how to use the M2 compass to measure azimuths and angles of site.

Procedure:
1. Understand the M2 Compass Features: The M2 compass is a rustproof, dustproof magnetic instrument with an azimuth scale from 0 to 6400 mils and an angle-of-site scale from 1200-0-1200 mils.
2. Measure Azimuths:
- Hold the compass level and steady in front of you, ensuring that the magnetic needle is free to rotate.
- Rotate your body until the north end of the magnetic needle aligns with the 0-mil mark on the azimuth scale.
- Read the azimuth from the scale where the index line intersects the scale.
3. Measure Angle of Site:
- Hold the compass level and steady, pointing it toward the target.
- Align the base of the angle-of-site scale with the object being measured (e.g., a distant landmark).
- Read the angle from the scale where the line of sight intersects the scale.
4. Calibrate for Grid Azimuths: If needed, calibrate the compass to provide grid azimuth readings based on local declination values.
5. Use Without Calibration: The M2 compass can also be used directly to determine magnetic azimuths without calibration.
M2 compass,azimuth measurement,angle of site,map navigation,declination adjustment

Aviation Operations (14) -

Understanding Aircraft Operations in Arctic Conditions
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Overview:
Aircraft operations in the arctic and subarctic regions require special considerations due to environmental factors.

Key Points:
• Aircraft are nearly indispensable for operations in the arctic, and invaluable in **subarctic...
Overview:
Aircraft operations in the arctic and subarctic regions require special considerations due to environmental factors.

Key Points:
• Aircraft are nearly indispensable for operations in the arctic, and invaluable in subarctic operations.
• The general principles for employing tactical air support, as outlined in CFP 311, apply equally well in northern operations.
• A comprehensive understanding of the use of air, and the effect of the environment on tactical air support operations in the north is a particularly essential qualification for command and staff deployed in the north.
arctic aviation, cold weather flying, aircraft operations, northern conditions
Winter Aircraft Operations and Environmental Challenges
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Overview:
Aircraft operations during the winter months in the north face significant environmental challenges.

Key Winter Challenges:
• Lack of daylight restricts short take-off and landing (STOL) aircraft operations on unlighted...
Overview:
Aircraft operations during the winter months in the north face significant environmental challenges.

Key Winter Challenges:
• Lack of daylight restricts short take-off and landing (STOL) aircraft operations on unlighted strips, limiting landings to occupied stations for four months of the year.
• Extreme low temperatures increase operational difficulty.
• Ice fog is a major restriction during winter, occurring frequently at temperatures of -37°C or lower but may occur as warm as -5°C.
• Visibility in ice fog can be reduced to almost zero at ground level, though it does not rise above 30 m (100 ft).
• Ice fog can be self-induced by rotor systems and engine exhausts.
• It takes 15 to 30 minutes for ice fog to dissipate after aircraft take off but does not cause icing of aircraft due to the absence of water droplets.
winter flying challenges, ice fog conditions, northern aviation safety
Aircraft Performance in Cold Weather Conditions
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Overview:
Cold weather conditions affect aircraft performance and require careful consideration.

Key Points About Aircraft Performance in the North:
• Cold dense air results in a low density altitude, which enhances aircraft...
Overview:
Cold weather conditions affect aircraft performance and require careful consideration.

Key Points About Aircraft Performance in the North:
• Cold dense air results in a low density altitude, which enhances aircraft performance.
• Pilots must study operating data charts carefully to ensure that recommended power settings are not exceeded in low temperatures.
cold weather aircraft performance, density altitude effects, northern flying safety
Assess Aircraft Payload Capabilities in Northern Operations
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Consider the following factors when planning aircraft operations in northern areas:

1. Payload Reduction: The payload capability of aircraft is reduced due to:
- Added weight from ski or float installation.
- Required survival...
Consider the following factors when planning aircraft operations in northern areas:

1. Payload Reduction: The payload capability of aircraft is reduced due to:
- Added weight from ski or float installation.
- Required survival equipment.
2. Distance Limitations: Long distances between suitable airfields limit the use of air support.
3. Fuel Load Increase: Fuel loads will be higher because of:
- Long distances from supporting airfield to forward bases and lodgement areas.
- Long distances to alternative airfields, which may be the same as take-off airfields.
4. Compensating Factors: The reduction in payload may be offset by increased aircraft performance due to favourable density altitude conditions.
aircraft,payload,cold weather,aviation,northern operations
Challenges of Summer Air Operations in the North
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Overview:
Air operations during the summer months in the north face specific challenges.

Key Challenges During Summer:
• Increasing amounts of fog and low cloud formations over the ocean.
• Increased likelihood of **icing...
Overview:
Air operations during the summer months in the north face specific challenges.

Key Challenges During Summer:
• Increasing amounts of fog and low cloud formations over the ocean.
• Increased likelihood of icing conditions.
• Presence of ice crystal haze.
• Landings, except on prepared surfaces, become increasingly difficult.
• Visibility improves somewhat in late June and most of July over continental areas but is never as good as in winter.
• In the arctic islands, visibility deteriorates quickly after early July due to fog blowing in from open water.
summer flying challenges, northern fog conditions, aircraft icing
Identify and Utilize Airfield and Landing Strip Information in Northern Operations
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Ensure the following information is available to commanders:

1. Airfield Data: Information on all airfields, airstrips, and emergency strips in the area of operations.
2. Esker Utilization: Pay particular attention to eskers and raised...
Ensure the following information is available to commanders:

1. Airfield Data: Information on all airfields, airstrips, and emergency strips in the area of operations.
2. Esker Utilization: Pay particular attention to eskers and raised beaches:
- Many eskers provide emergency landing strips for light and medium aircraft.
- Most eskers run north-west to south-east, aligning with prevailing winds.
- Eskers are particularly valuable during summer when fully drained and during fall before snow drifts make them unsafe.
- In winter, a frozen lead surrounded by long ridges indicates ice thick enough to support light aircraft.
airfield information,northern operations,esker landing strips
Navigate in Darkness in Northern Areas with Limited Visibility
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Consider the following when navigating during darkness in northern areas:

1. Visual Navigation Challenges: Visual navigation is extremely difficult due to:
- Lack of lights in sparsely populated regions.
2. Snow Reflection Aid:...
Consider the following when navigating during darkness in northern areas:

1. Visual Navigation Challenges: Visual navigation is extremely difficult due to:
- Lack of lights in sparsely populated regions.
2. Snow Reflection Aid: Reflection from snow-covered terrain may serve as an aid to visibility under some circumstances.
3. Mountain Pass Navigation Risks: Navigation through mountain passes after dark under overcast conditions is not recommended except in emergency.
dark navigation,northern visibility,mountain pass risks
Navigate in Low-Level Conditions in Northern Areas
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Challenges of low-level navigation in northern areas include:

1. Terrain Similarity: The terrain appears uniform, making it difficult to distinguish features.
2. Map Detail Limitations: Many maps lack sufficient detail for effective...
Challenges of low-level navigation in northern areas include:

1. Terrain Similarity: The terrain appears uniform, making it difficult to distinguish features.
2. Map Detail Limitations: Many maps lack sufficient detail for effective navigation.
3. Winter Navigation Challenges: During winter:
- The arctic landscape appears as a vast expanse of snow and ice.
- Very little ground is visible due to snowfall and storms.
- Winter storms drift snow into gullies and valleys, obscuring features.
4. Loss of Landscape Details: Features indicated on topographical maps such as shorelines and coastlines may not be recognizable.
low-level navigation,northern terrain,winter navigation
Optimal Season for Flying Operations in the North
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Overview:
There are specific seasons and conditions that make flying operations more viable in the north.

Best Season for Flying:
• Late winter (February to May) is generally the best season for flying operations in the north, although...
Overview:
There are specific seasons and conditions that make flying operations more viable in the north.

Best Season for Flying:
• Late winter (February to May) is generally the best season for flying operations in the north, although this varies slightly with latitude.

Conditions During This Period:
• Increasing amounts of daylight.
• Surfaces are generally still frozen.
• Not enough open water to cause long periods of fog, low visibility, or indistinct horizons.
• Weather is mainly clear and cold, but temperatures have moderated somewhat.
arctic flying season, northern weather conditions, optimal flight time
Plan for Increased Fuel Consumption in Northern Flights
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Consider the following when planning extended flights in northern areas:

1. Higher Fuel Consumption: Due to:
- Lengthened warm-up periods.
- Fuel burning heaters used for warmth.
2. Refuelling Challenges: Refuelling becomes a...
Consider the following when planning extended flights in northern areas:

1. Higher Fuel Consumption: Due to:
- Lengthened warm-up periods.
- Fuel burning heaters used for warmth.
2. Refuelling Challenges: Refuelling becomes a major problem due to:
- Long distances from airfields.
- Inability of surface transport to move fuel to isolated points.
fuel consumption,northern flights,refuelling challenges
Study Climatological History for Flight Planning in Northern Areas
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
To plan flight operations effectively in northern areas:

1. Analyze Weather Patterns: Study the climatological history of the operational area to determine:
- The probable frequency of poor weather conditions.
- How these conditions...
To plan flight operations effectively in northern areas:

1. Analyze Weather Patterns: Study the climatological history of the operational area to determine:
- The probable frequency of poor weather conditions.
- How these conditions may limit or preclude flight operations.
weather analysis,northern flight planning,climatology
Turbulence in Mountainous Regions and Its Impact on Flights
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Overview:
Mountain passes can present turbulence that affects flight operations.

Key Points About Turbulence:
• Some degree of turbulence is frequently present in mountain passes.
• When this condition is severe, flights may be...
Overview:
Mountain passes can present turbulence that affects flight operations.

Key Points About Turbulence:
• Some degree of turbulence is frequently present in mountain passes.
• When this condition is severe, flights may be prohibitive or significantly lengthened due to the need for alternative routes.
mountain turbulence, flight route planning, northern aviation safety
Use Inertial Navigation Systems in High Magnetic Latitude Areas
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
In high magnetic latitude areas, use the following:

1. Magnetic Compass Limitations: The magnetic compass becomes less reliable due to proximity to the magnetic pole.
2. Alternative Navigation Systems: Use low precession-rate...
In high magnetic latitude areas, use the following:

1. Magnetic Compass Limitations: The magnetic compass becomes less reliable due to proximity to the magnetic pole.
2. Alternative Navigation Systems: Use low precession-rate directional inertial systems for accurate navigation.
inertial navigation,magnetic latitude,compass limitations
Use Lakes for Navigation in Northern Areas with Caution
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Consider the following when using lakes for navigation:

1. Lake Utilization: Lakes may be used in conjunction with pilotage.
2. Spring Thaw Challenges: During spring thaw:
- The number of lakes increases due to melting snow.
-...
Consider the following when using lakes for navigation:

1. Lake Utilization: Lakes may be used in conjunction with pilotage.
2. Spring Thaw Challenges: During spring thaw:
- The number of lakes increases due to melting snow.
- Accurate identification becomes extremely difficult due to changes in outline caused by snow drifts.
3. Orientation Requirement: Pilots must ensure proper orientation at all times when using lakes for navigation.
lake navigation,northern areas,spring thaw challenges

Cold Weather Navigation (12) -

Cold Weather Navigation Techniques and Equipment Use
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Use magnetic compass: Rely on the magnetic compass as the primary navigation tool in cold weather conditions.
2. Combine with map and dead reckoning: Use a combination of map reading, dead reckoning, and other techniques...
Procedure:

1. Use magnetic compass: Rely on the magnetic compass as the primary navigation tool in cold weather conditions.
2. Combine with map and dead reckoning: Use a combination of map reading, dead reckoning, and other techniques to ensure accurate navigation.
3. Limit use of astro compass: While the astro compass is taught for training purposes, it cannot be relied on as a standard aid due to limited visibility in cold weather conditions.
cold weather navigation, magnetic compass use
Crossing Water Bodies During Spring Break-Up
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Exercise extreme caution when crossing large streams and lakes during spring break-up.
2. Check shorelines and inlets/outlets of streams thoroughly before attempting to cross.
3. Ensure that exit areas on the other side are strong...
Procedure:

1. Exercise extreme caution when crossing large streams and lakes during spring break-up.
2. Check shorelines and inlets/outlets of streams thoroughly before attempting to cross.
3. Ensure that exit areas on the other side are strong enough to support vehicles.
4. Be aware that water receding below ice cover renders the ice unsafe, even if it appears intact.

Notes:
• Ice strength is compromised when water is no longer under it.
• Always verify the stability of both entry and exit points before crossing.
spring break-up, ice safety, water crossings, shoreline inspection
Navigating Muskeg and Floating Bogs in Summer
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Identify muskeg or swamp areas covered with moss and lichens as potential hazards.
2. Avoid rupturing the moss layer, as it can lead to unstable ground above permafrost levels.
3. Use a long probe (Dole) to determine the location...
Procedure:

1. Identify muskeg or swamp areas covered with moss and lichens as potential hazards.
2. Avoid rupturing the moss layer, as it can lead to unstable ground above permafrost levels.
3. Use a long probe (Dole) to determine the location of floating bogs before crossing.
4. Avoid floating bogs entirely by conducting careful reconnaissance and selecting alternative routes.
5. In areas with glacier boulders just below the surface, be prepared for potential damage to suspension systems and tracks.

Notes:
• Floating bogs may support a person but not vehicles.
• Route selection is critical in muskeg environments.
muskeg navigation, floating bogs, probe use, glacier boulders
Navigating in Cold Weather Environments
High Priority Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. General Navigation Principles: Mapreading and navigation under cold weather conditions follow the same principles as in temperate zones, but additional precautions are necessary due to environmental challenges.

2....
Procedure:

1. General Navigation Principles: Mapreading and navigation under cold weather conditions follow the same principles as in temperate zones, but additional precautions are necessary due to environmental challenges.

2. Environmental Challenges: The following conditions make accurate navigation very difficult:
- (1) Lack of adequate large-scale maps in sparsely populated areas increases reliance on aerial photographs.
- (2) Photos may be hard to read and interpret due to the absence of relief, contrast, and manmade works for reference points.
- (3) Dense forests and wildernesses offer few landmarks and limit visibility. Barren tundra areas north of the tree line also lack landmarks.
- (4) In winter, short daylight, fog, snowfall, blizzards, drifting snow, and overcast skies with snow-covered terrain can create a phenomenon called whiteout, making recognition of terrain irregularities extremely difficult.
- (5) Heavy snow may completely obliterate existing tracks, trails, outlines of small lakes, and similar landmarks. Terrain appearance in winter differs significantly from summer, so careful identification of landmarks on the ground and in aerial photos is essential.
- (6) Magnetic disturbances can make compass readings unreliable or difficult to interpret.
- (7) Magnetic declination varies considerably across different localities and must be considered when transposing from a map to a compass.
- (8) Handling maps, compasses, and other navigation instruments in low temperatures with bare hands is difficult. Removing handgear may only be possible for very short periods of time.
cold weather navigation,map reading,whiteout,magnetic declination,aerial photographs,landmarks,compass use
Perform Land Navigation in Cold Weather Conditions
High Priority Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. Know the compass deviation and magnetic declination to ensure accurate navigation.
2. Become familiar with landmarks, ridge lines, direction of river flow, and prevailing wind direction before entering unknown areas.
3. Estimate...
Procedure:

1. Know the compass deviation and magnetic declination to ensure accurate navigation.
2. Become familiar with landmarks, ridge lines, direction of river flow, and prevailing wind direction before entering unknown areas.
3. Estimate distance traveled using methods outlined in paragraph 6-9.
4. Keep the parka fur hood with wire loop away from the compass when taking readings to avoid interference.
land navigation,cold weather,compass deviation,magnetic declination,parka hood interference
Tracked Vehicle Operations in Muskeg and Soft Terrain
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. When crossing open muskeg, make your own tracks to avoid destabilizing the ground.
2. Avoid abrupt turns that could cause vehicles to become stuck or belly down.
3. Ensure tracked vehicles have normal rubber track pads, trim...
Procedure:

1. When crossing open muskeg, make your own tracks to avoid destabilizing the ground.
2. Avoid abrupt turns that could cause vehicles to become stuck or belly down.
3. Ensure tracked vehicles have normal rubber track pads, trim vanes, and skirting mounted for better traction.
4. Recovery from muskeg is exceptionally difficult; winch the vehicle to a solid area before attempting recovery.
5. In most cases, at least two additional vehicles are required for successful recovery operations.

Notes:
• Muskeg can quickly trap vehicles if not navigated carefully.
• Recovery in such terrain requires careful planning and multiple vehicles.
muskeg operations, vehicle recovery, track pads, abrupt turns
Navigating During Spring Break-Up
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Avoid following in the tracks of other vehicles during spring break-up.
2. Select routes where the permafrost table is at least 15 cm (6 in) from the surface to prevent becoming mired even in mid-summer.
3. Prefer gravel...
Procedure:

1. Avoid following in the tracks of other vehicles during spring break-up.
2. Select routes where the permafrost table is at least 15 cm (6 in) from the surface to prevent becoming mired even in mid-summer.
3. Prefer gravel ridges as travel routes when available.
4. In wooded areas, choose shaded slopes of hills, shaded sides of woods, ground with good moss cover, and crests where drainage is best for route selection.

Notes:
• Spring thaw conditions can severely restrict tracked vehicle movement.
• Proper route selection is critical to avoid miring.
spring break-up, permafrost table, gravel ridges, shaded slopes, navigation
Navigating During Spring Crystalization of Snow
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Be aware that during spring, snow may crystallize and lose all bearing capacity.
2. Avoid movement in areas with crystallized snow as tracked vehicles can easily become high-centred.
3. Movement is often halted due to the lack of...
Procedure:

1. Be aware that during spring, snow may crystallize and lose all bearing capacity.
2. Avoid movement in areas with crystallized snow as tracked vehicles can easily become high-centred.
3. Movement is often halted due to the lack of firm footing for recovery operations.
4. In such conditions, prioritize minimizing traffic and avoid heavy loads on vehicles.

Notes:
• High permafrost levels prevent bogging in mud or gravel but do not eliminate movement challenges.
• Traffic should be limited during periods of crystallized snow.
spring crystalization, snow bearing capacity, vehicle movement, high centring
Preventing Fires in Tracked Vehicles During Summer Operations
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Inspect sprockets and road wheels regularly during summer operations, especially in Arctic regions.
2. Be aware that small limestone rocks (approximately 10 by 5 by 5 cm (4 by 2 by 2 in)) can become locked between rubber...
Procedure:

1. Inspect sprockets and road wheels regularly during summer operations, especially in Arctic regions.
2. Be aware that small limestone rocks (approximately 10 by 5 by 5 cm (4 by 2 by 2 in)) can become locked between rubber wheels due to centrifugal force.
3. Friction from these rocks may cause fires when crossing loose gravel areas with high surface-water content.
4. If this condition is encountered, halt the vehicle and reverse 1 to 1-1/2 in (3 to 4 ft) before continuing forward.

Notes:
• This issue is common on loose gravel surfaces with high water content.
• Immediate action can prevent potential fires.
vehicle fire prevention, gravel crossings, limestone rocks, summer operations
Summer Navigation in Northern Regions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. In summer, northern regions are characterized by lakes, streams, and swamps that impede movement.
2. In the Arctic, use eskers or gravel ridges for better travel conditions.
3. Medium to heavy tracked vehicles can move on most...
Procedure:

1. In summer, northern regions are characterized by lakes, streams, and swamps that impede movement.
2. In the Arctic, use eskers or gravel ridges for better travel conditions.
3. Medium to heavy tracked vehicles can move on most terrain in Arctic islands due to high permafrost levels, except where steep hills or cuttings restrict movement.
4. When climbing hills, ascend straight up to avoid slipping surface gravel caused by ice at the permafrost level (10-15 cm (4-6 in) north of latitude 75°).

Notes:
• Ice may remain on lakes north of latitude 75° throughout summer but can be unsafe due to candling.
• Route selection is crucial for safe and efficient movement.
summer navigation, northern regions, permafrost level, hill climbing
Train Personnel in Advanced Navigational Techniques for Northern Operations
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Objective: Prepare personnel with advanced navigational skills required for northern operations.

Procedure:
1. Provide specialized training to commanders at all levels, reconnaissance personnel, and messengers in advanced navigational...
Objective: Prepare personnel with advanced navigational skills required for northern operations.

Procedure:
1. Provide specialized training to commanders at all levels, reconnaissance personnel, and messengers in advanced navigational techniques.
2. Ensure training is tailored to the specific needs of the operation area.
3. Refer to Part One for detailed information on navigation techniques.
advanced navigation,northern operations,reconnaissance personnel,messengers,navigation training
Vehicle Load and Traffic Management During Spring Break-Up
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Reduce vehicle load during the spring period to improve mobility.
2. Limit traffic to nighttime when temperatures are below freezing.
3. Allow engineers time for maintenance during the day without interruption.
4. Avoid heavy...
Procedure:

1. Reduce vehicle load during the spring period to improve mobility.
2. Limit traffic to nighttime when temperatures are below freezing.
3. Allow engineers time for maintenance during the day without interruption.
4. Avoid heavy traffic when nights become warm enough that roads no longer freeze, as this can turn roads into morasses.

Notes:
• Traffic management is critical to avoid turning roads into impassable conditions.
• Nighttime operations are safer and allow for better maintenance planning.
spring break-up, vehicle load reduction, traffic management, nighttime operations

Map Reading And Land Navigation (12) -

Be Cautious When Converting Azimuths to Back Azimuths
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
WARNING:
When converting azimuths into back azimuths, extreme care should be exercised when adding or subtracting the 180 degrees.

• A simple mathematical mistake could cause disastrous consequences, such as incorrect navigation or...
WARNING:
When converting azimuths into back azimuths, extreme care should be exercised when adding or subtracting the 180 degrees.

• A simple mathematical mistake could cause disastrous consequences, such as incorrect navigation or misdirection in a mission.
azimuth conversion,map navigation,warning
Know Where You Are (Step 1) - Land Navigation
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

To know where you are on the map and on the ground at all times, follow these steps:

1. Understand directional orientation: Know your current direction relative to north, south, east, and west.
2. **Determine distance to...
Procedure:

To know where you are on the map and on the ground at all times, follow these steps:

1. Understand directional orientation: Know your current direction relative to north, south, east, and west.
2. Determine distance to objective: Estimate how far you are from your destination using map scales or pacing techniques.
3. Recognize landmarks and features: Identify natural and man-made features such as rivers, roads, hills, and buildings that can help with navigation.
4. Assess terrain advantages/disadvantages: Be aware of impassable areas, enemy positions, danger zones, and how the terrain may affect your movement or concealment.
5. Use map reading skills: Know how to read a topographic map and identify specific features such as contour lines, elevation changes, and landforms.
6. Estimate direction: Use a compass or natural indicators (e.g., sun position) to determine your heading.
7. Measure distances: Use pacing, measuring tools, or map scales to estimate how far you have traveled or need to travel.
8. Plot and estimate position by resection: Use known landmarks to triangulate your current location on the map.

Notes:
• This step is critical for all tactical movements and should be performed continuously throughout the mission.
