Classify Rivers Based on Difficulty - Class 1 to Class 6
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Understanding river classifications is essential for safe navigation in swiftwater environments.

Rivers are classified based on their difficulty and the level of danger they pose to navigators.

### River Classification Overview:
• Class 1:...
Understanding river classifications is essential for safe navigation in swiftwater environments.

Rivers are classified based on their difficulty and the level of danger they pose to navigators.

### River Classification Overview:
• Class 1: Moving water, few obstacles, small ripples or waves. Safe for all skill levels.
• Class 2: Moving water, clear channels, wide waves to 3', no scouting required. Suitable for beginners with some experience.
• Class 3: Rapids, narrow channels that require complex maneuvering, waves capable of flipping rafts or swamping canoes, scouting required. Requires intermediate skills and experience.
• Class 4: Long hazardous rapids, precise boat handling in rough water, scouting required, rescues hazardous. Requires advanced skills and professional guidance.
• Class 5: Severe, extended, extremely violent rapids, professional boat handlers and scouting required. Rescues are extremely hazardous, situations are life-threatening.
• Class 6: Only for expert/professional river runners. Rescue may be impossible. Not recommended for anyone without extensive experience.

### Key Points:
• River classifications help in assessing the level of danger and determining appropriate safety measures.
• Always match your skill level with the river classification to ensure a safe navigation experience.
• Class 6 rivers should be avoided unless you are an expert with professional guidance.
river classification,class 1,class 2,class 3,class 4,class 5,class 6,navigation,survival
Understand Swiftwater Terminology - Cushion and Pillow
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Understanding cushions and pillows is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

A cushion, also known as a pillow, refers to the build-up of water on the upstream side of obstacles or the outside of curves.

These features can create...
Understanding cushions and pillows is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

A cushion, also known as a pillow, refers to the build-up of water on the upstream side of obstacles or the outside of curves.

These features can create powerful forces that may trap individuals or objects in their path.

### Key Points:
• Cushions and pillows provide temporary refuge from fast-moving currents but should not be relied upon for long-term safety.
• These areas are dangerous due to the strong currents and potential for entrapment.
• Awareness of these features helps in planning safe routes and avoiding dangerous areas.
cushion,pillow,swiftwater terminology,navigation,survival
Understand Swiftwater Terminology - Downstream V and Upstream V
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Understanding downstream V and upstream V is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

The downstream V, also known as the tonal, represents the largest volume of water or main channel.

The upstream V is caused by obstacles...
Understanding downstream V and upstream V is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

The downstream V, also known as the tonal, represents the largest volume of water or main channel.

The upstream V is caused by obstacles submerged just below the surface, creating a visible V-shaped pattern in the water.

### Key Points:
• The downstream V indicates the direction and strength of the current.
• The upstream V can indicate the presence of submerged obstacles that may pose a danger to navigation.
• Recognizing these features helps in planning safe routes and avoiding dangerous areas.
downstream v,upstream v,swiftwater terminology,navigation,survival
Understand Swiftwater Terminology - Eddy and Eddy Fence
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Understanding eddies and eddy fences is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

An eddy is a horizontal reversal of current, created when the flow is blocked by an obstacle. This blockage creates a vacuum that is filled by water flowing...
Understanding eddies and eddy fences is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

An eddy is a horizontal reversal of current, created when the flow is blocked by an obstacle. This blockage creates a vacuum that is filled by water flowing upstream.

The eddy fence is the reversal line between downstream current and the eddy. It can be very strong and may vary in height by several inches.

### Key Points:
• Eddies provide temporary refuge from fast-moving currents but should not be relied upon for long-term safety.
• The eddy fence acts as a barrier that can trap individuals or objects if approached improperly.
• Recognizing these features helps in planning safe routes and avoiding dangerous areas.
eddy,eddy fence,swiftwater terminology,navigation,survival
Understand Swiftwater Terminology - Hydraulic Effect
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Understanding the hydraulic effect is crucial for safe navigation in swiftwater environments.

The hydraulic or hydraulic effect refers to the movement of water caused by pressure. This phenomenon can create powerful forces that may trap...
Understanding the hydraulic effect is crucial for safe navigation in swiftwater environments.

The hydraulic or hydraulic effect refers to the movement of water caused by pressure. This phenomenon can create powerful forces that may trap individuals or objects underwater, making it essential to recognize and avoid these areas.

### Key Points:
• Hydraulic effects occur when water is forced through narrow channels or obstacles, creating high-pressure zones.
• These areas are dangerous due to the strong currents and potential for entrapment.
• Awareness of hydraulic effects can help prevent accidents in swiftwater environments.
hydraulic effect,swiftwater terminology,navigation,survival
Understand Swiftwater Terminology - River Right and River Left
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Understanding river right and river left is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

River Right refers to the right side of the river when looking downstream.

River Left refers to the left side of the river when looking...
Understanding river right and river left is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

River Right refers to the right side of the river when looking downstream.

River Left refers to the left side of the river when looking downstream.

### Key Points:
• Knowing the difference between river right and river left helps in communication and navigation.
• These terms are used consistently in swiftwater environments for clarity and safety.
• Awareness of these features is critical for safe navigation in swiftwater environments.
river right,river left,swiftwater terminology,navigation,survival
Understand Swiftwater Terminology - Standing Wave and Haystack
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Understanding standing waves and haystacks is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

A standing wave, also known as a haystack, refers to a series of waves in the main channel caused by obstacles, ledges, or increases in flow rate,...
Understanding standing waves and haystacks is essential for safe swiftwater navigation.

A standing wave, also known as a haystack, refers to a series of waves in the main channel caused by obstacles, ledges, or increases in flow rate, gradient, or volume.

These features can create powerful forces that may trap individuals or objects in their path.

### Key Points:
• Standing waves and haystacks are common in swiftwater environments and should be approached with caution.
• These features can cause injury or death if not navigated properly.
• Awareness of these features helps in planning safe routes and avoiding dangerous areas.
standing wave,haystack,swiftwater terminology,navigation,survival

Route Selection (9) -

Consider Vehicle Capabilities When Selecting a Route for Mounted Movement
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
When determining a route to be used when mounted, consider the capabilities of the vehicles to be used. Most military vehicles are limited in the degree of slope they can climb and the type of terrain they can negotiate. Swamps, thickly wooded...
When determining a route to be used when mounted, consider the capabilities of the vehicles to be used. Most military vehicles are limited in the degree of slope they can climb and the type of terrain they can negotiate. Swamps, thickly wooded areas, or deep streams may present no problems to dismounted Soldiers, but the same terrain may completely stop mounted Soldiers. The navigator must consider this when selecting a route.

(1) Most vehicles can knock down a tree. The bigger the vehicle, the bigger the tree it can knock down. Vehicles cannot knock down several trees at once. It is best to find paths between trees that are wide enough for your vehicle. Military vehicles are designed to climb 60-percent slopes on a dry, firm surface.

(2) You can easily determine approximate slope by looking at the route you have selected on a map. A contour line in any 100 meters of map distance on that route indicates a 10-percent slope, two contour lines indicate 20–percent slope, and so forth. If there are four contour lines in any 100 meters, look for another route.

(3) Side slope is even more important than the slope you can climb. Normally, a 30-percent slope is the maximum in good weather. When traversing a side slope, progress slowly and without turns. Rocks, stumps, or sharp turns can cause you to throw the downhill track under the vehicle, which would mean a big recovery task.

(4) For tactical reasons, you will often want to move in draws or valleys because they provide cover. However, side slopes force you to move slowly.
vehicle capabilities,map reading,navigation,route selection
Considering Snow Conditions for Route Selection
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Considering Snow Conditions for Route Selection

1. In early winter, there is more snow in open areas than in dense forests.
2. In late winter, the reverse is true (more snow in dense forests).
3. In early spring, the most snow is found in...
Considering Snow Conditions for Route Selection

1. In early winter, there is more snow in open areas than in dense forests.
2. In late winter, the reverse is true (more snow in dense forests).
3. In early spring, the most snow is found in ditches, ravines, and the lee side of hills.
snow conditions, route selection, winter, spring
Navigating Obstacles in Route Selection
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Navigating Obstacles in Route Selection

1. Even minor obstacles impede the progress of the main body, especially when pulling toboggans.
2. If possible, obstacles should be bypassed.
3. For larger obstacles (e.g., steep hills, ridges, or steep...
Navigating Obstacles in Route Selection

1. Even minor obstacles impede the progress of the main body, especially when pulling toboggans.
2. If possible, obstacles should be bypassed.
3. For larger obstacles (e.g., steep hills, ridges, or steep river banks), multiple trails should be broken across them to allow the main body to cross on a wide front.
4. Brush should be cut below the level of the ski/snowshoe track to avoid it becoming entangled in bindings and tow ropes.
obstacles, trail breaking, brush cutting, wide front crossing
Planning Routes for Night Marches
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Planning Routes for Night Marches

1. Skiing or snowshoeing at night is very slow and exhausting.
2. The trail for a night march must be broken over the easiest possible terrain.
3. Navigation is particularly difficult, so every navigational aid...
Planning Routes for Night Marches

1. Skiing or snowshoeing at night is very slow and exhausting.
2. The trail for a night march must be broken over the easiest possible terrain.
3. Navigation is particularly difficult, so every navigational aid should be used (e.g., streams, ridge lines, edges of woods).
4. A trail broken for a night march will be much longer than one broken for a day march.
5. Depending on weather conditions, a guide must be left at any point where the main body could take the wrong course.
night marches, navigation aids, trail breaking, guides
Route Selection in Enemy Areas
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Route Selection in Enemy Areas

1. When marching in forward areas, the trail will normally be broken where the terrain affords the best concealment.
2. Less emphasis will be placed on ease of movement.
3. It may be necessary for the...
Route Selection in Enemy Areas

1. When marching in forward areas, the trail will normally be broken where the terrain affords the best concealment.
2. Less emphasis will be placed on ease of movement.
3. It may be necessary for the trail-breaking party to send detachments to clear terrain features near the trail.
4. In some instances, these detachments may have to remain on these terrain features until the main body has passed in order to provide security.
5. These security forces will then fall in the line of march at the rear of the main body.
6. In some cases, it may be desirable to create numerous false trails that crisscross and angle off in different directions to deceive the enemy.
enemy areas, concealment, security detachments, false trails
Selecting a Route in Mountainous Terrain
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Route Selection in Mountainous Terrain

1. If possible, the trail should be broken along valleys, preferably on frozen rivers or streams.
2. If it is necessary to travel on mountain slopes, gentle traverses should be used to climb or descend.
3....
Route Selection in Mountainous Terrain

1. If possible, the trail should be broken along valleys, preferably on frozen rivers or streams.
2. If it is necessary to travel on mountain slopes, gentle traverses should be used to climb or descend.
3. A very careful study of the map and terrain should be made to select the slope with the least number of re-entrants.
4. Once the trail has reached the desired altitude, every effort should be made to follow the contour of the slope.
mountainous terrain, frozen rivers, gentle traverses, contour following
Selecting a Route in Open Terrain
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Route Selection in Open Terrain

1. Normally, only one track will be broken when crossing open ground.
2. This track will usually follow beside the tree line.
3. If more than one track has been ordered, they should be widely dispersed (a minimum...
Route Selection in Open Terrain

1. Normally, only one track will be broken when crossing open ground.
2. This track will usually follow beside the tree line.
3. If more than one track has been ordered, they should be widely dispersed (a minimum of 100 yards between tracks) and follow natural terrain features.
4. In some instances, such as when speed is essential and open terrain is extensive, oversnow vehicles may be used to break the trail.
5. If using vehicles, the trail-breaking party will ski-jore behind the vehicles.
open terrain, route selection, trail breaking, oversnow vehicles
Selecting a Route in Water Routes
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Route Selection in Water Routes

1. Frozen lakes, rivers, and streams offer the most suitable routes for trails.
2. Before using these water routes, the thickness of the ice must be tested.
3. A test should also be made to determine whether...
Route Selection in Water Routes

1. Frozen lakes, rivers, and streams offer the most suitable routes for trails.
2. Before using these water routes, the thickness of the ice must be tested.
3. A test should also be made to determine whether there is water on top of the ice (see Appendix 1, Annex B).
4. If this condition exists, either the water route must be abandoned or snow piled so as to form a raised trail.
5. The water will cause ice to form on the bottoms of toboggans and skis, restricting movement.
6. To gain maximum protection and concealment, the trail should be broken very close to the shore or bank.
water routes, frozen lakes, ice thickness testing, snow piling
Select a Movement Route Using a Map
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 4
Conditions: Given an operation or fragmentary order, a 1:50, 000 scale military map, and a compass.

Standards: Select a route with the following characteristics:

1. Take advantage of maximum cover and concealment.
2. Ensure observation and...
Conditions: Given an operation or fragmentary order, a 1:50, 000 scale military map, and a compass.

Standards: Select a route with the following characteristics:

1. Take advantage of maximum cover and concealment.
2. Ensure observation and fields of fire for overwatch or fire support elements.
3. Allow positive control of all elements.
4. Accomplish the mission quickly, without unnecessary or prolonged exposure to enemy fire.

Performance Steps:

1. Select the route that makes the best use of terrain. Use terrain to your best advantage. Take advantage of the terrain to—
- a. Cover and conceal the unit during movement.
- b. Provide maximum effectiveness of the platoon's weapons.

2. Use the military aspects of terrain and apply them to any given situation, whether it is a defense, a delay, or a road march behind the forward edge of the battle area (FEBA). Consider the following essential elements:
- a. Use cover and concealment for any type of movement on the battlefield.
- (1) Use cover to shield the unit from the effects of weapon fire, especially direct fire. Take advantage of every ravine or depression in the ground to protect and cover the force, especially if forward of the FEBA. Evaluate the terrain, the capabilities of the enemy's weapons systems, and the position of known or suspected enemy emplacements. Visualize a cross section of the terrain and determine where the enemy cannot place effective direct fire on your proposed route.
- (2) Conceal or disguise the unit. Consider concealment from both air and ground observation. Consider that exhaust smoke or dust can reveal your unit to the enemy.
- b. Ensure that your proposed route can be covered by fire from overwatch or fire support positions when moving in an area where contact with the enemy is expected.
- (1) Consider that direct fire weapons must have good observation to fire at known or suspected enemy positions along the movement route. Control the maneuver of your elements, if they make contact. Consider the effects of smoke and dust from friendly and enemy fire.
- (2) Select a route that gives your unit the best field of fire. They must be in a position to provide suppressive fires immediately. Use crew-served weapons to overwatch the movement. Ensure the overwatch element is able to observe the moving element and provide fire support all the way to the objective. Select overwatch positions that have unobstructed fields of fire to the next overwatch position.

3. Ensure that the route can be covered by fire from overwatch or fire support positions. Select the route that provides the most favorable tactical advantage and meets the mission requirements. If enemy air is active or enemy ground forces are in the area of the route, take maximum advantage of cover and concealment. If speed of movement is critical, select the route with the most easily negotiable terrain, avoiding difficult obstacles. Choose the route that provides movement from one easily distinguishable terrain feature to another. Check the terrain based on the above considerations, and then select the quickest and safest route.
map navigation, route selection, military terrain analysis, cover and concealment, fire support positions

Observation Techniques (9) -

Detect Objects by Contrast with Surroundings
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure:

1. Understand that objects are seen because they contrast with their surroundings.
2. There are 11 reasons why things are seen, including:
- Shadow: An object may cast a shadow that is visible even if the object itself is...
Procedure:

1. Understand that objects are seen because they contrast with their surroundings.
2. There are 11 reasons why things are seen, including:
- Shadow: An object may cast a shadow that is visible even if the object itself is not.
- Position: The spatial relationship between an object and its surroundings can indicate its identity.
- Texture: Differences in surface smoothness or roughness can make objects stand out.
- Noise: Sudden noises contrast with normal battlefield quiet and can pinpoint locations.
- Shine: Flashes of light from uncamouflaged materials like glass or metal attract attention.
3. Use this knowledge to improve observation skills in low-visibility conditions.
contrast,object detection,navigation,fieldcraft,observation
Detect Objects by Shine and Reflection
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure:

1. Understand that flashes of light reflected off uncamouflaged materials (e.g., glass, metal) can attract attention.
2. These reflections are visible to both ground observers and aerial surveillance.
3. Use this knowledge to avoid...
Procedure:

1. Understand that flashes of light reflected off uncamouflaged materials (e.g., glass, metal) can attract attention.
2. These reflections are visible to both ground observers and aerial surveillance.
3. Use this knowledge to avoid detection by minimizing reflective surfaces in your equipment or clothing.
4. Be aware that enemy forces may use this principle to detect you.
shine,reflection,detection,navigation,fieldcraft
Use Noise for Detection and Location
High Priority Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure:

1. Recognize that sudden noises contrast with the normal quiet of the battlefield.
2. Loud noises such as artillery fire or generator running can pinpoint locations.
3. During historical conflicts (e.g., Korean Conflict), sound...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that sudden noises contrast with the normal quiet of the battlefield.
2. Loud noises such as artillery fire or generator running can pinpoint locations.
3. During historical conflicts (e.g., Korean Conflict), sound ranging equipment was used to locate 80% of enemy indirect fire weapons based on noise.
4. Use this principle to detect enemy activity through auditory cues.
noise,detection,location,navigation,fieldcraft
Identify Objects by Position in Relation to Surroundings
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure:

1. Observe the spatial relationship between objects.
2. Recognize that an object's position can indicate its identity.
3. For example:
- A long object on a railroad track is likely a train.
- Similar objects on a river and...
Procedure:

1. Observe the spatial relationship between objects.
2. Recognize that an object's position can indicate its identity.
3. For example:
- A long object on a railroad track is likely a train.
- Similar objects on a river and parallel to its banks are likely boats or barges.
- A large structure among frame buildings may be a barn.
4. Use this understanding to identify potential targets or obstacles in the field.
position,object identification,navigation,fieldcraft,spatial awareness
Observing Off the Line of Vision
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure for observing incidents off the line of vision:

1. Be aware of peripheral awareness: Incidents rarely occur at the exact spot at which the observer is looking.
2. Develop peripheral awareness: The highly trained observer is...
Procedure for observing incidents off the line of vision:

1. Be aware of peripheral awareness: Incidents rarely occur at the exact spot at which the observer is looking.
2. Develop peripheral awareness: The highly trained observer is said to have 'eyes in the back of his head.' This ability to note incidents off the line of vision can see with ease and comfort.
peripheral awareness,navigation,off line of vision
Observing Through Obstacles
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure for observing through nearby screens or foliage:

1. Focus on the area under observation: The observer looks at the area under observation and ignores the screen.
2. Use small head movements: A small head movement automatically...
Procedure for observing through nearby screens or foliage:

1. Focus on the area under observation: The observer looks at the area under observation and ignores the screen.
2. Use small head movements: A small head movement automatically extends the area to be observed.
observing through obstacles,navigation,screen,foliage
Scanning Techniques in Minimal Light Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure for scanning in minimal light conditions (dusk, half moonlight, starlight):

1. Pause and scan: The observer pauses for a few seconds looking in one direction, paying attention to objects off the direct line of vision.
2. **Shift...
Procedure for scanning in minimal light conditions (dusk, half moonlight, starlight):

1. Pause and scan: The observer pauses for a few seconds looking in one direction, paying attention to objects off the direct line of vision.
2. Shift vision: Shift his line of vision by about 110 to 170 mils (approximately a fist's width at arm's length) and again pause until objects become visible near his line of vision.
3. Rest eyes periodically: Rest his eyes for 10 seconds every minute or two.
4. Use binoculars similarly: When using binoculars, use the same 'move and stop' method, with attention paid to objects visible 'out of the corner of the eye'.
minimal light conditions,navigation,scanning techniques,binoculars
Understand How Shadows Affect Visibility
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure:

1. Recognize that objects cast shadows which may be visible even when the object itself is not.
2. Against a dark background, light surfaces of an object will stand out.
3. Against a light background, dark or shadowed sides of an...
Procedure:

1. Recognize that objects cast shadows which may be visible even when the object itself is not.
2. Against a dark background, light surfaces of an object will stand out.
3. Against a light background, dark or shadowed sides of an object will show.
4. Be aware that the eye tends to overlook minor differences in darkness or lightness within uniformly shaded areas.
5. Use this knowledge to detect objects hidden by shadows.
shadows,visibility,object detection,navigation,fieldcraft
Visual Inference of Objects
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure for inferring the identity of an object from partial visibility:

1. Recognize relative positions: Even when seemingly insignificant portions of an object are visible, the identity of the complete object can be inferred.
2....
Procedure for inferring the identity of an object from partial visibility:

1. Recognize relative positions: Even when seemingly insignificant portions of an object are visible, the identity of the complete object can be inferred.
2. Identify presence based on parts: It is possible to determine that a person or a piece of equipment is present from the fact that small parts are in their correct relative positions.
visual inference,object identification,navigation

Weather Forecasting (9) -

Analog Method for Weather Forecasting
Standard Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
The Analog Method examines a day’s forecast and recalls a day in the past when the weather looked similar (an analogy). This method is difficult to use because finding a perfect analogy is difficult.
analog method, weather forecasting, historical comparison
Identify Five Signs of Nature for Weather Forecasting
Standard Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Animal Behavior:
- Observe if animals are more active or restless than usual.
- Birds may fly lower before a storm, and some species may leave the area entirely.
- Insects like bees and wasps often return to their...
Procedure:

1. Animal Behavior:
- Observe if animals are more active or restless than usual.
- Birds may fly lower before a storm, and some species may leave the area entirely.
- Insects like bees and wasps often return to their hives before bad weather.

2. Plant Indicators:
- Leaves of plants may curl inward or droop before rain.
- Flowers close up in response to changes in atmospheric pressure.
- Trees may sway more than usual, indicating wind changes.

3. Water Sources:
- Fish may rise to the surface and jump out of water before a storm.
- Water may become murky or have an unusual odor before rain.

4. Atmospheric Pressure Changes:
- Use your body as a barometer: if your ears pop, it indicates pressure changes.
- A sudden drop in temperature can signal the approach of a cold front.

5. Sky and Cloud Observations:
- Look for darkening skies or unusual cloud formations (e.g., cumulonimbus).
- The color of the sky may change to deep reds or purples before a storm.
natural weather signs, survival indicators, animal behavior, plant signals
Numerical Weather Prediction Method
Standard Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
The Numerical Weather Prediction method uses computers to analyze all weather conditions and is the most accurate of the five methods.
numerical weather prediction, computer modeling, forecasting accuracy
Persistence Method for Weather Forecasting
Standard Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
The Persistence Method assumes that the conditions at the time of the forecast will not change. For example, if today was hot and dry, the persistence method predicts that tomorrow will be the same.
persistence method, weather forecasting, forecast accuracy
State Cloud Progression for Cold and Warm Fronts
Standard Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Cold Front Progression (from high to low):
- Cirrus clouds appear first as the cold front approaches.
- Cirrostratus forms next, often creating a halo effect around the sun or moon.
- Altocumulus develops...
Procedure:

1. Cold Front Progression (from high to low):
- Cirrus clouds appear first as the cold front approaches.
- Cirrostratus forms next, often creating a halo effect around the sun or moon.
- Altocumulus develops in a layered pattern, sometimes forming a “mackerel sky.”
- Stratocumulus appears as thick, rolling clouds that may bring light precipitation.
- Nimbostratus forms next, bringing continuous rain or snow.
- Cumulonimbus develops if the front is strong enough to produce thunderstorms and severe weather.

2. Warm Front Progression (from high to low):
- Cirrus clouds appear first as the warm front approaches.
- Cirrostratus forms next, often creating a halo effect around the sun or moon.
- Altocumulus develops in a layered pattern, sometimes forming a “mackerel sky.”
- Stratocumulus appears as thick, rolling clouds that may bring light precipitation.
- Nimbostratus forms next, bringing continuous rain or snow.

3. Note the differences:
- Cold fronts typically produce more abrupt weather changes and severe conditions.
- Warm fronts tend to result in prolonged periods of steady precipitation.
cold front weather, warm front weather, cloud progression, survival forecasting
Trends Method for Weather Forecasting
Standard Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
The Trends Method involves determining the speed and direction of fronts, high- and low-pressure centers, and clouds and precipitation. For example, if a cold front moves 300 miles during a 24-hour period, we can predict that it will travel 300...
The Trends Method involves determining the speed and direction of fronts, high- and low-pressure centers, and clouds and precipitation. For example, if a cold front moves 300 miles during a 24-hour period, we can predict that it will travel 300 miles in another 24-hours.
trends method, weather forecasting, front movement, pressure centers
Understand the Structure of Earth's Atmosphere for Weather Forecasting
Standard Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Procedure:

1. Identify the Layers of the Atmosphere:
- The atmosphere is divided into several layers, with the troposphere being the lowest and most important layer for weather.
- The troposphere extends up to about 40,000 feet...
Procedure:

1. Identify the Layers of the Atmosphere:
- The atmosphere is divided into several layers, with the troposphere being the lowest and most important layer for weather.
- The troposphere extends up to about 40,000 feet (approximately 12,000 meters) above sea level.

2. Understand the Role of the Troposphere:
- Most weather phenomena occur within this layer due to its high concentration of water vapor and temperature gradients.
- The troposphere contains about 75% of the atmosphere’s mass and nearly all of its water vapor.

3. Recognize the Impact of Solar Radiation on Weather:
- Only about half of the sun's energy reaches the Earth's surface due to absorption or reflection by clouds, dust, and other atmospheric particles.
- This energy warms the Earth’s surface and lower atmosphere, influencing weather patterns.

4. Note the Role of Wind in Distributing Heat and Moisture:
- Winds redistribute heat from the equator toward the poles, balancing temperatures globally.
- Without this process, many regions would be too hot or cold to support life.
atmosphere layers, troposphere, weather formation, solar energy absorption
Understanding Weather Variations at Different Elevations
Standard Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Weather at various elevations may be quite different because cloud height, temperature, and barometric pressure will all be different. There may be overcast and rain in a lower area, with mountains rising above the low overcast into warmer clear...
Weather at various elevations may be quite different because cloud height, temperature, and barometric pressure will all be different. There may be overcast and rain in a lower area, with mountains rising above the low overcast into warmer clear weather.
elevation, weather variation, cloud height, temperature, barometric pressure
Use of Weather Instruments for Local Forecasting
Standard Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
To make local weather forecasts, use a portable aneroid barometer, thermometer, wind meter, and hygrometer. Reports from other localities and from any weather service, including USAF, USN, or the National Weather Bureau, are also helpful. Weather...
To make local weather forecasts, use a portable aneroid barometer, thermometer, wind meter, and hygrometer. Reports from other localities and from any weather service, including USAF, USN, or the National Weather Bureau, are also helpful. Weather reports should be used in conjunction with the locally observed current weather situation to forecast future weather patterns.
barometer, thermometer, wind meter, hygrometer, weather forecasting, USAF, USN, National Weather Bureau

Dead Reckoning (8) -

Bypass Obstacles During Dead Reckoning Navigation
Critical Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

To bypass obstacles during dead reckoning navigation:

1. At the edge of the obstacle, note the number of paces taken to this point.
2. To detour to the right, add 90 degrees to your original azimuth.
3. Using the new...
Procedure:

To bypass obstacles during dead reckoning navigation:

1. At the edge of the obstacle, note the number of paces taken to this point.
2. To detour to the right, add 90 degrees to your original azimuth.
3. Using the new azimuth, pick a steering mark and move toward it while beginning a new pace count.
4. Move on this azimuth until you reach the end of the obstacle.
5. Stop and note the number of paces taken, then add or subtract 90 degrees from the azimuth just read, and move to the far side of the obstacle.
6. Upon reaching the far side, stop the count and add this number to the pace count noted in step (1).
7. Again add or subtract 90 degrees from the azimuth used, then move the same number of paces you took on the first leg of your offset or detour.
8. Place the compass on your original azimuth, pick up the pace count you ended with when you cleared the obstacle, and proceed to your finish point.
obstacle bypassing, dead reckoning, navigation, detour, azimuth
Navigate Using Dead Reckoning Technique
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

To navigate using the dead reckoning technique:

1. Locate the start and finish points on the map (see figure 071-329-1006-5).
2. Determine the grid azimuth from the start point to the finish point or to the first...
Procedure:

To navigate using the dead reckoning technique:

1. Locate the start and finish points on the map (see figure 071-329-1006-5).
2. Determine the grid azimuth from the start point to the finish point or to the first intermediate point on the map.
3. Convert the grid azimuth to a magnetic azimuth for use with your compass.
4. Determine the distance between the start and finish points, or between any intermediate points, on the map.
5. If you do not know how many paces you take for each 100 meters:
- Move to a 100-meter course and determine your pace count.
6. Convert the map distance to pace count based on your known pace per 100 meters.
7. Conduct a thorough map reconnaissance of the area between the start point and the finish point to identify potential obstacles or terrain features.
dead reckoning, navigation, map reading, azimuth, pace count
Use Steering Points During Dead Reckoning Navigation
High Priority Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

To use steering points during dead reckoning navigation:

1. Before moving from the start point, shoot an azimuth on a well-defined object on the ground in the direction of travel. These objects are known as steering points...
Procedure:

To use steering points during dead reckoning navigation:

1. Before moving from the start point, shoot an azimuth on a well-defined object on the ground in the direction of travel. These objects are known as steering points and may include lone trees, buildings, rocks, or other easily identifiable features.
2. At night, use stars as steering points, but be aware that their positions change due to Earth's rotation. Check your azimuth frequently, but only when halted.
3. Once a steering point is selected:
- Move toward it while beginning your pace count.
- For every 100 meters traveled, track the number of 100-meter segments completed.
4. Upon reaching the first steering point, shoot an azimuth to another steering mark and repeat the process until you reach the finish point.
steering points, dead reckoning, navigation, azimuth, pace count
Gather Navigation Equipment for Dead Reckoning in Winter Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Ensure the following equipment is available:
- Maps (and air photos if available) to select and plot your route.
- Compass for direction finding.
- Protractor to plot directions and distances on maps or air...
Procedure:

1. Ensure the following equipment is available:
- Maps (and air photos if available) to select and plot your route.
- Compass for direction finding.
- Protractor to plot directions and distances on maps or air photos, remembering to convert magnetic bearings to grid bearings.
- Route card to outline the proposed march route.
- Logbook (any notebook) to record distance and direction covered during the journey.
- Pace counter to keep track of paces taken.
- Cable or measuring tape to measure distances if a pace counter is not used.
navigation equipment, dead reckoning, protractor, pace counter, route card
Plot and Adjust a Route Using Dead Reckoning in Winter Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Select the route based on maps or air photos.
2. Plot the selected route on a map or air photo.
3. Create a route card that outlines each leg of your journey, including distance and direction.
4. Begin the march and maintain a...
Procedure:

1. Select the route based on maps or air photos.
2. Plot the selected route on a map or air photo.
3. Create a route card that outlines each leg of your journey, including distance and direction.
4. Begin the march and maintain a log by recording:
- Bearings taken at regular intervals.
- Distance covered on each bearing.
- Any changes to the route due to terrain or tactical conditions.
5. If obstacles are encountered that prevent following the planned route, adjust your course using maps or air photos as guides and record these adjustments in the log.
route plotting, dead reckoning adjustments, terrain obstacles, map navigation
Prepare and Maintain a Navigation Log for Dead Reckoning
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Before departure, prepare a route card that outlines the distance and direction of each leg of your proposed route.
2. During the march, maintain a log by recording:
- Each bearing taken.
- Distance covered on each...
Procedure:

1. Before departure, prepare a route card that outlines the distance and direction of each leg of your proposed route.
2. During the march, maintain a log by recording:
- Each bearing taken.
- Distance covered on each bearing.
- Any changes to the original route due to terrain or tactical situations.
3. Use any notebook as a log if necessary.
4. Keep the log up to date at all times — memory is not sufficient for accurate navigation.
5. If obstacles or changes in the tactical situation prevent following your planned route, adjust the route using maps or air photos and record these adjustments in the log.
route card, navigation log, dead reckoning, terrain changes, tactical adjustments
Use Dead Reckoning for Navigation
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
To use dead reckoning for navigation:

1. Determine your starting point:
- Use a map and compass to determine your initial position and heading.
- Mark this location on the map as your starting reference point.

2. **Set a course and...
To use dead reckoning for navigation:

1. Determine your starting point:
- Use a map and compass to determine your initial position and heading.
- Mark this location on the map as your starting reference point.

2. Set a course and distance:
- Choose a direction of travel based on the map or compass reading.
- Estimate the distance you need to cover using known distances, pacing, or a measuring tool such as a rangefinder or GPS device.

3. Maintain your heading:
- Keep your course steady by frequently checking your compass and adjusting for any deviations caused by wind, terrain, or movement errors.
- Use natural landmarks to confirm that you are on the correct path.

4. Track your progress:
- Mark your position at regular intervals using a map, GPS, or by placing markers in the field.
- Record the time and distance traveled to help estimate your location if you become disoriented.

5. Adjust for errors:
- If you notice that you have deviated from your planned course, use your compass or map to reorient yourself and adjust your heading accordingly.
- Recalculate your position based on the time elapsed and distance traveled since your last checkpoint.
course setting, heading maintenance, progress tracking, error correction
Perform Dead Reckoning Navigation Without Steering Marks
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Dismount from the vehicle and move at least 18 meters to the front of it, ensuring the vehicle is oriented in the direction of travel.
2. Face the vehicle and read the azimuth to it. Add or subtract 180 degrees to determine the...
Procedure:

1. Dismount from the vehicle and move at least 18 meters to the front of it, ensuring the vehicle is oriented in the direction of travel.
2. Face the vehicle and read the azimuth to it. Add or subtract 180 degrees to determine the forward azimuth (direction of travel).
3. On the navigator's order, have the driver drive straight toward the navigator.
4. Remount the vehicle and hold the compass as it would be held during movement. Read the azimuth in the direction of travel.
5. Note any deviation between the original azimuth and the current compass reading (e.g., if the original azimuth was 75 degrees, the compass may show 67 degrees after remounting). Adjust future navigation based on this deviation.
6. At night, use a map to determine the grid azimuth, convert it to a magnetic azimuth, align the vehicle accordingly, and move forward while noting any deviations.
7. If the vehicle has a turret, ensure it is not traversed during movement, as this can change the deviation.
8. To calculate distance: Determine the map distance to travel and add 20% to convert to ground distance. Use the vehicle's odometer to verify the traveled distance.

Notes:
• This method is used only on flat, featureless terrain.
• The technique can be applied at night with proper preparation.
dead reckoning, navigation, compass, azimuth, vehicle travel, no steering marks

Distance Estimation (8) -

Factors Affecting Distance Perception
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure for recognizing factors that affect distance perception:

1. Objects appear more distant in the following situations:
- When there is dead ground between the target and the observer;
- When they are larger than other things...
Procedure for recognizing factors that affect distance perception:

1. Objects appear more distant in the following situations:
- When there is dead ground between the target and the observer;
- When they are larger than other things around them.
2. Objects can seem more distant than they really are when:
- The observer is looking over a depression, all of which is visible;
- There is poor light or fog or the sun is in the observer's eyes;
distance perception,navigation,factors affecting vision
Judging Distance to a Target Using Appearance Method
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure for judging distance using the appearance method:

1. Compare object appearance at different distances: By comparing the appearance of a man in several positions—at 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 metres—observers can establish a series...
Procedure for judging distance using the appearance method:

1. Compare object appearance at different distances: By comparing the appearance of a man in several positions—at 100, 200, 300, 400 and 500 metres—observers can establish a series of mental pictures.
2. Recognize visual changes with distance: As distances increase, a man's figure becomes smaller, his outline becomes increasingly blurred and his other features gradually fade out.
3. Use the following as a rough guide to determine distance:
- 200 metres — all parts of the body are distinct;
- 300 metres — outline of the face becomes blurred;
appearance method,navigation,distance estimation,target identification
Judging Distance to a Weapon Based on Time Delay
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure for judging distance to a weapon based on the time delay between crack and thump:

1. Identify time intervals: The time between the crack and thump at the following ranges is:
- 300 metres — 2/3 of a second;
- 600 metres — 1...
Procedure for judging distance to a weapon based on the time delay between crack and thump:

1. Identify time intervals: The time between the crack and thump at the following ranges is:
- 300 metres — 2/3 of a second;
- 600 metres — 1 1/3 seconds;
- 900 metres — 2 seconds.
2. Apply for automatic weapons: Judging the distance to an automatic weapon is slightly more difficult. The last crack and the last thump must be picked out in order to establish the correct automatic weapon range.
distance estimation,navigation,weapon detection,time delay
Estimate Distance Based on Travel Speed
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
To estimate distance based on travel speed:

• A good canoe moves at a rate of 4 to 5 miles per hour on dead water.
• A man walking covers approximately 3.5 miles per hour on good roads.
• A packtrain travels at about **2.5 miles...
To estimate distance based on travel speed:

• A good canoe moves at a rate of 4 to 5 miles per hour on dead water.
• A man walking covers approximately 3.5 miles per hour on good roads.
• A packtrain travels at about 2.5 miles per hour, or as slow as 1.5 miles per hour on mountain trails.

This method is useful for estimating how far you can travel in a given time, which helps with planning and navigation.
travel speed,distance estimation,navigation,canoe,walking
Estimate Distance Using Echo Time
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
To estimate distance using an echo:

1. Shout towards a distant object such as a bank, cliff, or building.
2. Measure the time between your shout and the echo returning to you.
3. Divide the time by 2 (this accounts for the sound...
To estimate distance using an echo:

1. Shout towards a distant object such as a bank, cliff, or building.
2. Measure the time between your shout and the echo returning to you.
3. Divide the time by 2 (this accounts for the sound traveling to the object and back).
4. Multiply the result by 1100 to get the distance in feet.

This method is useful when visual estimation is not possible, but it requires a clear path for the echo to return.
echo,distance,measurement,navigation,estimation
Identify Factors Affecting Range Estimation Accuracy
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Assess the clearness of the object’s outline and details:
- Underestimation occurs when most of the object is visible and offers a clear outline.
- Overestimation occurs when only a small part of the object is visible or...
Procedure:

1. Assess the clearness of the object’s outline and details:
- Underestimation occurs when most of the object is visible and offers a clear outline.
- Overestimation occurs when only a small part of the object is visible or it is small in relation to its surroundings.

2. Evaluate terrain and observer position:
- Underestimation may occur when looking across a depression that is mostly hidden from view, or when looking downward from high ground.
- Overestimation may occur when looking across a depression that is totally visible, or when vision is confined (e.g., in streets, forest trails).

3. Consider light and atmospheric conditions:
- Underestimation occurs when the object is in sharp contrast with the background or silhouetted due to size, shape, or color.
- Overestimation occurs when the object blends into the background or terrain.

4. Account for lighting conditions:
- Underestimation may occur in bright light or when the sun is shining from behind the observer.
- Overestimation may occur in poor light (e.g., dawn, dusk), rain, snow, fog, or when the sun is in the observer’s eyes.

Notes:
• These factors should be considered to improve accuracy in range estimation.
• Environmental conditions significantly influence perception of distance.
range estimation factors, map reading, land navigation
Judging Distance Using Bracketing Method
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Bracketing Method: If the target is between two reference points of known distance:

1. Identify the two reference points with known distances (X and Y).
2. Add the distances together (X + Y).
3. Divide the total by 2 to get an approximate...
Bracketing Method: If the target is between two reference points of known distance:

1. Identify the two reference points with known distances (X and Y).
2. Add the distances together (X + Y).
3. Divide the total by 2 to get an approximate distance to the target.
bracketing method,distance estimation,navigation,fieldcraft,military
Estimate Distance Traveled Using Odometer in a Vehicle
Reference Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Check the vehicle’s odometer to determine how far it has traveled.
2. Note the starting reading on the odometer before beginning your journey.
3. Note the ending reading after reaching your destination or at regular...
Procedure:

1. Check the vehicle’s odometer to determine how far it has traveled.
2. Note the starting reading on the odometer before beginning your journey.
3. Note the ending reading after reaching your destination or at regular intervals during travel.
4. Calculate the distance traveled by subtracting the starting reading from the ending reading.

Conversion Notes:
• 0.1 mile is approximately 160 meters.
• 1 mile is approximately 1,600 meters (1.6 kilometers).

Notes:
• The odometer provides a reliable estimate of distance traveled in vehicles.
• This method is useful for dead reckoning and terrain association while navigating mounted.
odometer,distance estimation,navigation,military vehicle,travel tracking

Map Reading And Orientation (7) -

Orient Yourself and Remember Your Location
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Objective: Maintain awareness of your location to ensure safe navigation.

Steps:
1. If you have a map, spot your location and relate it to the surrounding terrain.
2. Constantly orient yourself. Pay close attention to where you are and...
Objective: Maintain awareness of your location to ensure safe navigation.

Steps:
1. If you have a map, spot your location and relate it to the surrounding terrain.
2. Constantly orient yourself. Pay close attention to where you are and where you are going.
3. Determine how your location relates to:
- The location of enemy units and controlled areas.
- The location of friendly units and controlled areas.
- The location of local water sources.
- Areas that will provide good cover and concealment.
orient yourself, map reading, enemy location, friendly units, water sources, cover and concealment
Determine Six-Digit Grid Coordinates of Unknown Point Using Intersection Method
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Set up the scenario: In a field location, provide the Soldier with a standard 1:50,000 scale military map of the area, a compass, straight edge, coordinate scale and protractor, pencil, unknown point, and two points (at least...
Procedure:

1. Set up the scenario: In a field location, provide the Soldier with a standard 1:50,000 scale military map of the area, a compass, straight edge, coordinate scale and protractor, pencil, unknown point, and two points (at least 400 meters apart) from which to determine azimuths to the unknown point.
2. Brief the Soldier: Instruct them to determine the six-digit grid coordinates of the unknown point, including the 100,000-meter square identifier.
3. Use intersection method:
- Convert magnetic azimuths to grid azimuths using G-M angle adjustments.
- Plot lines on the map from two known positions based on calculated grid azimuths.
- Identify the intersection of these lines as the unknown point's location.
4. Verify accuracy: Ensure coordinates are determined within 100 meters of the actual grid coordinates.
six-digit grid coordinates, intersection method, map accuracy, military map reading
Locate Unknown Point Using Resection Method with Map and Compass
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Ensure you have the following equipment: A standard 1:50,000 scale military map of the area, compass, straight edge, coordinate scale and protractor (GTA 5-212), pencil, and two terrain features visible from your location that...
Procedure:

1. Ensure you have the following equipment: A standard 1:50,000 scale military map of the area, compass, straight edge, coordinate scale and protractor (GTA 5-212), pencil, and two terrain features visible from your location that are identifiable on the map.
2. Identify two or more known terrain features visible from your current position and mark them on the map.
3. Determine magnetic azimuths to each of these features using a compass.
4. Convert magnetic azimuths to grid azimuths by adjusting for the G-M angle (add easterly angles, subtract westerly angles).
5. Plot lines from your position on the map along the calculated grid azimuths to intersect with the marked terrain features.
6. Determine your location: The intersection of these lines represents your current location on the map.
7. Record six-digit grid coordinates, including the 100,000-meter square identifier, ensuring accuracy within 100 meters.
resection method, map and compass, terrain features, grid coordinates
Perform Modified Resection Without a Compass
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
To perform modified resection without a compass, follow these steps:

1. Ensure you are on a linear feature: This can be a road, stream bank, railroad, or ridge.
2. Orient your map as closely as possible. If a compass is not available, use...
To perform modified resection without a compass, follow these steps:

1. Ensure you are on a linear feature: This can be a road, stream bank, railroad, or ridge.
2. Orient your map as closely as possible. If a compass is not available, use natural landmarks or other methods to orient the map.
3. Identify a known feature on the map and in reality: For example, locate a water tower on both the map and in the real world.
4. Use a straightedge (such as a ruler, rifle cleaning rod, or section of a radio antenna) to align with the known feature on the map so that it points directly at the real-world feature.
5. Draw a line along the straightedge on the map from the known feature to where it crosses the linear feature you are currently located on.
6. The intersection point of this line and the linear feature is your current location.

_Note: Always orient your map as closely as possible. If you do not have a ruler, use your rifle cleaning rod or a section of the radio antenna as a straightedge.
modified resection,map orientation,straightedge,navigation,linear feature
Perform a Two-Point Resection for Navigation
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Identify two terrain features or objects that can be seen from your current position and also appear on your map.

2. Determine the magnetic azimuth to one of these distant terrain features using your compass.

3....
Procedure:

1. Identify two terrain features or objects that can be seen from your current position and also appear on your map.

2. Determine the magnetic azimuth to one of these distant terrain features using your compass.

3. Convert the magnetic azimuth into a grid azimuth using the declination diagram on your map.

4. Calculate the back azimuth by adding 180 degrees to the grid azimuth (or subtracting 180 degrees if it exceeds 360 degrees).

5. Draw a line on your map from the identified distant point back toward your current position, placing a small mark at this point.

6. Use a straight edge and protractor to draw a line from where you plotted the distant object to the mark made by the edge of the protractor. Extend this line through the area where you are located.

7. Repeat steps 2-6 for the second terrain feature, ensuring that it is at least 60 degrees away from the first one in terms of direction.

8. Locate your position by identifying the point where the two lines intersect on the map. This intersection marks your current location.

Notes:
• Ensure accuracy when measuring angles and drawing lines to avoid errors in determining your position.
• If you are unsure about any step, double-check using a known reference point or consult additional resources.
resection,navigation,map reading,compass use,terrain features,grid azimuth,magnetic azimuth
Use Straight Edge Method to Locate Unknown Point Without Compass
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Orient the map using map-terrain association (refer to task 071-329-1012 in STP 21-1-SMCT).
2. Locate and mark your position on the map.
3. Place a straight edge on the map with one end at your current position (A) as...
Procedure:

1. Orient the map using map-terrain association (refer to task 071-329-1012 in STP 21-1-SMCT).
2. Locate and mark your position on the map.
3. Place a straight edge on the map with one end at your current position (A) as a pivot point.
4. Rotate the straight edge until the unknown point is sighted along its edge.
5. Draw a line along the straight edge from your position to the unknown point.
6. Repeat steps 3-5 at a second known position (B), and for accuracy, repeat at a third position if possible.
7. Identify the intersection of the lines drawn on the map; this is the location of the unknown point (C).
straight edge method, intersection without compass, map orientation, terrain association
Determine Terrain Features and Locate Position on a Map
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Determine Terrain Features and Locate Position on a Map

#### Steps:
1. Determine the type of terrain feature you are located on.
2. Determine what types of terrain features surround your location.
3. Orient the map.
4. **Determine...
### Determine Terrain Features and Locate Position on a Map

#### Steps:
1. Determine the type of terrain feature you are located on.
2. Determine what types of terrain features surround your location.
3. Orient the map.
4. Determine the four cardinal directions (North, South, East, and West).
5. Determine your location.
- a. Relate the terrain features on the ground to those shown on the map.
- b. After you have determined where the terrain features on the ground and those on the map coincide, determine the coordinates of your location using the coordinate scale and protractor.

#### Evaluation Preparation:
• _Setup_: Select a field site whose terrain features are shown on the map. At the test site, provide a field table, a 1:50,000-scale military map of the area, pencil, paper, and a coordinate scale.
• _Brief Soldier_: Tell the Soldier that he/she must determine a six-digit coordinate of his/her location within 7 minutes. Tell the Soldier one of the cardinal directions (North, South, East, or West).

#### Performance Measures:
|Performance Measure|GO|NO GO|
|---|---|---|
|Identified the type of terrain feature where you are located.|——|——|
|Identified the terrain features around your location.|——|——|
|Oriented the map.|——|——|
|Determined the remaining three cardinal directions.|——|——|
|Identified the same terrain features on the map.|——|——|
|Determined the six-digit grid coordinate of your own location (the point selected must be within 100 meters of your location).|——|——|

#### Evaluation Guidance:
Refer to chapter 1, paragraph 1-9e, (1) and (2).

#### References:
• Required: FM 3-25.26
• Related:
- STP 21-1-SMCT
- 3-234
- 071-329-1005
terrain features,map orientation,cardinal directions,coordinate scale,protractor,location determination

River Navigation (7) -

Assess Factors Influencing Hydraulic Size at Low Head Dams
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Identify the factors that influence the size of the hydraulic created by a low head dam:
- (1) Height of drop: The greater the height, the larger the hydraulic.
- (2) Angle of drop: A steeper angle increases the...
Procedure:

1. Identify the factors that influence the size of the hydraulic created by a low head dam:
- (1) Height of drop: The greater the height, the larger the hydraulic.
- (2) Angle of drop: A steeper angle increases the force and size of the hydraulic.
- (3) Volume of water in CFS: Higher volume leads to more powerful hydraulics.
- (4) Velocity of water in MPH: Faster-moving water creates larger hydraulics.
- (5) Bottom composition of runout: Hard, smooth surfaces increase the size and strength of the hydraulic.

Notes:
• These factors are critical for understanding the danger level associated with low head dams.
• Assessing them helps in planning safe river navigation and rescue operations.
hydraulic size,factors,low head dam
Identify Compounding Factors for Rescuer Difficulty at Low Head Dams
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Recognize the compounding factors that increase the difficulty of rescue operations at low head dams:
- (1) Water depth below dam: Deeper water increases the risk and complexity of rescue.
- (2) Debris build-up:...
Procedure:

1. Recognize the compounding factors that increase the difficulty of rescue operations at low head dams:
- (1) Water depth below dam: Deeper water increases the risk and complexity of rescue.
- (2) Debris build-up: Accumulated debris can hinder movement and create additional hazards.
- (3) Dam construction/conformation: The structure or shape of the dam affects how dangerous it is to approach.
- (4) Lack of access: Limited access points make rescue operations more challenging.
- (5) Lack of natural anchors: No stable ground or objects for securing equipment or oneself.
- (6) Distance between riverbanks: Greater distance increases the difficulty and time required for a rescue.

Notes:
• These factors must be considered when planning rescue operations near low head dams.
• Understanding these challenges helps in preparing for safe and effective interventions.
rescuer difficulty,factors,low head dam
Understand the Hydrology of Low Head Dams
High Priority Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Understand that the basic hydrology of low head dams is similar to any other pour-over obstacle.
2. Recognize how water flows over a low head dam:
- As the river flows over the dam, the water follows the face of the dam to...
Procedure:

1. Understand that the basic hydrology of low head dams is similar to any other pour-over obstacle.
2. Recognize how water flows over a low head dam:
- As the river flows over the dam, the water follows the face of the dam to the river bottom.
3. Understand the formation of the boil line: When sufficient speed diminishes, the water rebounds to the surface and continues downstream, creating a vacuum or pocket directly below the dam that must be filled.
4. Recognize how this creates a vertical reversal or pour-over eddy as some of the water flows back upstream.
5. Understand that the boil line is the demarcation between upstream and downstream flow.
6. Be aware that the shape and angle of the dam face, along with the volume and velocity of the water, determine the distance and width of the boil line.

Notes:
• Understanding this hydrology helps in planning safe river navigation and rescue operations.
• The boil line is a critical feature to identify for safety reasons.
boil line,hydrology,low head dam
Calculate New River Velocity After Change in Width or Depth
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Determine the volume of water (in CFS) using the formula: Volume = Depth × Width × Velocity.
2. If the river changes shape and becomes wider or narrower, recalculate the velocity using the following formula:
- **New...
Procedure:

1. Determine the volume of water (in CFS) using the formula: Volume = Depth × Width × Velocity.
2. If the river changes shape and becomes wider or narrower, recalculate the velocity using the following formula:
- New Velocity = Volume in CFS / (New Width × New Depth)

Example:
• Suppose a river has a volume of 1,200 CFS and the width increases from 100 feet to 150 feet while the depth remains at 6 feet.
- New Velocity = 1,200 / (150 × 6) = 1.33 ft/sec

Notes:
• This formula is used when river conditions change and you need to adjust for new velocity values.
• Accurate measurements of width and depth are critical for this calculation.
new velocity,cfs formula,width change,depth change
Calculate River Volume Using CFS Formula
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Measure the depth of the river at a specific point.
2. Measure the width of the river at that same point.
3. Determine the velocity of the river (in feet per second) by timing how long it takes for a float to travel...
Procedure:

1. Measure the depth of the river at a specific point.
2. Measure the width of the river at that same point.
3. Determine the velocity of the river (in feet per second) by timing how long it takes for a float to travel 100 feet upstream to downstream.
4. Use the formula: Volume in CFS = Depth × Width × Velocity.

Example:
• A river that is 6 feet deep, 100 feet wide, and has a velocity of 2 feet per second would have a volume of:
- 6 ft × 100 ft × 2 ft/sec = 1,200 CFS.

Notes:
• This formula is used to estimate the amount of water flowing through a river at any given point.
• Accurate measurements are essential for understanding river behavior and planning rescue operations.
river volume,cfs formula,depth,width,velocity
Determine River Velocity Using Float Method
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Select a straight section of the river that is at least 100 feet long.
2. Drop a float (such as a buoyant object) into the water at the upstream end of the 100-foot section.
3. Start a timer when the float enters the water...
Procedure:

1. Select a straight section of the river that is at least 100 feet long.
2. Drop a float (such as a buoyant object) into the water at the upstream end of the 100-foot section.
3. Start a timer when the float enters the water and stop it when it reaches the downstream end.
4. Use the following chart to determine velocity in feet per second and miles per hour (MPH):

| TIME IN SECONDS | VELOCITY (FT/SEC) | VELOCITY (MPH) |
|-----------------|------------------|----------------|
| 5 | 20 | 13 |
| 10 | 10 | 7 |
| 15 | 6.7 | 5 |
| 17 | 5.9 | 4 |
| 20 | 5 | 3.5 |
| 25 | 4 | 3 |
| 29 | 3.5 | 2.5 |
| 37 | 2.7 | 2 |
| 50 | 2 | 1.5 |
| 80 | 1.3 | 1 |
| 110 | 0.7 | 0.5 |

Notes:
• This method is used to estimate the speed of river current, which is essential for planning rescue operations and understanding river dynamics.
• Ensure that the float moves freely without obstruction.
river velocity,float method,velocity chart
Estimate Force Exerted by River Current on Objects
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Determine the velocity of the river in miles per hour (MPH) using the float method or other means.
2. Use the following chart to estimate the force exerted on different objects:

| CURRENT VELOCITY (MPH) | FORCE EXERTED ON...
Procedure:

1. Determine the velocity of the river in miles per hour (MPH) using the float method or other means.
2. Use the following chart to estimate the force exerted on different objects:

| CURRENT VELOCITY (MPH) | FORCE EXERTED ON OBJECT (LB) |
|------------------------|----------------------------|
| Legs | Body | Wrapped Raft |
| 3 | 17 | 34 | 168 |
| 6 | 67 | 134 | 672 |
| 9 | 151 | 302 | 1,512 |
| 12 | 269 | 538 | 2,688 |

Notes:
• This chart is used to estimate the force of river current on different objects, such as a person or a raft.
• Understanding this force helps in planning rescue operations and assessing risks.
force exerted,current velocity,object force

Terrain Analysis (7) -

Analyze Mountain Terrain Characteristics for Navigation
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Understand the general characteristics of mountainous terrain:
- High, inaccessible peaks and steep slopes.
- May be snow-covered depending on altitude.
- Prominent ridges and large valleys are common.
2. **Assess...
Procedure:

1. Understand the general characteristics of mountainous terrain:
- High, inaccessible peaks and steep slopes.
- May be snow-covered depending on altitude.
- Prominent ridges and large valleys are common.
2. Assess navigational challenges:
- Terrain can be navigated effectively with careful map and terrain examination.
3. Consider the impact of elevation:
- Temperature decreases by 3 to 5 degrees per 300-meter gain in altitude.
- Increased rainfall and wind speeds are typical.
4. Be aware of weather conditions:
- Sudden severe storms, fog, and rapidly fluctuating visibility are common.
5. Evaluate vegetation:
- Below the tree line, heavy vegetation is present due to increased rainfall and lack of farming activity.
mountain terrain, navigation, weather conditions, elevation impact
Analyze Mountainous Terrain for Navigation and Security
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Purpose: To analyze mountainous terrain to improve navigation, security, and understanding of environmental conditions.

Procedure:
1. Examine the map carefully, paying special attention to contour intervals, ridges, valleys, and...
Purpose: To analyze mountainous terrain to improve navigation, security, and understanding of environmental conditions.

Procedure:
1. Examine the map carefully, paying special attention to contour intervals, ridges, valleys, and peaks.
2. Identify features such as high, inaccessible peaks, steep slopes, prominent ridges, and large valleys that are typical of mountainous terrain.
3. Consider the effects of elevation on climate: expect colder temperatures (approximately 3–5 degrees per 300-meter gain in altitude), increased rainfall, and higher wind speeds.
4. Be aware of sudden severe storms, fog, and rapidly fluctuating weather, which can significantly impact visibility and movement.
5. Use natural features such as rock outcroppings, boulders, and heavy vegetation for cover and concealment during movement.
6. Understand that only a few routing options are available, so prioritize all-round security and be prepared to use alternative routes if necessary.
7. Conduct detailed reconnaissance or gather information from local inhabitants before moving off-road to ensure the route is negotiable.
mountain terrain analysis, navigation security, weather effects
Conduct Winter Terrain Studies for Northern Operations
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. Prepare detailed studies of terrain and revise them as necessary to ensure accuracy and reflect changing trafficability conditions.
2. Locate and indicate the condition of existing road networks on maps.
3. Locate and plot local...
Procedure:

1. Prepare detailed studies of terrain and revise them as necessary to ensure accuracy and reflect changing trafficability conditions.
2. Locate and indicate the condition of existing road networks on maps.
3. Locate and plot local roads that are used during winter but not shown on standard maps.
4. Show forest density, tree size, water routes, ice conditions and thickness, snow depth, and general terrain features applicable to cross-country movement, routes of march, and avenues of approach.
5. Conduct exacting ice reconnaissance before considering frozen rivers or lakes as axes of advance for vehicles.
winter terrain study,ice reconnaissance,frozen rivers,trafficability conditions,map revision
Analyze Terrain Using OAKOC Method
Standard Source: STP 21 24 Army Warrior Leader Skills Level 2 3 and 4
Objective: Analyze terrain using the OAKOC method (Observation and fields of fire, Avenues of approach, Key terrain, Obstacles, and Cover and concealment) to determine how each aspect affects a tactical mission.

Conditions: Given any...
Objective: Analyze terrain using the OAKOC method (Observation and fields of fire, Avenues of approach, Key terrain, Obstacles, and Cover and concealment) to determine how each aspect affects a tactical mission.

Conditions: Given any tactical mission that involves a specified route or location on the ground or a map, and a standard 1:50,000 scale military map.

Standards: Analyze the route or location in terms of the five military aspects of terrain and determine how each aspect affects the mission.

### Steps:

1. Observation and fields of fire
- Identify terrain features that provide favorable observation for friendly or enemy forces, such as high ground.
- Consider visibility from these features; note that visibility is affected by weather conditions rather than terrain itself.
- Analyze the effects of terrain on direct and indirect fire weapons:
- Indirect fire is mainly affected by terrain conditions within the target area.
- Direct fire is mainly affected by terrain conditions between the weapon and the target.

2. Avenues of approach
- Define an avenue of approach as a route wide enough for a force of a particular size to reach an objective or key terrain.
- Analyze the following terrain considerations:
- Observation and fire: Determine if the avenue provides favorable observation and fire for the moving force.
- Concealment and cover: Assess whether the route offers adequate concealment and cover, noting that these may conflict with observation and fire.
- Obstacles: Identify obstacles perpendicular to the direction of advance (which should be avoided) and those parallel to the direction of advance (which can be advantageous).
- Use of key terrain: Consider how the route interacts with key terrain features.
- Adequate maneuver space: Ensure there is sufficient space for movement and deployment.

3. Key terrain
- Identify terrain that provides a significant advantage to either friendly or enemy forces due to its position, elevation, or other characteristics.
- Consider how key terrain features influence observation, fire, and avenues of approach in your analysis.

4. Obstacles
- Analyze the type, location, and impact of obstacles on movement, observation, and fire.
- Determine if obstacles are natural (e.g., rivers, cliffs) or man-made (e.g., minefields, barriers).
- Consider how obstacles can be used to restrict enemy movement or provide defensive advantages.

5. Cover and concealment
- Identify terrain features that offer cover (protection from direct fire) and concealment (hiding from observation).
- Assess the trade-off between cover/concealment and visibility for observation and fire.
- Consider how these factors influence movement, positioning, and engagement opportunities.

Note: Use the OAKOC acronym as a structured framework to ensure all aspects of terrain are analyzed systematically.
analyze terrain, OAKOC method, military map, observation fields of fire, avenues of approach, key terrain, obstacles, cover concealment
Construct a Terrain Model for Patrol Planning
Standard Source: Ranger Handbook 2006
Purpose: Create a visual aid to communicate patrol routes and actions on the objective during planning.

Materials Needed:
• Cardboard, MRE boxes, paper, or other available materials.
• String, colored tape, spray paint, chalk, etc.
• Moss,...
Purpose: Create a visual aid to communicate patrol routes and actions on the objective during planning.

Materials Needed:
• Cardboard, MRE boxes, paper, or other available materials.
• String, colored tape, spray paint, chalk, etc.
• Moss, pine needles, leaves, or grass for vegetation.
• Blue yarn, tin foil, or MRE creamer for water features.
• Red yarn, toy Rangers, poker chips, or M-16 rounds for enemy positions.
• Colored yarn or string for identifying key positions (breach, support by fire, assault).

Steps:
1. Create a base model of the terrain with exaggerated relief and water obstacles.
2. Use grid lines made from string or colored tape to mark coordinates.
3. Add objective location, friendly patrol locations, and targets (including indirect fires) using labels and symbols.
4. Mark primary and alternate routes for movement on the model.
5. Identify planned RPs (ORP, L/URP, RP) with clear markers.
6. Highlight danger areas such as roads, trails, or open spaces that may pose risks to the patrol.
7. Include a legend to explain all symbols and markings used on the model.
