8. Construct an enlarged terrain model of the objective area for detailed briefing purposes.
9. Use field-expedient techniques:
- Label key sites with 3 x 5 cards or MRE boxes.
- Replicate trees and vegetation using moss, green/brown spray paint, or pine needles.
- Designate water features with blue chalk, yarn, or tin foil.
- Use north-seeking arrows made from sharpened twigs, pencils, or colored yarn to indicate direction.
- Represent enemy positions with red yarn, toy Rangers, or poker chips.
- Mark friendly positions using M-16 rounds, toy Rangers, or small MRE packets.
- Use cardboard or paper for target reference points and indirect fire targets, ensuring grids are shown.
- Construct bunkers and buildings with MRE boxes or sticks.
- Create perimeter wire from a spiral notebook.
- Mark key phase lines with colored string or yarn.
- Replicate trench lines using colored tape, furrows, or chalk/spray paint.
terrain model, patrol planning, field-expedient techniques, map visualization, military navigation
Identify Major Mountain Systems and Their Locations
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Purpose: To recognize major mountain systems and their geographical locations for navigation and terrain analysis.

Procedure:
1. Identify the following major mountain systems and their respective locations:
- Andes: Central and South...
Purpose: To recognize major mountain systems and their geographical locations for navigation and terrain analysis.

Procedure:
1. Identify the following major mountain systems and their respective locations:
- Andes: Central and South America
- Rockies: North America (USA and Canada)
- Appalachians: North America (USA and Canada)
- Alps: Central Europe
- Himalayas: Asia
- Caucasus: Western Asia and Europe (Russia)
2. Use this knowledge to understand terrain characteristics, climate conditions, and potential navigational challenges in these regions.
3. Apply this information during map reading and land navigation exercises to improve situational awareness.
mountain systems, geography, navigation, terrain analysis
Conduct a Terrain Analysis at the Finish Point
Reference Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Steps:
1. After reaching the finish point, conduct a detailed terrain analysis to confirm your location.
2. Use map features and surrounding landmarks to verify that you are at the correct destination.
terrain analysis, map verification, navigation confirmation

Map Reading And Azimuths (7) -

Bypass an Obstacle Using 90-Degree Turns
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. At the edge of the obstacle, note the number of paces taken to reach this point.
2. To detour to the right, add 90 degrees to your original azimuth and use this new azimuth to select a steering mark.
3. **Move toward the...
Procedure:

1. At the edge of the obstacle, note the number of paces taken to reach this point.
2. To detour to the right, add 90 degrees to your original azimuth and use this new azimuth to select a steering mark.
3. Move toward the new steering mark on the new azimuth, beginning a new pace count.
4. Upon reaching the end of the obstacle, stop and note the number of paces taken.
5. Add or subtract 90 degrees from your current azimuth to continue moving around the obstacle.
6. Reach the far side of the obstacle, stop, and add this pace count to the one noted in step (1).
7. Adjust the azimuth again by adding or subtracting 90 degrees, then move the same number of paces as on the first leg of your detour.
8. Return to your original azimuth and continue using the final pace count from when you cleared the obstacle to proceed toward your finish point.
obstacle bypass,navigation detour,90 degree turns
Bypass an Obstacle at Night Using Celestial Navigation
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. At the edge of the obstacle, note the number of paces taken to reach this point.
2. Use a star or other celestial object as your steering mark, ensuring frequent checks of the azimuth when halted (not while moving).
3....
Procedure:

1. At the edge of the obstacle, note the number of paces taken to reach this point.
2. Use a star or other celestial object as your steering mark, ensuring frequent checks of the azimuth when halted (not while moving).
3. Add 90 degrees to your original azimuth and use it to select a new steering point.
4. Move toward the new steering point, beginning a new pace count.
5. Upon reaching the end of the obstacle, stop and note the number of paces taken.
6. Adjust the azimuth by adding or subtracting 90 degrees and continue moving around the obstacle using celestial navigation.
7. Reach the far side of the obstacle, stop, and add this pace count to the one noted in step (1).
8. Return to your original azimuth and use the final pace count from when you cleared the obstacle to proceed toward your finish point.
night navigation,celestial bypass,obstacle detour
Conduct Map Reconnaissance for Navigation Route
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Study the map area between the start point and finish point, paying attention to terrain features, obstacles, and potential landmarks.
2. Identify natural or man-made features that can be used as steering points (e.g.,...
Procedure:

1. Study the map area between the start point and finish point, paying attention to terrain features, obstacles, and potential landmarks.
2. Identify natural or man-made features that can be used as steering points (e.g., trees, buildings, rocks).
3. Note any potential obstacles or hazards along the route for planning detours or alternate paths.
4. Mark key reference points on the map to use during navigation.
map reconnaissance,route planning,navigation hazards
Convert Map Distance to Pace Count
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Determine the distance between checkpoints or from start to finish point (as measured on the map).
2. Calculate your pace count per 100 meters, if not already known.
3. Divide the total map distance by 100 and multiply...
Procedure:

1. Determine the distance between checkpoints or from start to finish point (as measured on the map).
2. Calculate your pace count per 100 meters, if not already known.
3. Divide the total map distance by 100 and multiply by your pace count per 100 meters to get the total number of paces needed.
4. Record the calculated pace count for use during navigation.
pace counting,map distance conversion,navigation planning
Determine Distance Between Checkpoints on a Map
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Locate the start point and finish point or intermediate points on the map.
2. Measure the straight-line distance between these points using a ruler or the map's scale bar.
3. **Convert the measured distance to real-world...
Procedure:

1. Locate the start point and finish point or intermediate points on the map.
2. Measure the straight-line distance between these points using a ruler or the map's scale bar.
3. Convert the measured distance to real-world units (e.g., meters) by referring to the map's scale.
4. Record the distance for use during navigation.
distance measurement,map scale,navigation planning
Determine Grid Azimuth on a Map
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Identify the start point and finish point or first intermediate point on the map.
2. Use a protractor or compass to determine the grid azimuth from the start point to the finish point or first intermediate point.
3....
Procedure:

1. Identify the start point and finish point or first intermediate point on the map.
2. Use a protractor or compass to determine the grid azimuth from the start point to the finish point or first intermediate point.
3. Record the grid azimuth for reference during navigation.

_Note: If you do not know how many paces you take for each 100 meters, move to a 100-meter course and determine your pace count._
grid azimuth,map navigation,determine direction
Select and Use Steering Points for Navigation
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Before moving from the start point, identify a well-defined object on the ground in the direction of travel (e.g., tree, building, rock). This is your first steering point.
2. Use your compass to shoot an azimuth to this...
Procedure:

1. Before moving from the start point, identify a well-defined object on the ground in the direction of travel (e.g., tree, building, rock). This is your first steering point.
2. Use your compass to shoot an azimuth to this steering point and record it for reference.
3. Begin your pace count as you move toward the first steering point.
4. At night, use a star or other celestial object as a steering mark, ensuring frequent checks of the azimuth when halted (not while moving).
5. Continue to select new steering points at each intermediate checkpoint and repeat the process until reaching the finish point.
steering points,celestial navigation,map to compass

Boat Handling (7) -

Approaching a Landing or Wharf Safely
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Approaching a Landing or Wharf Safely:

1. Always approach on the leeward side, which is opposite to where the wind is blowing.
2. Position the boat so that it is headed into the wind and waves for stability.
3. Stop rowing at a safe...
Approaching a Landing or Wharf Safely:

1. Always approach on the leeward side, which is opposite to where the wind is blowing.
2. Position the boat so that it is headed into the wind and waves for stability.
3. Stop rowing at a safe distance from the landing place and move forward with gentle headway.
4. Take in the oar nearest the landing, and if necessary, back water with the other oar to control the approach.
approaching a wharf, boat docking
Feathering Oars to Reduce Wind Resistance
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Feathering Oars to Reduce Wind Resistance:

1. When pulling: Turn the hands backward at the wrist as the oar blades leave the water, positioning them horizontally.
2. When backing water: Turn the hands forward at the wrist as the oar...
Feathering Oars to Reduce Wind Resistance:

1. When pulling: Turn the hands backward at the wrist as the oar blades leave the water, positioning them horizontally.
2. When backing water: Turn the hands forward at the wrist as the oar blades leave the water, positioning them horizontally.
3. This technique prevents wind from slowing down the boat by reducing resistance on the oars.
feathering oars, rowing efficiency
Proper Seating Position in Row-Boats
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Proper Seating Position in Row-Boats:

1. Always sit in the middle of the thwart or seat when entering a row-boat.
2. Avoid standing up in a small boat while it is in motion.
3. Never rock a boat for fun, as this can be dangerous and...
Proper Seating Position in Row-Boats:

1. Always sit in the middle of the thwart or seat when entering a row-boat.
2. Avoid standing up in a small boat while it is in motion.
3. Never rock a boat for fun, as this can be dangerous and disruptive to others.
4. This practice is instinctive among experienced sailors, fishermen, and those who use boats regularly.
seating position, row-boat safety, boat handling
Rowing Techniques: Pulling and Backing Water
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Rowing Techniques: Pulling and Backing Water:

1. Pulling: Sit with your back to the bow.
2. Propel the boat by pulling the handles toward your body, pressing the blades of the oars against the water toward the stern.
3. Push with your feet...
Rowing Techniques: Pulling and Backing Water:

1. Pulling: Sit with your back to the bow.
2. Propel the boat by pulling the handles toward your body, pressing the blades of the oars against the water toward the stern.
3. Push with your feet against a brace for stability.
4. Backing Water: Reverse the action of the oars by pushing the handles away from your body and pressing the blades of the oars against the water toward the bow.
rowing techniques, pulling, backing water
Sculling Technique for Propelling a Boat
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Sculling Technique for Propelling a Boat:

1. Use a single oar at the stern of the boat.
2. The boat must be equipped with a rowlock or semicircular scoop in the stern.
3. Propel the boat by moving the oar obliquely from side to side at...
Sculling Technique for Propelling a Boat:

1. Use a single oar at the stern of the boat.
2. The boat must be equipped with a rowlock or semicircular scoop in the stern.
3. Propel the boat by moving the oar obliquely from side to side at the stem.
4. This method is useful for short distances and when maneuvering among other boats in water.
sculling, boat propulsion
Steering a Boat Without a Rudder
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Steering a Boat Without a Rudder:

1. Fix your gaze on an object in the landscape that is in line with the middle of the stern and bow.
2. Keep this object aligned with the middle of the stern board to maintain course without constantly...
Steering a Boat Without a Rudder:

1. Fix your gaze on an object in the landscape that is in line with the middle of the stern and bow.
2. Keep this object aligned with the middle of the stern board to maintain course without constantly turning your head.
3. This technique helps you stay on course while reducing the need for frequent adjustments.
steering, boat navigation
Turning a Row-Boat
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Turning a Row-Boat:

1. To turn right: Row only with the left oar.
2. For a sharp turn: Pull with the left oar and back water with the right oar.
3. To turn left: Reverse the action of the oars (pull with the right oar and back water...
Turning a Row-Boat:

1. To turn right: Row only with the left oar.
2. For a sharp turn: Pull with the left oar and back water with the right oar.
3. To turn left: Reverse the action of the oars (pull with the right oar and back water with the left oar).
turning a boat, row-boat maneuvering

Geographical Definitions (6) -

Characterize the Arctic Region for Military Purposes
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
The factors that characterize the arctic in relation to other geographical regions are:

• a. location in the higher latitudes of the northern hemisphere;
• b. reception of less solar radiation and hence, less warming because of the oblique...
The factors that characterize the arctic in relation to other geographical regions are:

• a. location in the higher latitudes of the northern hemisphere;
• b. reception of less solar radiation and hence, less warming because of the oblique angle of incident solar radiation;
• c. mean temperature substantially lower than those of temperate regions;
• d. the absence of trees;
• e. an area consisting mainly of an ice-covered ocean and contiguous seas;
• f. wide ranges of duration of daylight and darkness;
• g. many areas receiving no more precipitation than most deserts;
• h. permafrost (permanently frozen ground) preventing drainage, and resulting in many lakes, rivers, and poorly drained areas;
• j. long periods of reduced visibility due to blowing snow, ice crystals or fog; and
• k. complete snow cover which, because of the barren ground, changes the appearance of the terrain.
arctic,charactersitics,military,navigation
Define the Arctic Region for Military Purposes
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
The arctic is defined as that region in which the average temperature in the warmest month is less than 10°C and the mean annual temperature is below 0°C. This definition describes the area north of the tree line, although small patches of trees may...
The arctic is defined as that region in which the average temperature in the warmest month is less than 10°C and the mean annual temperature is below 0°C. This definition describes the area north of the tree line, although small patches of trees may be found there as a result of drainage patterns and southern exposures. The northern limit of trees is generally restricted to 1° or 110 kilometres (km) (60 nautical miles) north of the tree line.
arctic,definition,military,navigation
Describe Ice Islands in the Arctic Ocean
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Ice islands are very extensive pieces of fresh-water ice that circulate in the central Arctic Ocean. They may be up to tens of kilometres in linear dimension, and have a height of 7 metres (m) or more (23 feet (ft) or more) above sea level. Such...
Ice islands are very extensive pieces of fresh-water ice that circulate in the central Arctic Ocean. They may be up to tens of kilometres in linear dimension, and have a height of 7 metres (m) or more (23 feet (ft) or more) above sea level. Such islands can be used as bases for both fixed-wing and rotary-wing aircraft.
ice_islands,definition,military,navigation
Describe Multi-Year Ice Floes in the Arctic Basin
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Throughout the Arctic Basin there are a large number of multi-year ice floes. These ice floes consist of large areas of cold sea ice which may be as much as 9 m (30 ft) thick. These ice floes have been used as sites for research and meteorological...
Throughout the Arctic Basin there are a large number of multi-year ice floes. These ice floes consist of large areas of cold sea ice which may be as much as 9 m (30 ft) thick. These ice floes have been used as sites for research and meteorological stations on which aircraft have been based.
ice_floes,definition,military,navigation
Describe the Arctic Ocean and Its Features
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
The Arctic Ocean surrounds the North Pole and is bordered by Canada, Alaska, Greenland, Europe, and Asia. Its unique feature is the presence of almost continuous ice cover. Throughout the winter, there are breaks in the pack ice, called leads, which...
The Arctic Ocean surrounds the North Pole and is bordered by Canada, Alaska, Greenland, Europe, and Asia. Its unique feature is the presence of almost continuous ice cover. Throughout the winter, there are breaks in the pack ice, called leads, which are continuously opening and closing exposing areas of relatively warmer water to cold temperature. In summer, the contrast between the colder ice and water and the overlying warmer air causes low, stratus cumulus clouds which can inhibit flying both in the arctic basin and its surrounding islands.
arctic_ocean,charactersitics,military,navigation
Describe the Canadian Subarctic and Its Significance
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Subarctic Canada embraces most of the forested areas but the main part is north of the more densely populated areas of eastern Canada, and to a lesser degree of western Canada. Northern Europe, and most of Alaska and the Soviet Union are within the...
Subarctic Canada embraces most of the forested areas but the main part is north of the more densely populated areas of eastern Canada, and to a lesser degree of western Canada. Northern Europe, and most of Alaska and the Soviet Union are within the subarctic. The subarctic is a primary area for exploitation and development because of its resources, and their accessibility by a rapidly developing transportation system.
canadian_subarctic,definition,military,navigation

Terrain Navigation (6) -

Determine Vehicle Slope-Climbing Capability Based on Surface Conditions
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Assess the slope surface condition:
- If the surface is dry and firm, military vehicles can climb a 60-percent (30-degree) slope.
- If the slope has gravel, vegetation, or mud, the practical climbing capability decreases...
Procedure:

1. Assess the slope surface condition:
- If the surface is dry and firm, military vehicles can climb a 60-percent (30-degree) slope.
- If the slope has gravel, vegetation, or mud, the practical climbing capability decreases to about 40 percent (20 degrees).

2. Use map contour lines to estimate slope:
- One contour line in 100 meters of map distance indicates a 10-percent slope.
- Two contour lines indicate a 20-percent slope, and so forth.
- If there are four contour lines in 100 meters, consider selecting an alternate route.

3. Adjust for different contour intervals:
- For example, if the map has a 10-meter interval and one contour line is present in 100 meters, this indicates a 10-percent slope.

4. Consider side slopes more than climbing slopes:
- The maximum safe side slope in good weather is 40 percent.
- Traverse side slopes slowly and without turning to avoid losing control or causing recovery issues.

5. Avoid obstacles on side slopes:
- Rocks, stumps, or sharp turns can cause a downhill track to be thrown under the vehicle, which is a major recovery task.
slope climbing, side slope, map contour lines, vehicle movement, terrain navigation
Understand and Adapt to Terrain in Northern Operations
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. **Recognize the importance of terrain:
- Terrain is a critical factor in military operations, particularly in northern regions where it can be more rugged than normal.
- The variety of terrain in northern areas means that...
Procedure:

1. **Recognize the importance of terrain:
- Terrain is a critical factor in military operations, particularly in northern regions where it can be more rugged than normal.
- The variety of terrain in northern areas means that different challenges exist depending on location.

2. **Assess terrain characteristics:
- Identify whether the terrain is flat (e.g., frozen lakes, prairies) or rugged (e.g., mountains, hills).
- Note the presence of natural features such as rocks, trees, or willows that may affect visibility and navigation.

3. **Adapt operations to terrain:
- Use the terrain to your advantage by selecting routes that provide cover, concealment, or elevation advantages.
- Avoid areas with poor visibility (e.g., flat snow-covered surfaces) during white-out or grey-out conditions.
- Ensure that all personnel are trained in navigating different types of northern terrain.

4. **Train personnel:
- Educate soldiers and operators on how to read and interpret northern terrain features.
- Conduct drills that simulate movement across various terrains to improve adaptability and preparedness.
northern terrain, military navigation, cold weather operations, survival tactics
Evaluate Lakes and Streams in Winter Conditions
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Consider the following when evaluating lakes and streams during winter:

1. Winter transformation: Bodies of water that are natural obstacles in summer may become avenues of approach in winter due to ice formation.
2. **Reevaluation...
Consider the following when evaluating lakes and streams during winter:

1. Winter transformation: Bodies of water that are natural obstacles in summer may become avenues of approach in winter due to ice formation.
2. Reevaluation needed: Defensive positions should be reevaluated before cold weather arrives to account for this change in terrain characteristics.
lakes,streams,winter navigation,ice formation
Move Through Forested Areas in Cold Weather
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Overview:
Forested areas present unique challenges for movement due to deep snow and low temperatures.

Steps:
1. Plan routes carefully, as few trails exist through forests, and those that do are poorly maintained.
2. **Use frozen...
Overview:
Forested areas present unique challenges for movement due to deep snow and low temperatures.

Steps:
1. Plan routes carefully, as few trails exist through forests, and those that do are poorly maintained.
2. Use frozen waterways whenever possible, as they provide stable routes for foot and vehicle movement.
3. Avoid deep snow areas, which can significantly slow progress and increase the risk of injury.
4. Maintain equipment regularly, especially vehicles, to ensure they function properly in cold conditions.
5. Train troops in navigation skills, as forested areas may be difficult to navigate without proper guidance.

Notes:
• Forested areas are more challenging for movement due to deep snow and low temperatures.
• Frozen waterways should be used strategically to improve mobility.
forested areas, deep snow, frozen waterways, navigation skills, cold weather movement
Navigate Snow-Covered and Icy Slopes
Standard Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
When encountering snow-covered or icy slopes:

1. Recognize the obstacle: Steep slopes become more difficult to traverse when covered by deep snow or ice.
2. Impact on mobility: Deep snow can cause vehicles to bog down and lose traction,...
When encountering snow-covered or icy slopes:

1. Recognize the obstacle: Steep slopes become more difficult to traverse when covered by deep snow or ice.
2. Impact on mobility: Deep snow can cause vehicles to bog down and lose traction, making slopes harder to surmount.
3. Vehicle adaptation: Remove track-laying vehicle pads when encountering this type of terrain to improve mobility.
snow-covered slopes,icy slopes,vehicle mobility,bogging down
Understand the Effects of Side Slopes on Mounted Vehicle Movement
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
When traversing side slopes:

• Maximum slope: Normally, a 30-percent slope is the maximum in good weather.
• Movement technique: Progress slowly and without turns. Rocks, stumps, or sharp turns can cause you to throw the downhill track...
When traversing side slopes:

• Maximum slope: Normally, a 30-percent slope is the maximum in good weather.
• Movement technique: Progress slowly and without turns. Rocks, stumps, or sharp turns can cause you to throw the downhill track under the vehicle, which would require significant recovery effort.
• Tactical considerations: Moving in draws or valleys provides cover but forces you to move more slowly due to side slopes.
side slope, vehicle movement, terrain navigation

Jungle Navigation (6) -

Create a Personal Pace Table for Jungle Navigation
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Determine your average pace count per 100 meters for each type of terrain you are likely to encounter in the jungle (e.g., tropical rain forest, secondary jungle, swamps, rice paddies).
2. Record or memorize these values...
Procedure:

1. Determine your average pace count per 100 meters for each type of terrain you are likely to encounter in the jungle (e.g., tropical rain forest, secondary jungle, swamps, rice paddies).
2. Record or memorize these values in a personal pace table.
3. Use this table during navigation to estimate distances traveled and adjust your route accordingly.
4. Update your table periodically based on actual performance in different terrains.

Notes:
• A personal pace table improves accuracy in dead reckoning.
• Terrain conditions such as dense vegetation, mud, or water can affect pace count.
personal pace table,jungle navigation,map reading,dead reckoning,terrain association
Navigate Through Bamboo Stands in the Jungle
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Avoid bamboo stands whenever possible due to their dense growth and difficulty in movement.
2. If passage through a bamboo stand is necessary:
- Move slowly and carefully to avoid noise that may attract attention or disrupt...
Procedure:

1. Avoid bamboo stands whenever possible due to their dense growth and difficulty in movement.
2. If passage through a bamboo stand is necessary:
- Move slowly and carefully to avoid noise that may attract attention or disrupt stealth operations.
- Use the compass for navigation since visibility is limited by the thick vegetation.
3. Be prepared for slow progress as bamboo stands are known for making movement difficult and exhausting.
4. Watch for signs of human activity, such as cut paths or markers, which may indicate previous use of the area.

Notes:
• Bamboo can be a formidable obstacle for both vehicles and personnel.
• Movement through bamboo is noisy and should be avoided if possible.
bamboo navigation,jungle terrain,map reading,stealth movement,obstacle avoidance
Navigate Through Swamps in Jungle Terrain
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Analyze the map and ground carefully before entering a swampy area.
2. Travel in small groups with only essential equipment to minimize the risk of being immobilized by water or vegetation.
3. Use dead reckoning as the...
Procedure:

1. Analyze the map and ground carefully before entering a swampy area.
2. Travel in small groups with only essential equipment to minimize the risk of being immobilized by water or vegetation.
3. Use dead reckoning as the primary navigation technique due to limited visibility and lack of natural features.
4. Be aware of the two basic types of swamps: mangrove and palm, each presenting different challenges for movement and concealment.
5. Move cautiously through waterlogged areas, avoiding deep or unstable ground that could lead to injury or equipment loss.

Notes:
• Mangrove swamps have tangled root systems that restrict both foot and small boat movement.
• Navigation in swamps is slow and requires careful planning.
swamp navigation,jungle terrain,map reading,dead reckoning,concealment
Navigate in Jungle Terrain Using Dead Reckoning
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Use a compass as the primary navigation tool due to limited visibility caused by dense vegetation.
2. Maintain an accurate pace count based on your personal pace table for different types of terrain (e.g., tropical rain...
Procedure:

1. Use a compass as the primary navigation tool due to limited visibility caused by dense vegetation.
2. Maintain an accurate pace count based on your personal pace table for different types of terrain (e.g., tropical rain forest, secondary jungle, swamps, rice paddies).
3. Estimate distances traveled using Table 13-3 as a rough guide for maximum travel distance per hour in various terrains:
- Tropical rain forest: up to 1,000 meters
- Deciduous forest: 500 meters
- Secondary jungle: 100 to 500 meters
- Tallgrass: 500 meters
- Swamps: 100 to 500 meters
- Rice paddies (wet): 800 meters
- Rice paddies (dry): 2,000 meters
- Plantations: 2,000 meters
- Trails: up to 3,000 meters
4. Adjust for terrain conditions such as thick vegetation and uneven ground that may slow movement.
5. Follow a body of water with a downstream flow if lost in the jungle, as it can lead back to civilization.
6. Be cautious of potential threats, including unfriendly civilizations encountered along the way.

Notes:
• Jungle navigation is complicated by limited visibility and difficult terrain.
• Use dead reckoning when direct observation is not possible.
dead reckoning,jungle navigation,map reading,compass use,personal pace table,terrain association
Use Modified Area/Point Navigation in the Jungle
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Primary use of the compass for dead reckoning to navigate toward an expanded objective that is easily identified by terrain association.
2. Once near the objective, switch to point navigation for the final leg of the...
Procedure:

1. Primary use of the compass for dead reckoning to navigate toward an expanded objective that is easily identified by terrain association.
2. Once near the objective, switch to point navigation for the final leg of the journey.
3. Use landmarks or terrain features visible above the canopy, if possible, to confirm your location.
4. Adjust your route as needed based on changes in terrain or unexpected obstacles.

Notes:
• This method combines area and point navigation for greater accuracy.
• It is useful when navigating through dense vegetation where direct observation is limited.
modified area navigation,jungle navigation,map reading,terrain association,compass use
Use Resection with Indirect Fire for Jungle Navigation
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Identify two widely separated grids that are not on terrain features you are currently occupying and are a safe distance from your estimated location.
2. Call for mortar or artillery fire, such as airbursts of white...
Procedure:

1. Identify two widely separated grids that are not on terrain features you are currently occupying and are a safe distance from your estimated location.
2. Call for mortar or artillery fire, such as airbursts of white phosphorus or illumination, to mark these grids.
3. Determine directions to the airbursts by sound if visual observation is not possible.
4. Use the marked grids to perform resection and determine your location relative to known points.
5. Adjust your navigation plan based on this information to correct your course.

Notes:
• This method requires coordination with supporting fire units.
• It is useful in areas where terrain association is not feasible due to dense vegetation.
resection,jungle navigation,map reading,indirect fire,artillery support

Skiing Techniques (6) -

Applying Variations and Applications of Ski Steps in Terrain Navigation
Reference Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. Assess Terrain: Evaluate the terrain for long, flat areas, bumps, dips, or uneven ground.
2. Use Glide Efficiently: In long, cross-country movement (especially with a pack and rifle), apply techniques that maximize glide...
Procedure:

1. Assess Terrain: Evaluate the terrain for long, flat areas, bumps, dips, or uneven ground.
2. Use Glide Efficiently: In long, cross-country movement (especially with a pack and rifle), apply techniques that maximize glide during each step to conserve energy.
3. Alternate Steps: Avoid using the same step repeatedly to prevent fatigue. Use variations such as one step without poling or alternate combinations of steps and poling.
4. Bumpy Terrain Technique:
- In bumpy terrain, use ski steps and poling individually or in combination for a strong pushoff through dips and over bumps.
- Apply double poling on the crest of the first bump to gain momentum for the next bump.
- Use a step supported by double poling when skiing through dips.
5. Adjust Poling Technique: In some situations, apply double poling without taking a step to increase forward motion.
ski variations, terrain navigation, cold weather, military training, cross-country skiing
Execute Straight Downhill Running Technique
Reference Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Use: Straight downhill running is the first technique learned in skiing downhill. It provides the individual with the balance which he must have before he can effectively descend a slope or learn more advanced techniques. Although it is the...
Use: Straight downhill running is the first technique learned in skiing downhill. It provides the individual with the balance which he must have before he can effectively descend a slope or learn more advanced techniques. Although it is the fastest means of descending, speed must be kept within the capabilities of the skier.

Technique:
1. Assume a normal standing position with skis flat and parallel.
2. Advance one ski 10 to 15 cm (4" to 6").
3. Evenly distribute body weight on both skis, keeping knees bent and pushed forward from the ankles with heels flat on the skis.
4. Lean slightly forward in a relaxed and natural upright position, head up, with knees and ankles flexed without bending the body at the waist to the front.
5. Hold ski poles pointing to the rear with baskets above the snow, arms bent slightly at the elbows and held close to the body with hands to the front.
6. Keep body and arms relaxed, knees supple to act as shock absorbers. Remain alert at all times.
downhill running, skiing technique, slope descent
Execute the Herringbone Technique for Ascent
Reference Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Use: The herringbone is used to climb short, moderate, or steep slopes. It provides a quicker ascent than the sidestep. It is more tiring and should be used only for relatively short ascents.

Technique:
1. Face uphill with skis spread to...
Use: The herringbone is used to climb short, moderate, or steep slopes. It provides a quicker ascent than the sidestep. It is more tiring and should be used only for relatively short ascents.

Technique:
1. Face uphill with skis spread to form a wide V by spreading both ski tips outward.
2. Edge the skis sharply inward, bending the knees forward and inward to prevent backslip.
3. Place weight on one ski, raise the other slightly above the snow, and move it forward and upward.
4. Place the raised ski in the snow, edge it inward, and transfer body weight to it.
5. Move the other ski in the same manner and place it slightly ahead.
6. Use ski poles in the same manner as the sidestep, but alternately place them to the rear of the body and to the outside of each ski to act as a brace and aid in the climb.
herringbone, skiing technique, ascent, slope climbing
Execute the Herringbone Turn for Direction Change
Reference Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Use: The herringbone turn is a method of changing direction while traversing a slope, while climbing, or when in confined areas where a kick turn may be difficult to use. It is also used to change direction from a herringbone...
Use: The herringbone turn is a method of changing direction while traversing a slope, while climbing, or when in confined areas where a kick turn may be difficult to use. It is also used to change direction from a herringbone position.

Technique:
1. From a traversing position, move the upper ski first in the desired direction using its heel as a pivot point.
2. Place this ski into the snow, as in a herringbone step, with full body weight on it.
3. Move the other ski up in the same way and place it into the snow, bringing the skier into a herringbone position.
4. Hold both poles to the rear to brace the body during this movement.
5. Repeat the cycle until the lower ski has reached the desired direction.
6. Bring the upper ski parallel with the lower ski into a traversing position again, completing the herringbone turn.
herringbone turn, direction change, skiing technique
Executing the Two Step Ski Technique
Reference Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure:

1. Starting Position: Begin in a standing position with knees slightly bent.
2. First Step: Take a walking step with the left ski to initiate motion.
3. Lunge and Glide: Make a lunge from the left leg, creating a long...
Procedure:

1. Starting Position: Begin in a standing position with knees slightly bent.
2. First Step: Take a walking step with the left ski to initiate motion.
3. Lunge and Glide: Make a lunge from the left leg, creating a long glide on the right ski.
4. Positioning Left Ski: While gliding on the right ski, slowly bring the left ski forward until it is even with the right ski. This completes the first two step and prepares for the next one.
5. Pole Technique: As the first step is made, bring both ski poles straight to the front in a comfortable reach and set them into the snow alongside the skis in coordination with the lunge of the second step.
6. Pushing Action: Apply pushing action with the poles as described for one pole use. Bring the poles forward in a straight line in preparation for the next step, ensuring proper timing to coordinate with the next lunge.
two step, skiing technique, cold weather, military training, cross-country skiing
Executing an Uphill Christiania Turn for Speed Control and Stopping
Reference Source: FM 31 70 Basic Cold Weather Manual
Procedure for Executing an Uphill Christiania Turn:

1. Preparation for the Turn:
- Bring the upper shoulder well forward to increase body rotation that will be applied during the turn.

2. Initiating the Turn:
- Decrease the...
Procedure for Executing an Uphill Christiania Turn:

1. Preparation for the Turn:
- Bring the upper shoulder well forward to increase body rotation that will be applied during the turn.

2. Initiating the Turn:
- Decrease the amount of edging on the skis.
- Rotate the lower shoulder and hip forward in the direction of the turn.
- Increase forward lean of the body and knee bend.
- Ensure that the upper ski leads throughout the turn (see figure 4-28).

3. Controlling the Skis During the Turn:
- Control both skis by gradually edging them into the slope.
- Keep your weight directly over the skis.
- Avoid leaning into the slope.

4. Continuing the Turn:
- Increase and continue forward lean and body rotation as the turn progresses.
- Forward speed will gradually decrease, allowing you to choose whether to continue in a new direction or come to a stop.

5. Pole Control During the Turn:
- Be careful not to allow the ski poles to swing forward during the rotation of the body.

6. Turn Flexibility and Initiation from Fall Line:
- This turn can be made from any angle across the slope, including the fall line.
- From a fall line, the turn can be made in either direction.
- To prepare for a turn from the fall line:
- Advance the ski corresponding with the direction of the turn (e.g., left ski leads for a left turn).
- Place more body weight on the other ski.
- Emphasize body rotation and knee bend to initiate the turning action of the skis.

7. Using Down-Up Motion for Assistance:
- As the turning action is initiated as described in step 2, lower your body and return it to normal position as the turn is completed.
uphill christiania, skiing technique, speed control, stopping, slope navigation

Map Reading And Sketching (6) -

Determine Target Direction, Elevation, and Range Using T&E Mechanism
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Procedure: Determine Target Direction, Elevation, and Range Using T&E Mechanism

Steps:
1. Using the T&E mechanism, determine the direction, elevation, and range to each target.
2. Write this information in the DATA SECTION of the range card...
### Procedure: Determine Target Direction, Elevation, and Range Using T&E Mechanism

Steps:
1. Using the T&E mechanism, determine the direction, elevation, and range to each target.
2. Write this information in the DATA SECTION of the range card as follows:
- Center the traversing handwheel on the center mark.
T&E mechanism, target direction, elevation calculation, range card
Determine and Mark Gun Position Relative to Terrain Feature
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Procedure: Determine and Mark Gun Position Relative to Terrain Feature

Steps:
1. Determine the location of the gun position relative to a prominent terrain feature (e.g., hilltop, road junction, building).
2. Use any terrain feature located...
### Procedure: Determine and Mark Gun Position Relative to Terrain Feature

Steps:
1. Determine the location of the gun position relative to a prominent terrain feature (e.g., hilltop, road junction, building).
2. Use any terrain feature located within 1,000 meters of the gun.
3. If no such feature exists, write in the eight-digit map coordinates for your position on the sketch.
4. Use a compass to determine the azimuth in mils or degrees from the terrain feature to the gun position or from the gun position to the terrain feature.
5. Compute the back azimuth from the gun to the feature by adding or subtracting 3,200 mils or 180 degrees.
6. Determine the distance between the gun and the feature by pacing or looking at a map.
7. Draw the terrain feature in the lower left- or right-hand corner of the card, whichever better shows the terrain feature's position relative to the gun.
terrain feature, azimuth calculation, map coordinates, compass use
Determine and Mark Magnetic North on Range Card
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Procedure: Determine and Mark Magnetic North on Range Card

Steps:
1. Using the lensatic compass, determine magnetic north.
2. Write the following information in the top portion of the range card:
- SQD: Write in the squad's...
### Procedure: Determine and Mark Magnetic North on Range Card

Steps:
1. Using the lensatic compass, determine magnetic north.
2. Write the following information in the top portion of the range card:
- SQD: Write in the squad's designation.
- PLT: Write in the platoon's designation.
- CO: Write in the company's designation.
- MAGNETIC NORTH: Sketch the direction for magnetic north using the symbol shown in B, figure 071-000-0005-1.
magnetic north, lensatic compass, range card, military designation
Draw Barbed Line and Record Information on Range Card
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Procedure: Draw Barbed Line and Record Information on Range Card

Steps:
1. Draw a barbed line between the position and the terrain feature.
2. Above the line, record the distance in meters.
3. Below the line, record the azimuth in mils or...
### Procedure: Draw Barbed Line and Record Information on Range Card

Steps:
1. Draw a barbed line between the position and the terrain feature.
2. Above the line, record the distance in meters.
3. Below the line, record the azimuth in mils or degrees from the terrain feature to the gun.
4. Use the direction of the arrow's barbs to show the direction of the azimuth.
barbed line, distance recording, azimuth marking, range card
Sketch Primary Sector of Fire on Range Card
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Procedure: Sketch Primary Sector of Fire on Range Card

Steps:
1. Use either a PDF (Primary Direction of Fire) or an FPL (Final Protection Line) to sketch the primary sector of fire.
2. FPL: Use an FPL only if the position offers a good...
### Procedure: Sketch Primary Sector of Fire on Range Card

Steps:
1. Use either a PDF (Primary Direction of Fire) or an FPL (Final Protection Line) to sketch the primary sector of fire.
2. FPL: Use an FPL only if the position offers a good grazing distance. Base the primary sector of the line of fire. Make the FPL the sector limit closest to friendly troops.
- Sketch the limits of the primary sector of fire.
- Sketch an FPL on the sector limit assigned by your leader.
- Have someone walk the FPL (if the enemy situation permits) to identify and measure dead spaces (sections of an FPL where a person drops below the light of sight).
- Show the dead space(s) on the sketch by a break(s) in the FPL symbol. Write in the ranges to the beginning and end of each dead space(s).
- Write in the maximum range of graze.
3. PDF: If you cannot identify an FPL, assign a PDF.
- Sketch the limits of the assigned primary sector of fire; the angle should not exceed 875 mils (the maximum traverse of a tripod-mounted machine gun).
- Sketch the symbol for a light automatic weapon. Orient the symbol toward the most dangerous target in the sector (get this information from the platoon leader).
primary sector of fire, FPL, PDF, range card, military symbol
Sketch Terrain and Machine Gun Position on Range Card
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
### Procedure: Sketch Terrain and Machine Gun Position on Range Card

Steps:
1. On the range card, roughly sketch the terrain in front of the position.
- Center the machine gun position at the bottom of the sketch.
2. Include any prominent...
### Procedure: Sketch Terrain and Machine Gun Position on Range Card

Steps:
1. On the range card, roughly sketch the terrain in front of the position.
- Center the machine gun position at the bottom of the sketch.
2. Include any prominent natural and man-made features that a target could use to take cover.
terrain sketch, machine gun position, range card, natural features, man-made features

Sailing Techniques (6) -

Reefing the Sail in Strong Winds
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. If the wind is too strong for your boat and you are carrying too much sail:
- Let the boat come up into the wind.
2. Take in one or two reefs by doing the following steps:
- Step 1: Let out both the throat and peak...
Procedure:

1. If the wind is too strong for your boat and you are carrying too much sail:
- Let the boat come up into the wind.
2. Take in one or two reefs by doing the following steps:
- Step 1: Let out both the throat and peak halliards enough to give sufficient slack of sail.
- Step 2: Haul the sail out toward the end of the boom.
- Step 3: Roll the sail up and tie the points under and around it, but not around the boom.
- Step 4: Always use a square or reef knot in tying your reef points.
3. In case of a squall or strong puff of wind:
- Ease pressure on the sail by turning the bow into the wind.
- If you need to shorten suddenly, drop your peak by loosening the peak halliards.
reefing sail, strong wind, sailing safety
Right of Way in Sailing
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. When two boats approach each other in opposite directions and are close hauled:
- The boat on the starboard tack has the right of way and should continue her course.
- The responsibility of avoiding a collision rests...
Procedure:

1. When two boats approach each other in opposite directions and are close hauled:
- The boat on the starboard tack has the right of way and should continue her course.
- The responsibility of avoiding a collision rests with the boat sailing on the port tack.
2. A boat running before the wind must always give way to a boat that is close hauled.
3. When sailing through high waves:
- Try to head into them directly at right angles as much as possible.
- Steer as steadily as possible.
- Keep the boat on her course and avoid letting your mind wander.
- Only slight motion of the tiller from side to side will be necessary.
right of way, sailing safety, navigation
Sailing Before the Wind (Free Sailing)
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. When sailing free or before the wind, the boat is pointing with the wind blowing directly from behind.
2. This is the extreme opposite of sailing close hauled or on the wind.
3. If sailing free on the port tack, with the boom at...
Procedure:

1. When sailing free or before the wind, the boat is pointing with the wind blowing directly from behind.
2. This is the extreme opposite of sailing close hauled or on the wind.
3. If sailing free on the port tack, with the boom at right angles to the mast on the starboard side:
- Steering too far to starboard can cause the wind to strike the sail's outer edge (leech), resulting in a violent movement of the sail and boom toward the port side.
4. This dangerous maneuver is called *jibing* and should be carefully avoided when sailing before the wind.
free sailing, jibing, navigation, boat handling
Executing 'Ready About' Maneuver
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Before turning your bow so that the wind will strike the other side of the sail at its mast end (called *going about*), the person at the helm should give a warning by singing out: "ready about."
2. This maneuver is just the...
Procedure:

1. Before turning your bow so that the wind will strike the other side of the sail at its mast end (called *going about*), the person at the helm should give a warning by singing out: "ready about."
2. This maneuver is just the opposite of jibbing.
ready about, sailing maneuvers, navigation
Sailing Close to Wind
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Position the boat so that it is pointing as nearly as possible against the wind.
2. This technique is called *sailing close to the wind*.
3. When the bow is turned to the right or left, and the wind strikes both the boat and sail...
Procedure:

1. Position the boat so that it is pointing as nearly as possible against the wind.
2. This technique is called *sailing close to the wind*.
3. When the bow is turned to the right or left, and the wind strikes both the boat and sail at right angles, you are sailing with the wind abeam.
4. As you let out your sheet so that the boom makes a larger angle with an imaginary line running from the mast to the middle of the stern, you are sailing off the wind.
5. When the sail stands at right angles to this same line, you are sailing free or before the wind.
sailing, close to wind, navigation, boat handling
Flying the Flag on a Sailboat
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. The fly or pennant is carried at the top of the mast.
2. The flag is carried at the peak or upper corner of the sail at the end of the gaff.
3. To salute:
- Tip or slightly lower the flag and raise it again into...
Procedure:

1. The fly or pennant is carried at the top of the mast.
2. The flag is carried at the peak or upper corner of the sail at the end of the gaff.
3. To salute:
- Tip or slightly lower the flag and raise it again into position.
flying the flag, sailing etiquette, navigation

Orientation (5) -

Confirm Grid North Direction with Pilot for Weather Observations
High Priority Source: US Antarctic Field Manual
Procedure:

1. Confirm with the pilot which is grid north by using a GPS or the sun.
2. Use this information to establish directional flags for weather observations.

Details:
• This ensures accurate orientation and proper setup of weather...
Procedure:

1. Confirm with the pilot which is grid north by using a GPS or the sun.
2. Use this information to establish directional flags for weather observations.

Details:
• This ensures accurate orientation and proper setup of weather observation systems.
grid north, directional flags
Determine Cardinal Directions
High Priority Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
Objective: Identify cardinal directions (north, south, east, west) in a survival situation.

Methods:
1. Use the sun’s position: In the Northern Hemisphere, the sun rises in the east and sets in the west. At noon, it is approximately due...
Objective: Identify cardinal directions (north, south, east, west) in a survival situation.

Methods:
1. Use the sun’s position: In the Northern Hemisphere, the sun rises in the east and sets in the west. At noon, it is approximately due south.
2. Use natural indicators: Moss tends to grow more on the north side of trees in the Northern Hemisphere; animal tracks may be deeper on the south side.
3. Use a watch: In the Northern Hemisphere, point the hour hand at the sun and bisect the angle between the hour hand and 12 o’clock to find south.
4. Use stars: Locate the North Star (Polaris) in the night sky for north.

Notes: Practice these methods in advance and verify with a compass if available.
cardinal directions, navigation, survival orientation
Determine Direction Using a Knife Blade and Shadow
High Priority Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Use the knife blade method:
- Place the point of your knife blade on your thumb nail.
- Rotate the blade until the full shadow of the blade falls directly on the nail.
- This indicates the direction of the sun, and...
Procedure:

1. Use the knife blade method:
- Place the point of your knife blade on your thumb nail.
- Rotate the blade until the full shadow of the blade falls directly on the nail.
- This indicates the direction of the sun, and thus helps determine cardinal directions (east, west, etc.).

2. Use the sun's position for orientation:
- Face the sun in the morning and spread your arms out to the sides.
- The east is before you.
- The west is behind you.
- South is to your right.
- North is to your left.

3. Observe grass growth:
- Grass tends to lean toward the sun, which can help determine direction at night.

Notes:
• This method works best on cloudy days when the sun's position is not directly visible.
direction finding,sun shadow,navigation survival
Determine East-West Line Using Shadow During Equinoxes
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. Identify Equinoctial Periods: Use this method only on March 21st or September 21st, when the sun is directly over the equator.

2. Set Up a Vertical Object: Insert a straight stick or pole into the ground so that it stands...
Procedure:

1. Identify Equinoctial Periods: Use this method only on March 21st or September 21st, when the sun is directly over the equator.

2. Set Up a Vertical Object: Insert a straight stick or pole into the ground so that it stands upright and casts a shadow.

3. Mark Shadow Position at Two Times:
- At time T1, mark the tip of the shadow with a small object (e.g., a stone) to create point A.
- Wait approximately 5 minutes and mark the new position of the shadow tip with another object to create point B.

4. Draw Line Between Points: Connect points A and B with a straight line using a string or stick. This line represents a true East-West direction, with the sun moving from east to west during the day.

5. Accuracy Note: For latitudes less than 40°N or 40°S, this method can be used with reasonable accuracy between March 1st and April 14th, or September 1st and October 14th. Outside these dates, use the curved shadow method for greater precision.

Notes:
• This technique works best on flat ground with minimal obstructions to ensure a clear shadow.
• Ensure that the object casting the shadow is perfectly vertical for accuracy.
east-west line, equinox, shadow method, navigation, orientation
Use Watch Method to Determine True North/South in North Temperate Zone
Standard Source: How To Avoid Getting Lost
Procedure:

1. In the North temperate zone, point the hour hand of your watch toward the sun.
2. The south line is midway between the hour hand and 1200 hours (standard time).
3. If it is daylight saving time, the...
Procedure:

1. In the North temperate zone, point the hour hand of your watch toward the sun.
2. The south line is midway between the hour hand and 1200 hours (standard time).
3. If it is daylight saving time, the north-south line is found between the hour hand and 1300 hours.

Note:
• If there is any doubt as to which end of the line is north, remember that the sun is in the east before noon and in the west after noon.
watch method,north temperate zone,true north,south line,daylight saving time

Map Orientation (5) -

Orient a Map Using a Built-In Protractor
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Orienting a Map Using a Built-In Protractor:

1. Identify built-in protractor: Some maps have a built-in protractor consisting of a pivot point "P" on the south neat line and several degrees of arc along the north neat...
Procedure for Orienting a Map Using a Built-In Protractor:

1. Identify built-in protractor: Some maps have a built-in protractor consisting of a pivot point "P" on the south neat line and several degrees of arc along the north neat line.

2. Form G-M angle using protractor: Connect the pivot point "P" with the appropriate value of the G-M angle (taken from the declination diagram) on the arc to form the G-M line.

3. Align compass with G-M line: Place the compass parallel along this G-M line and rotate the map and compass until the needle point aligns with the continuous line formed by the index line and the sighting wire.

4. Final orientation: Ensure that the map is properly oriented when the needle of the compass aligns with the line created by the black index line and the sighting wire.

Alternate method for maps without built-in protractors:

1. Draw magnetic north line: Use a protractor to draw a magnetic north line from any N-S and E-W grid line intersection on the map.

2. Align compass with magnetic north line: Place the straightedge of the compass along this drawn magnetic north line.

3. Rotate map and compass together: Rotate both the map and compass until the north arrow falls beneath the fixed black index line on the compass.

Notes:
• This procedure is only required when the G-M angle exceeds 3 degrees (50 mils) or if the map has a built-in protractor.
• Ensure that the magnetic north line drawn aligns with the declination diagram for accuracy.
built-in protractor,map orientation,navigation,G-M angle,compass alignment
Orient a Map Using a Compass and Declination Diagram
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure for Orienting a Map Using a Compass and Declination Diagram:

1. Position the compass on the map: Place the compass parallel to a north-south grid line with the cover side of the compass pointing towards the top of the map. This...
Procedure for Orienting a Map Using a Compass and Declination Diagram:

1. Position the compass on the map: Place the compass parallel to a north-south grid line with the cover side of the compass pointing towards the top of the map. This will align the black index line on the dial of the compass parallel to grid north.

2. Align declination diagram: Rotate the map and compass until the directions of the declination diagram formed by the black index line and the compass needle match the direction shown in the declination diagram printed on the margin of the map. This step ensures that magnetic north aligns with grid north, accounting for declination.

3. Adjust for G-M angle:
- If the magnetic north arrow on the map is to the left of the grid north, the compass reading equals the G-M angle (given by the declination diagram).
- If the magnetic north is to the right of the grid north, the compass reading equals 360 degrees (6,400 mils) minus the G-M angle.

4. Align compass needle with index line: Ensure that the compass north arrow points in the same direction as the magnetic north arrow on the map. The compass reading will be apparent after this alignment.

5. Special case for small G-M angles: If the G-M angle is less than 3 degrees (50 mils), do not align the north arrow; it is not necessary for accurate orientation.

Notes:
• This procedure assumes that the map has a declination diagram on its margin and that the compass includes a black index line and magnetic needle.
• Ensure there are no sources of magnetic interference (e.g., power lines, vehicles) when performing this task.
compass,map orientation,declination diagram,G-M angle,navigation
Orienting a Map Using Land Features
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. Identify two or three recognizable land features visible from your position.
2. Locate these same features on your map.
3. Rotate the map until the identified features on the map align with their corresponding positions on the...
Procedure:

1. Identify two or three recognizable land features visible from your position.
2. Locate these same features on your map.
3. Rotate the map until the identified features on the map align with their corresponding positions on the ground.
4. Ensure that the map fits all visible ground features from your current location.

Notes:
• This method is most effective when multiple landmarks are available for reference.
• Accuracy improves with more recognizable and distinct land features.
map orientation,land features,navigation,map reading
Orienting a Map Using a Compass
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. Place your compass on the map, aligning its axis with the TRUE north line.
2. Rotate both the compass and the map until the compass needle points to MAGNETIC north on the map.
3. Note that **MAGNETIC north may differ...
Procedure:

1. Place your compass on the map, aligning its axis with the TRUE north line.
2. Rotate both the compass and the map until the compass needle points to MAGNETIC north on the map.
3. Note that MAGNETIC north may differ from TRUE north, depending on your location (this is shown as magnetic variation on maps).
4. Hold a shadow stick on the north-south line of the sun clock, opposite the appropriate date.
5. Where the shadow falls across the latitude line indicates local sun time.
6. Correct for standard or clock time by adjusting using:
- The equation of time shown for the current date.
- Longitude correction: Deduct 4 minutes per degree east of standard time longitude, or add 4 minutes per degree west.
7. When the compass needle aligns with magnetic north on the map, your map is correctly oriented.

Notes:
• Magnetic variation must be accounted for to ensure accurate orientation.
• This method requires a functioning compass and knowledge of local time zones.
compass,map orientation,magnetic north,TRUE north
Orienting a Map Using a Watch and Sun Compass
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. Place a thin shadow stick on the center line of the sun compass, ensuring it is held flat.
2. Set the shadow stick opposite the appropriate date on the sun compass.
3. Rotate the map until the shadow falls across the adjusted...
Procedure:

1. Place a thin shadow stick on the center line of the sun compass, ensuring it is held flat.
2. Set the shadow stick opposite the appropriate date on the sun compass.
3. Rotate the map until the shadow falls across the adjusted time on the latitude line.
4. Once aligned, your map will be oriented to TRUE north and match ground features.

Notes:
• This method requires a watch set to correct local time.
• The sun compass must be used in conjunction with accurate timekeeping for proper orientation.
sun compass,map orientation,watch navigation

Astronomy (5) -

Identify the Stars Using Reference Books
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Obtain one or more astronomy reference books such as:
- *A Field Book of the Stars* by W. F. Olcutt (Putnam)
- *Astronomy* by Julia McNair Wright (Penn Pub. Co.)
- *Astronomy by Observation* by Eliza A. Bowen (American...
Procedure:

1. Obtain one or more astronomy reference books such as:
- *A Field Book of the Stars* by W. F. Olcutt (Putnam)
- *Astronomy* by Julia McNair Wright (Penn Pub. Co.)
- *Astronomy by Observation* by Eliza A. Bowen (American Book Co.)
- *Astronomy for Everybody* by Simon Newcomb (Doubleday, Page & Co.)
- *Astronomy with an Opera Glass* by G. P. Serviss
- *A Study of the Sky* by H. A. Howe (Scribners)
- *Astronomy with the Naked Eye* by P. Serviss (G. Harper Bros.)
- *Children's Book of the Stars* by Milton (Macmillan Co.)
- *Earth and Sky Every Child Should Know* by J. E. Rogers
- *How to Identify the Stars* by W. J. Milham
- *How to Locate the Stars* by Hinds, Noble & Co.
- *Popular Astronomy* by G. Flammarion
- *Round the Year with the Stars* by G. P. Serviss
- *Starland Ball* by Ginn & Co.
- *Steele's Popular Astronomy* by J. D. Steele (American Book Co.)
- *The Friendly Stars* by M. E. Martin
- *The Romance of Modern Astronomy* by McPherson (Lippincott)

2. Use these books to identify constellations, stars, and celestial objects in the night sky.
3. Cross-reference star positions with the time of year and location on Earth for accurate identification.
4. Utilize additional resources like *A Study of the Sky* or *How to Locate the Stars* for detailed star maps and navigation techniques.
identify stars, astronomy books, constellations, celestial objects, star maps, navigation
Determine Sun's Declination and Height in the Sky for Any Period of the Year
Reference Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. Understand Sun's Annual Path: The sun's declination (its angular distance north or south of the equator) changes throughout the year:
- June 21st: Farthest North (approximately +23.5°)
- September 21st: Over the...
Procedure:

1. Understand Sun's Annual Path: The sun's declination (its angular distance north or south of the equator) changes throughout the year:
- June 21st: Farthest North (approximately +23.5°)
- September 21st: Over the Equator (0°)
- December 21st: Farthest South (approximately -23.5°)
- March 21st: Over the Equator again (0°)

2. Determine Declination for a Specific Date:
- Use a declination table or estimate based on the sun's position relative to equinoxes and solstices.
- For example, in mid-April, the sun is approximately +15° north of the equator.

3. Measure Sun's Zenith (Highest Point):
- At solar noon (when the sun reaches its highest point), measure the angle between the sun and the horizon using a simple method:
- Use a stick or pole placed vertically in the ground to cast a shadow at solar noon.
- Measure the length of the shadow and use basic trigonometry: tan(angle) = (length of shadow)/(height of object)

4. Calculate Sun's Height Above Horizon:
- The sun's height above the horizon is equal to 90° minus the angle measured in step 3.
- Example: If the angle between the sun and the horizon is 65°, then the sun's height is 25° above the horizon.

Notes:
• This method assumes you have access to a declination table or can estimate based on known dates (e.g., equinoxes, solstices).
• Solar noon occurs when the sun reaches its highest point in the sky and casts the shortest shadow of the day.
sun declination, solar height, navigation, astronomy, sun position
Identify and Locate Major Stars in the Night Sky
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
### Procedure: Identify and Locate Major Stars in the Night Sky

Overview:
This procedure helps identify major stars visible to the naked eye, including those visible from New York and the Southern Hemisphere.

Steps:
1. **Understand star...
### Procedure: Identify and Locate Major Stars in the Night Sky

Overview:
This procedure helps identify major stars visible to the naked eye, including those visible from New York and the Southern Hemisphere.

Steps:
1. Understand star visibility patterns:
- Each fifteen days, the stars appear an hour earlier in the sky.
- In three months, they gain one fourth of the circle; in a year, they complete the full circle.
2. List of visible stars (as per Flammarion):
- Visible from New York:
1. Sirius, the Dog-star
2. Arcturus, of Bootes
3. Vega, of the Lyre
4. Rigel, of Orion's foot
5. Capella, of Auriga
6. Procyon, or the Little Dog-star
7. Betelguese, of Orion's right shoulder
8. Aldebaran, of Taurus, the Bull's right eye
9. Antares, of Scorpio
10. Altair, of the Eagle
11. Spica, of Virgo
12. Fomalhaut, of the Southern Fish
13. Regulus, of the Lion
- Visible only in the Southern Hemisphere:
1. [Canopus, of Argo]
2. [Alpha, of the Centaur]
3. [Beta, of the Centaur]
4. [Achernar, of Eridanus]
5. [Alpha, of the Southern Cross]
6. [Beta, of the Southern Cross]

Notes:
• There are about seven thousand stars visible to the naked eye.
• Of these, only nineteen are first-magnitude stars (the brightest).
stars, navigation, astronomy, Flammarion, Sirius, Canopus, Arcturus, Vega, Rigel, Capella, Procyon, Betelguese, Aldebaran, Antares, Altair, Spica, Fomalhaut, Regulus
Identify and Locate the Constellation Orion
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
### Procedure: Identify and Locate the Constellation Orion

Overview:
This procedure helps identify the constellation Orion in the night sky.

Steps:
1. Recognize key stars of Orion:
- Betelguese (right shoulder)
- Rigel (foot)
...
### Procedure: Identify and Locate the Constellation Orion

Overview:
This procedure helps identify the constellation Orion in the night sky.

Steps:
1. Recognize key stars of Orion:
- Betelguese (right shoulder)
- Rigel (foot)
- The Three Kings (three stars forming the belt)
2. Understand the mythological background:
- Orion was a hunter who was taken to Heaven after death and now marches around the sky.
- He is visible only in winter because during summer, he passes over during the day.
3. Identify related stars and constellations:
- Sirius (the Dog-star) is in the head of Orion's hound.
- Procyon (the Little Dog-star) follows farther back from Sirius.
4. Historical context:
- In old star charts, Orion is shown with his hound hunting Taurus (the Bull).

Notes:
• Orion is considered the finest constellation in the heavens due to its striking array of brilliant stars.
Orion, navigation, astronomy, Betelguese, Rigel, Three Kings, Sirius, Procyon, Taurus
Identify and Locate the Pleiades Star Cluster
Reference Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
### Procedure: Identify and Locate the Pleiades Star Cluster

Overview:
This procedure helps identify the Pleiades star cluster in the night sky.

Steps:
1. Locate the Pleiades:
- In winter, they can be seen as a cluster of small...
### Procedure: Identify and Locate the Pleiades Star Cluster

Overview:
This procedure helps identify the Pleiades star cluster in the night sky.

Steps:
1. Locate the Pleiades:
- In winter, they can be seen as a cluster of small stars between Aldebaran and Algol.
- A line drawn from the back bottom through the front rim of the Dipper, about two Dipper lengths, touches this group.
2. Understand their position relative to other stars:
- They are near Aldebaran (right eye of Taurus) on the shoulder of the Bull.
- They are closer to the Pole-star than Aldebaran and on the side away from the Dipper.
3. Identify their alignment with other stars:
- Nearly on a line between Beta of the Dipper (front bottom) and Capella.
4. Historical context:
- The Pleiades are associated with the Great Pyramid, as they were visible at midnight through its southward-pointing passage around 2170 B.C., when the beginning of spring coincided with their culmination.

Notes:
• The Pleiades may be considered the seven arrow wounds made by Orion.
Pleiades, navigation, astronomy, Aldebaran, Algol, Dipper, Capella, Great Pyramid, Orion

Map Compass Usage (5) -

Determine a Course Using a Sylva or High-Quality Compass and Map
High Priority Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Place your map on a flat, level surface.

2. If desired, draw a line from the objective to your starting point (this is not required).

3. Align the transparent plate of your compass along your intended path of travel so that the...
Procedure:

1. Place your map on a flat, level surface.

2. If desired, draw a line from the objective to your starting point (this is not required).

3. Align the transparent plate of your compass along your intended path of travel so that the line of sight points in the direction you want to go.

4. Rotate the compass housing until the lines on the housing are parallel to the north-south lines on the map:
- If the map lacks north-south lines, draw one crossing your path of travel.
- The north arrow on the housing will now point to true north.

5. Adjust the compass housing by rotating it clockwise or counterclockwise, depending on the declination value:
- If the declination is east, rotate clockwise by that number of degrees.
- If the declination is west, rotate counterclockwise by that number of degrees.

6. The index pointer now shows the magnetic bearing for your path of travel.

7. To follow this course:
- Rotate the entire compass (without moving the housing) until the north end of the needle aligns with the engraved arrow pointing north on the housing.
- Your direction of travel is now aligned with the line of sight or edges of the transparent plate.
course determination,compass,map,bearing,declination
Compensate for Declination When Using Map and Compass
Standard Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Understand that declination is the difference between true north (the geographic North Pole) and magnetic north (where the compass needle points). This difference can range from nearly zero degrees to twenty-five degrees east or...
Procedure:

1. Understand that declination is the difference between true north (the geographic North Pole) and magnetic north (where the compass needle points). This difference can range from nearly zero degrees to twenty-five degrees east or west depending on your location.

2. Declination must be considered when using a map with both magnetic-variance lines and grid lines based on true north.

3. Both Geodetic Survey and U.S. Coast maps show:
- Magnetic variance as dotted lines
- Grid lines (based on true north) as solid lines

4. Declination is always given in degrees east or west. This information is typically found on the map's margin or legend.

5. When using a compass and map together, apply the declination value to convert between magnetic bearings and true bearings.
declination,map,compass,navigation,true north,magnetic north
Carry a Map and Compass When Trapping
Standard Source: Student manual 2005
Know the Importance of Carrying a Map and Compass:

• It is easy to get lost if you are in unfamiliar territory.
• When looking for sign and places to make sets, you may not be paying close attention to landmarks and trails.
• Always carry a map...
Know the Importance of Carrying a Map and Compass:

• It is easy to get lost if you are in unfamiliar territory.
• When looking for sign and places to make sets, you may not be paying close attention to landmarks and trails.
• Always carry a map of the place you are trapping, and a compass.
• Many people carry a global positioning system (GPS) unit. If you do, ensure it is fully charged and functional.
map usage, compass navigation, GPS for trapping, orientation in the field
Protect and Prepare Your Map for Use in the Wilderness
Standard Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Choose a map with an appropriate scale:
- For canoe tripping, use a map with a scale of 2 miles to 1 inch.
- For hiking, use a larger-scale map, preferably one that includes contour lines for better...
Procedure:

1. Choose a map with an appropriate scale:
- For canoe tripping, use a map with a scale of 2 miles to 1 inch.
- For hiking, use a larger-scale map, preferably one that includes contour lines for better topographical detail.

2. Study the map before your trip:
- Familiarize yourself with the topographical features along your route (e.g., rivers, hills, valleys).
- Learn how to read the legend and symbols on the map.

3. Protect your map from moisture by using one of these methods:
- Cover it with a clear, self-adhesive plastic sheeting.
- Spray it with clear plastic spray for added protection.
- Use a plastic map case that allows you to fold the map and keep only the relevant sections visible.

4. Always carry your map in a secure, waterproof container or case during travel.
map protection,map preparation,wilderness navigation
Use a Compass as a Direction Finder for Short Distances
Standard Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Ensure you have a compass and know how to read it.

2. Wait until the compass needle stops moving.

3. Rotate the dial on your compass so that the north arrow on the dial aligns with the compass needle.

4. Use the bearing...
Procedure:

1. Ensure you have a compass and know how to read it.

2. Wait until the compass needle stops moving.

3. Rotate the dial on your compass so that the north arrow on the dial aligns with the compass needle.

4. Use the bearing indicated by the compass to head toward your destination.

5. This method is suitable for short distances or when the objective is large (e.g., a river, road) and you don’t need to hit it precisely.

6. If you lose sight of the objective, use the same bearing to continue in that direction.
compass,direction finder,navigation,bearing

Distance Measurement (5) -

Distance Measurement Using Pacing and Cable
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Pace counting is useful only if you know the length of your pace.

2. Convert paces to metres for accurate distance measurement.

3. Measuring distance by cable is more accurate than pacing, especially in difficult...
Procedure:

1. Pace counting is useful only if you know the length of your pace.

2. Convert paces to metres for accurate distance measurement.

3. Measuring distance by cable is more accurate than pacing, especially in difficult terrain or poor visibility conditions.

4. Use a cable (a known length of rope or chain) to measure distances when precision is required.

5. Ensure that the cable used has been pre-measured and marked for accuracy.
