- Use two pieces of paper rolled into tubes (about 10" [25 cm] long and 1" [2.5 cm] in diameter). The exact size is not critical.
2. Place one tube over your left eye and...
Procedure:

1. To view aerial photo pairs stereoscopically:
- Use two pieces of paper rolled into tubes (about 10" [25 cm] long and 1" [2.5 cm] in diameter). The exact size is not critical.
2. Place one tube over your left eye and position it a few inches above the left eye picture.
3. Place the other tube over your right eye, with its end a few inches above the right eye picture.
4. Ensure both pictures are side by side, and identical spots on each image are no more than 3" [7.5 cm] apart.
5. Look at the images with a slight effort to cross your eyes until they merge into one stereoscopic image.
6. Focus on merging the two images (e.g., bring black dots on both pictures together) to see the full three-dimensional effect.
7. Once trained, you can view aerial pairs without tubes by holding them in your vision and fusing the images instantly.
8. Be careful not to reverse left and right eye images, as this will cause hills to appear as craters and valleys as ranges.
stereoscopic viewing,aerial photos,map reading,navigation,bushcraft
Properly Hold and Interpret Aerial Photographs
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. To read an aerial photograph or pair of photographs correctly, ensure the shadows fall towards you as you look at the image.
2. If held upside down (with shadows falling away from you), hills may be misread as valleys and vice...
Procedure:

1. To read an aerial photograph or pair of photographs correctly, ensure the shadows fall towards you as you look at the image.
2. If held upside down (with shadows falling away from you), hills may be misread as valleys and vice versa.
3. For a single aerial photo:
- Ensure it is viewed with shadows falling towards you to correctly interpret terrain features such as hills, valleys, and land use.
4. Aerial photographs can provide more detailed information than maps but require special skills to read accurately.
5. Experts can identify features like ploughed land, forest land, or grass country by analyzing the texture of the earth's surface in the photograph.
aerial photos,map reading,navigation,bushcraft,terrain interpretation

Map Acquisition (2) -

How to Locate Topographical Maps for National Forests in the United States
Reference Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Determine which national forest you are interested in exploring.
2. Contact one of the U.S. Forest Service Regional Centers, located in the following cities:
- Atlanta, Georgia
- Milwaukee, Wisconsin
-...
Procedure:

1. Determine which national forest you are interested in exploring.
2. Contact one of the U.S. Forest Service Regional Centers, located in the following cities:
- Atlanta, Georgia
- Milwaukee, Wisconsin
- Albuquerque, New Mexico
- Denver, Colorado
- Ogden, Utah
- Missoula, Montana
- San Francisco, California
- Portland, Oregon
- Juneau, Alaska
3. Request topographical maps for the specific national forest area you want to explore.
4. Purchase the maps directly from the regional center if available.

Notes:
• These maps are useful for planning outdoor activities such as hiking, camping, and wildlife observation.
• Maps may also be available online or through other local outdoor recreation offices.
national forest maps, USFS map acquisition, navigation tools
How to Obtain Topographical Maps in the United States
Reference Source: The Complete Outdoorsmans Handbook
Procedure:

1. Determine the location you are interested in mapping.
2. Contact the appropriate U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) office:
- For states east of the Mississippi River: Map Information Office, U.S. Geological Survey,...
Procedure:

1. Determine the location you are interested in mapping.
2. Contact the appropriate U.S. Geological Survey (USGS) office:
- For states east of the Mississippi River: Map Information Office, U.S. Geological Survey, Washington, D.C. 20244.
- For states west of the Mississippi River: Map Information Office, U.S. Geological Survey, Federal Center, Denver, Colorado 80225.
3. Request an index sheet for the state or province you are interested in. This will show what maps are available for that area.
4. Follow instructions on the index sheet to order individual maps for the specific area you want to explore.
5. Allow approximately four weeks for delivery if ordering by mail, so plan your trip early and order well ahead of time.

Notes:
• Topographical maps are relatively inexpensive.
• Maps for national parks can be purchased over-the-counter at all park headquarters.
• In Canada, topo maps can be obtained from the Map Distribution Office, Department of Mines and Technical Service, Ottawa, Ontario.
topographical maps, obtaining maps, USGS, map acquisition, navigation tools

Route Navigation (2) -

Detour Around an Obstacle Using 90-Degree Offset Method
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Stop the pace count you have been making for movement along your assigned route.
2. Decide which side of the obstacle to detour around (e.g., right flank).
3. Add 90 degrees to your original azimuth (e.g., if your original azimuth...
Procedure:

1. Stop the pace count you have been making for movement along your assigned route.
2. Decide which side of the obstacle to detour around (e.g., right flank).
3. Add 90 degrees to your original azimuth (e.g., if your original azimuth was 360 degrees, new direction is 90 degrees) and move on this new azimuth until you can clear the obstacle.
4. Start a new pace count from the point where you made the 90-degree turn to the point where you can clear the obstacle.
5. Change direction by subtracting 90 degrees from your current azimuth (e.g., 90 degrees - 90 = 0 degrees) and move parallel to your original course along the right flank of the obstacle.
6. Continue moving on this new azimuth until you have bypassed the obstacle, and pick up the pace count you were making before the detour.
7. Make another change in direction by subtracting 90 degrees from your current azimuth (e.g., 0 degrees - 90 = 270 degrees) to move back toward your original line of march.
8. Move on this new azimuth for the same number of paces as you made off the original line of march.
9. Once back on your original line of march, make a final change in direction by adding 90 degrees to your current azimuth (e.g., 270 + 90 = 360) to realign with your original route.
10. Resume your original pace count and continue following the original azimuth.
detour, obstacle navigation, 90-degree offset, wilderness navigation, route realignment
Detour Around an Obstacle Using Nearside Landmark Method
Standard Source: US Marine Corps Wilderness Medicine
Procedure:

1. Identify a feature on the nearside of the obstacle that lies on your line of march and is visible from both sides.
2. Move off your original route to navigate around the obstacle.
3. From the far side, determine the back azimuth...
Procedure:

1. Identify a feature on the nearside of the obstacle that lies on your line of march and is visible from both sides.
2. Move off your original route to navigate around the obstacle.
3. From the far side, determine the back azimuth of your original route (e.g., if your original azimuth was 198 degrees, the back azimuth would be 18 degrees).
4. Shoot an azimuth back toward the nearside landmark and move until it lies at the calculated back azimuth.
5. Once aligned with the nearside landmark, continue following your original azimuth of 198 degrees.
detour, obstacle navigation, nearside landmark, wilderness navigation, back azimuth

Winter Navigation (2) -

Navigate in Winter Conditions with Limited Visibility
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Be aware that long nights, fog, snowfall, blizzards, and drifting snow will drastically limit visibility.
2. In extreme cases, a white-out condition may occur where the sky and ground are both overcast and snow-covered, making it...
Procedure:

1. Be aware that long nights, fog, snowfall, blizzards, and drifting snow will drastically limit visibility.
2. In extreme cases, a white-out condition may occur where the sky and ground are both overcast and snow-covered, making it difficult to recognize ground features.
3. Heavy snow can completely blot out tracks, trails, lakes, and other landmarks.
4. Pay special attention to identifying landmarks on the ground and in air photos because the landscape looks different in winter.
5. In areas with limited large-scale maps, rely more heavily on air photos for navigation.
visibility, winter navigation, landmarks, white-out, air photos
Use Appropriate Navigation Methods in Winter Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. Use the following methods of land navigation under winter conditions:
- Map reading (useful when visibility is good).
- Map and compass combined (essential when landmarks are scarce or visibility is limited).
-...
Procedure:

1. Use the following methods of land navigation under winter conditions:
- Map reading (useful when visibility is good).
- Map and compass combined (essential when landmarks are scarce or visibility is limited).
- Dead reckoning (used when there are no adequate landmarks).
2. In the Canadian Arctic, magnetic compasses may be unreliable due to magnetic disturbances.
3. Use an astro compass to calibrate the magnetic compass and determine true direction by sighting on a celestial body.
4. Refer to Annex C for detailed information on using the astro compass.
map reading, compass navigation, dead reckoning, astro compass, magnetic disturbances

Snow Travel (2) -

Downhill Skiing Technique
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Technique for downhill skiing:

1. Keep your knees bent and flexible to act as shock absorbers.
2. Bend your knees at the crests of hummocks and the lips of depressions.
3. Straighten your knees while skiing in the hollows.
4. Keep one ski well...
Technique for downhill skiing:

1. Keep your knees bent and flexible to act as shock absorbers.
2. Bend your knees at the crests of hummocks and the lips of depressions.
3. Straighten your knees while skiing in the hollows.
4. Keep one ski well ahead (approximately two feet) during descent.
downhill skiing,skiing technique,knee position,snow travel
Navigating Uneven Snow Terrain
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Technique for navigating uneven snow terrain:

1. Identify small hummocks and depressions on the surface of the snow.
2. When encountering a small depression, make a lunge from the bottom of the depression.
3. Slide the other ski over the crest...
Technique for navigating uneven snow terrain:

1. Identify small hummocks and depressions on the surface of the snow.
2. When encountering a small depression, make a lunge from the bottom of the depression.
3. Slide the other ski over the crest of the next hummock, keeping the knee of the sliding leg well bent.
4. Reach with one ski pole over the next hummock to provide an effective thrust once on the hummock.
5. If the depression is deep (four feet or more), place one ski about two feet ahead of the other as you ski through the depression.
6. Keep most of your weight on your rear foot, keep your body straight, and bend your knees while sliding over each hummock.
7. Straighten your knees while sliding through the depression.
uneven terrain,snow navigation,skiing technique,hummocks,depressions

Extreme Weather Navigation (2) -

Navigate and Operate During Grey-Out Conditions
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure:

1. **Identify grey-out conditions:
- Look for an overall greyness in the environment during twilight or when the sun is near the horizon.
- Note that the sky may be overcast with dense clouds, leading to a loss of shadows and...
Procedure:

1. **Identify grey-out conditions:
- Look for an overall greyness in the environment during twilight or when the sun is near the horizon.
- Note that the sky may be overcast with dense clouds, leading to a loss of shadows and depth perception.

2. **Assess impact on operations:
- Grey-outs make it difficult to distinguish between the road and ditches, increasing the risk of accidents.
- Pilots, drivers, skiers, or walkers may experience disorientation due to reduced visibility.
- Fatigue exacerbates the effects of grey-out conditions.

3. **Implement mitigation measures:
- Use vehicle lights to improve visibility during movement.
- Maintain a clear understanding of the terrain and use the horizon as a reference point for direction.
- Avoid driving in fatigued states, especially under grey-out conditions.

4. **Train personnel:
- Educate soldiers and operators on how to recognize the signs of a grey-out (e.g., greyness during twilight, lack of shadows).
- Practice navigation techniques that do not rely solely on visual cues.
grey-out, navigation, cold weather, military operations, subarctic conditions
Navigating Fall Freeze-Up Conditions in Northern Areas
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Procedure for Navigating Fall Freeze-Up Conditions in Northern Areas:

1. Assess terrain conditions during fall freeze-up:
- Be aware that fall rains can create deep mud on unpaved roads, which will freeze overnight, making movement...
Procedure for Navigating Fall Freeze-Up Conditions in Northern Areas:

1. Assess terrain conditions during fall freeze-up:
- Be aware that fall rains can create deep mud on unpaved roads, which will freeze overnight, making movement difficult.
- Avoid areas with small steep-banked ditches and streams hidden by leaves or tall grasses.
- Watch for warm water springs covered in snow, as they create thin ice that poses a hazard.

2. Plan routes carefully:
- Use maps for route planning during the first three weeks of fall when conditions are most difficult.
- Conduct reconnaissance using helicopters and confirm with foot patrols to identify safe paths.

3. Use willow mats along streams and swamps:
- These areas generally support tracked vehicles and should be followed where possible.
- Be cautious of leaf cover on willows, which can obscure ground observations.

4. Avoid following vehicles too closely:
- This prevents the rear vehicles from turning out to bypass a mired vehicle.

5. Use foot reconnaissance by crew members:
- This helps identify potential hazards and safe routes.

6. Utilize gravel ridges, shallow lakes, and stream beds:
- These areas normally provide better mobility conditions during fall freeze-up.

7. Act quickly if a vehicle becomes mired:
- Recovery must be immediate to prevent the vehicle from freezing in place.
- Crews may need to agitate water around the vehicle to prevent it from freezing further.
fall freeze-up, northern areas, military movement, unpaved roads, willow mats, mired vehicles, recovery, gravel ridges, shallow lakes, stream beds

Water Navigation (2) -

Conduct Operations in Northern Inland Waterways with Environmental Limitations
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Operations will be influenced by the following factors peculiar to northern operations:

• a. The long hours of daylight during the summer months in northern areas allow operations to be easily conducted 24 hours a day. This also means the...
Operations will be influenced by the following factors peculiar to northern operations:

• a. The long hours of daylight during the summer months in northern areas allow operations to be easily conducted 24 hours a day. This also means the operations can be easily detected from the air.
• b. With few exceptions, major population centres are non-existent along the navigable or tributary rivers in northern areas.
• c. Weather is unpredictable and can change rapidly.
• d. Maintenance requirements are greater, especially on boat motors.
• e. Flooding may submerge or sweep away landing sites. During flooding periods the current may increase, making the river unnavigable.
• f. Low water may reduce channel depth below minimum requirements, and reduce or eliminate temporarily the usefulness of the waterways as a means of transportation.
• g. Land and river navigation is important because of the difficulties caused by:
- (1) declination and fluctuation of magnetic field,
- (2) lack of identifiable landmarks, and
- (3) lack of detail on most maps.
northern operations,navigation challenges,flooding,low water,magnetic field fluctuations
Navigate Northern Inland Waterways with Braided Channels
Standard Source: Canadian Military Basic Cold Weather Training
Northern inland waterways are characterized by the following:

• a. Many channels (braided). The main river channel may be changed significantly from year to year.
• b. Water is normally filled with silt, sand, and debris.
• c. Current...
Northern inland waterways are characterized by the following:

• a. Many channels (braided). The main river channel may be changed significantly from year to year.
• b. Water is normally filled with silt, sand, and debris.
• c. Current is normally swift 15 to 19 kmph (8 to 10 knots).
• d. Rivers and streams are shallow (61 to 305 cm (2 to 10 feet)). In glacier fed streams the depth of water may double between early morning and noon. This is because of increased melting of the glacier caused by warmer daytime temperature.
• e. The rivers and streams may have many sandbars.
northern waterways,braided channels,river navigation,current speed,silt,sandbars

Binocular Use (2) -

Systematic Use of Binoculars in Open Country
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure for using binoculars systematically in open country:

1. Cover sector systematically: When using binoculars in open country, the sector should be covered systematically.
2. Examine entire field of view: The eye examines the...
Procedure for using binoculars systematically in open country:

1. Cover sector systematically: When using binoculars in open country, the sector should be covered systematically.
2. Examine entire field of view: The eye examines the whole of each field of view, both horizontally and vertically.
binoculars,navigation,open country,systematic scanning
Use of Binoculars in Close Country
Standard Source: Canadian Military Fieldcraft
Procedure for using binoculars in close country:

1. Examine suspicious objects and areas: In close country where the naked eye alone is inadequate, binoculars are used to examine suspicious objects, areas of good cover, hedges, ground seen...
Procedure for using binoculars in close country:

1. Examine suspicious objects and areas: In close country where the naked eye alone is inadequate, binoculars are used to examine suspicious objects, areas of good cover, hedges, ground seen through trees, and distant areas.
close country,navigation,binoculars,suspicious objects

Map Security (2) -

Protect Map Coordinates and Locations Using Authorized Numerical Codes
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Use an authorized low-level numerical code to express locations on maps to prevent the enemy from intercepting and interpreting messages.
2. Refer to Army Regulation 380-40 for procedures on obtaining authorized codes.
3. The...
Procedure:

1. Use an authorized low-level numerical code to express locations on maps to prevent the enemy from intercepting and interpreting messages.
2. Refer to Army Regulation 380-40 for procedures on obtaining authorized codes.
3. The authorized numerical code allows encryption of map references and other numerical information when operational reasons require transmitting the remainder of a message in plain language.
4. These codes are available in easy-to-use booklets with sufficient material for one month’s operation.
5. Sample training editions can be obtained through the unit's communications and electronics officer.
6. Do not use any encryption methods other than authorized codes, as this is unauthorized by regulation.
map security,map coordinates,authorized code,encryption,Army Regulation 380-40
Secure Military Maps to Prevent Unauthorized Access or Damage
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Military maps contain sensitive information that must be protected from unauthorized access, damage, or loss.

Steps:
1. Store maps in a secure location such as a locked cabinet or safe.
2. Limit access to maps only to authorized personnel who...
Military maps contain sensitive information that must be protected from unauthorized access, damage, or loss.

Steps:
1. Store maps in a secure location such as a locked cabinet or safe.
2. Limit access to maps only to authorized personnel who have a legitimate need for them.
3. Use tamper-evident seals on map containers to detect any unauthorized opening.
4. Regularly inspect storage areas to ensure that maps are not damaged or missing.
5. Implement digital security measures if using electronic versions of maps, such as encryption and access controls.
map security,military maps,map protection

Combination Methods (2) -

Use Combination Navigation Techniques
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
To use combination navigation techniques:

1. Integrate multiple methods:
- Combine terrain association with compass readings or GPS data to improve accuracy and reliability.
- Use dead reckoning in conjunction with stabilized turret...
To use combination navigation techniques:

1. Integrate multiple methods:
- Combine terrain association with compass readings or GPS data to improve accuracy and reliability.
- Use dead reckoning in conjunction with stabilized turret alignment for more precise navigation in challenging environments.

2. Cross-reference information:
- Compare your position using different techniques such as map reading, compass headings, and GPS coordinates to ensure consistency.
- If discrepancies arise between methods, use the most reliable source of information (e.g., GPS or a well-defined landmark) to adjust your course.

3. Maintain situational awareness:
- Continuously monitor your surroundings for changes in terrain, weather, or visibility that could affect navigation accuracy.
- Use landmarks and natural features as visual checkpoints to confirm your location and direction of travel.

4. Adjust for environmental factors:
- Account for wind, slope, and obstacles that may impact your movement or the performance of your navigation equipment.
- Recalculate your position periodically based on the time elapsed and distance traveled since your last checkpoint.
method integration, cross-reference, environmental factors, situational awareness
Combine Dead Reckoning and Terrain Association for Navigation
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Use dead reckoning to travel across large, flat areas where it is accurate and precise.
2. Transition to terrain association once reaching a ridge or other recognizable feature that allows for easier navigation using natural...
Procedure:

1. Use dead reckoning to travel across large, flat areas where it is accurate and precise.
2. Transition to terrain association once reaching a ridge or other recognizable feature that allows for easier navigation using natural landmarks.
3. Combine both methods as needed depending on the terrain and conditions.

Details:
• Terrain association is fast, error-tolerant, and can be used day or night with proficiency.
• Dead reckoning requires precision but works well on flat terrain.
• Common errors to avoid include:
- Failure to determine distances to travel.
- Failure to travel the proper distance.
- Failure to properly plot or locate objectives.
- Failure to select easily recognized checkpoints or landmarks.
- Failure to consider ease of movement factors.

Recommendation:
• Be proficient in both methods and use them in combination as needed for effective navigation.
terrain association, dead reckoning, combined navigation, error prevention

Night Navigation (2) -

Using AN/PVS-7B/D Night Vision Goggles for Land Navigation
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Prepare the device: Ensure the AN/PVS-7B/D is fully charged and functioning properly.
2. Use ambient light conditions: The device relies on ambient light, so it should be used in low-light or limited visibility conditions...
Procedure:

1. Prepare the device: Ensure the AN/PVS-7B/D is fully charged and functioning properly.
2. Use ambient light conditions: The device relies on ambient light, so it should be used in low-light or limited visibility conditions (e.g., moonlight or starlight).
3. Place chemical lights: Position chemical light sources at selected intervals along your unit’s route of movement.
4. Observe using night vision goggles: Use the AN/PVS-7B/D to observe the chemical lights placed along the route, allowing for navigation under limited visibility conditions.
5. Assign roles: Have one person read the map and another person read the terrain, both using AN/PVS-7B/Ds.
6. Exchange information: The map reader and terrain interpreter should exchange observations between the map and actual terrain.
7. Focus on tasks: Each user should concentrate the AN/PVS-7B/D on one task (either map interpretation or terrain observation).
8. Use in mounted navigation: When navigating while mounted, have one soldier interpret the map in the cargo area of the vehicle, while another (e.g., the driver) relays information about observed terrain to the map reader.

Notes:
• The AN/PVS-7B/D has a field of view of 40 meters and a range of 300 meters in moonlight and 150 meters in starlight.
• It is lightweight (1.5 pounds) and functions similarly to the AN/PVS-5/5A.
• This technique is particularly useful in low-light or limited visibility conditions.
AN/PVS-7B/D, night vision goggles, land navigation, chemical lights, map reading, terrain observation
Use Night Navigation Techniques
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
To use night navigation techniques:

1. Prepare your equipment:
- Ensure that all navigation tools such as compasses, GPS devices, and headlamps are fully charged or have sufficient power for the duration of travel.
- Use red light filters...
To use night navigation techniques:

1. Prepare your equipment:
- Ensure that all navigation tools such as compasses, GPS devices, and headlamps are fully charged or have sufficient power for the duration of travel.
- Use red light filters on headlamps to preserve night vision and avoid disorienting yourself with bright white light.

2. Use star navigation:
- Identify key constellations such as the North Star (Polaris) to determine your direction of travel in the Northern Hemisphere.
- Use a star chart or app to help identify stars and their positions relative to your location.

3. Maintain situational awareness:
- Stay alert to changes in terrain, weather, and visibility that could affect navigation accuracy.
- Use landmarks such as rivers, ridges, or roads for orientation if they are visible under moonlight or artificial light sources.

4. Use GPS and compasses:
- Rely on GPS devices for precise location tracking and route guidance in low-light conditions.
- Use a compass to maintain a consistent heading when GPS signals are weak or unavailable.

5. Mark your path:
- Place markers such as reflective tape, glow sticks, or small flags along the trail to help identify your route during night travel.
- Record your position periodically using GPS or map coordinates for reference if you become disoriented.
star navigation, red light filters, GPS usage, path marking

Tactical Navigation (2) -

Maintain Concealment in Desert Environments
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Alter vehicle shapes: Modify the standard shapes of vehicles to avoid instant recognition by enemy observers.
2. Eliminate shine and glitter: Cover reflective surfaces or use matte finishes to prevent glare from attracting...
Procedure:

1. Alter vehicle shapes: Modify the standard shapes of vehicles to avoid instant recognition by enemy observers.
2. Eliminate shine and glitter: Cover reflective surfaces or use matte finishes to prevent glare from attracting attention.
3. Blend equipment with terrain: Paint or mud all equipment to match the desert color and texture for better concealment.
4. Control light and noise discipline: Avoid unnecessary light sources or noise that can be detected over long distances in the desert.
5. Shield heat sources: Use thermal camouflage or other methods to prevent detection by modern heat imaging technology, especially during night operations.
6. Minimize movement and dust: Move quickly and efficiently to reduce noise and dust, which can reveal your position to the enemy.

Notes:
• Concealment in the desert is critical due to the open terrain and long-range visibility.
• All measures must be taken to avoid detection by both visual and technological means.
concealment,desert navigation,tactical movement
Using the AN/PSN-11 for Tactical Missions
High Priority Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
Procedure for Using the AN/PSN-11 in Tactical Operations:

The data provided by the AN/PVS-11 helps complete various tactical missions, including:

• Siting.
• Surveying.
• Tactical reconnaissance.
• Sensor emplacement.
-...
Procedure for Using the AN/PSN-11 in Tactical Operations:

The data provided by the AN/PVS-11 helps complete various tactical missions, including:

• Siting.
• Surveying.
• Tactical reconnaissance.
• Sensor emplacement.
• Artillery forward observing.
• Close air support.
• General navigation.
• Mechanized maneuvers.
• Engineer surveying.
• Amphibious operations.
• Parachute operations.
• Signal intelligence.
• Electronic warfare.
• Ground-based forward air control.

The data is displayed on the AN/PSN-11 display and can also be accessed from a serial data port.
AN/PSN-11, tactical missions, navigation device, military operations

Map Printing (2) -

Using QRMP for Map and Graphic Reproduction
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
The Quick Response Multicolor Printer (QRMP) is a self-contained, laser xerography printer capable of reproducing maps, photographs, annotated graphics, transparent originals, and digital terrain data in full color on transparent material or...
The Quick Response Multicolor Printer (QRMP) is a self-contained, laser xerography printer capable of reproducing maps, photographs, annotated graphics, transparent originals, and digital terrain data in full color on transparent material or standard map paper.

System Components:
• QRMP housed in an 8-by-8-by-20-foot ISO shelter mounted on a 5-ton truck with a dedicated military-standard 30-kilowatt generator
• Each system carries at least a seven-day supply of all necessary materials

Capabilities:
• Map size: 24-by-30-inch paper size and 22.5-by-29-inch image size
• Full color printing on transparent material or standard map paper
• Produces the first copy in less than five minutes in full color
• Sustains a copy rate of 50 to 100 copies per hour for full color products
• Uses a charged couple device array for scanning and sophisticated electronic signal processing to electrostatically discharge a selenium photoreceptor drum
• Can print terrain and other graphics directly from digital output from the digital topographic support system or another QRMP
QRMP,map printing,graphic reproduction,navigation
Operate the Quick Response Multicolor Printer (QRMP)
Reference Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
The QRMP is a self-contained, laser xerography printer capable of reproducing maps, photographs, annotated graphics, transparent originals, and digital terrain data in full color on transparent material or standard map paper.

**System...
The QRMP is a self-contained, laser xerography printer capable of reproducing maps, photographs, annotated graphics, transparent originals, and digital terrain data in full color on transparent material or standard map paper.

System Components:

• The QRMP system consists of:
- A QRMP housed in an 8-by-8-by-20-foot ISO shelter mounted on a 5-ton truck.
- A dedicated military-standard 30-kilowatt generator.

Capabilities and Specifications:

• Produces the first copy in less than five minutes in full color.
• Sustains a copy rate of 50 to 100 copies per hour for full-color products.
• Uses a charged couple device array for scanning.
• Utilizes sophisticated electronic signal processing to electrostatically discharge a selenium photoreceptor drum.

Operational Features:

• Map size: 24-by-30-inch paper size and 22.5-by-29-inch image size.
• Capable of printing terrain and other graphics directly from digital output from the digital topographic support system or another QRMP.
• Each system carries at least a seven-day supply of all necessary materials.
QRMP,map printing,terrain data,digital topographic support

Device Operations (2) -

Perform Operational Checkout Procedures on DAGR
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading and Land Navigation
External Body Inspection:

1. Inspect the DAGR and external cables and equipment for damaged or missing parts.

Power Up Procedure:

1. Push the POWER key to turn the DAGR on.
2. Ensure the DAGR has a clear view of the sky.
3. During power...
External Body Inspection:

1. Inspect the DAGR and external cables and equipment for damaged or missing parts.

Power Up Procedure:

1. Push the POWER key to turn the DAGR on.
2. Ensure the DAGR has a clear view of the sky.
3. During power up, observe the power-on status display.
4. Confirm that the DAGR passes self-tests and the battery strength indicator shows sufficient battery power remaining.
5. Do not use the DAGR if a failed self-test is indicated.

NOTE: A Test Summary page can be accessed from the System submenu for a listing of tests that passed or failed. Do not use the DAGR if the Status field shows FAILED.

Operator-Induced Commanded Self-Test Procedure:

1. After the DAGR successfully completes the power-on self-test and shows the SV Sky View page or Present Position page, perform the following procedure:

a. Activate commanded DAGR self-test.

b. If the Present Position page is not already displayed, push and hold the POS key (except when showing a message pop-up, then push the QUIT key first). The Present Position page is displayed. Push the MENU key.

c. Highlight Select Op Mode, then push the ENTER key.

d. Highlight Test, then push the ENTER key.

e. The DAGR displays an ENTER TEST MODE message prompting the operator to confirm or cancel entering test mode. Push ENTER key to confirm.

f. A Test In Progress display appears with the specific area of testing listed at the bottom and a bar graph denoting progress. The DAGR automatically tests multiple areas.

NOTE: While performing the following keypad test, push and hold the ENTER key to test the ENTER key. Push and release the ENTER key to advance to the next display.

g. After the displayed tests are completed, Keypad Test is displayed. Push each key on the keypad and verify the corresponding key shown on the display toggles between normal and highlighted appearance. Push the ENTER key to continue to the next display.

h. The Display Light Test display appears with the brightness adjustment cycling between 0% and 100%. The percentage adjustment is reflected in the light bulb of the display. Push the ENTER key to continue.

i. The Contrast Test display appears with the contrast adjustment cycling between 0% and 100%. The percentage adjustment is reflected in the bar graph of the display. Push the ENTER key to continue.

j. The Display Test Beginning message appears momentarily. After sequencing through white, light gray, dark gray, and black, the Display Test Completed message appears, followed by the Power-On Status display listing self-test results as Pass or Fail.

NOTE: A Test Summary page can be accessed from the System submenu for a listing of tests that passed or failed. Do not use the DAGR if the Status field shows FAILED.

k. If the Power-On Status remains displayed and does not time out, push the ENTER key to acknowledge.

l. The SV (satellite vehicle) Sky View page is displayed. Push the MENU key.

m. Highlight Select Op Mode, then push the ENTER key.

n. Highlight Continuous, then push the ENTER key. This mode enables the DAGR to acquire a current position fix.

o. After satellites are acquired and a current position fix is obtained, the DAGR display stops blinking and Tracking SVs is shown on the SV Sky View page. The display then automatically switches to the Present Position page.

NOTE: If the DAGR does not acquire satellites, the display blinks between black and gray text and goes into Standby mode (both handheld and host platform operation). If the DAGR display continues to blink, verify a clear view of the sky, then perform the manual initialization procedure.
DAGR operational checkout, DAGR self-test, navigation device maintenance, satellite vehicle (SV) test, GPS unit troubleshooting
Perform Operator-Induced Commanded Self-Test on DAGR
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Objective: Conduct a commanded self-test to verify the functionality of the DAGR after initial power-on.

Procedure:
1. Activate Commanded Self-Test:
- Ensure the DAGR has successfully completed the power-on self-test and is...
Objective: Conduct a commanded self-test to verify the functionality of the DAGR after initial power-on.

Procedure:
1. Activate Commanded Self-Test:
- Ensure the DAGR has successfully completed the power-on self-test and is displaying either the SV Sky View page or Present Position page.
2. Access Test Mode:
- If the Present Position page is not displayed, push and hold the POS key (except when showing a message pop-up; in that case, first push the QUIT key).
- Push the MENU key to access the menu.
3. Select Op Mode for Testing:
- Highlight Select Op Mode, then push the ENTER key.
4. Choose Test Option:
- Highlight Test, then push the ENTER key.
5. Confirm Entry into Test Mode:
- The DAGR displays an ENTER TEST MODE message prompting the operator to confirm or cancel entering test mode.
- Push the ENTER key to confirm entry into test mode.
6. Monitor Self-Test Progress:
- A Test In Progress display appears with the specific area of testing listed at the bottom and a bar graph denoting progress.
- The DAGR automatically tests multiple areas.
7. Conduct Keypad Test:
- Push and hold the ENTER key to test the ENTER key (it should toggle between normal and highlighted appearance on the display).
- Release the ENTER key to advance to the next display.
8. Perform Display Light Test:
- The brightness adjustment cycles between 0% and 100%, reflected in the light bulb of the display.
- Push the ENTER key to continue.
9. Conduct Contrast Test:
- The contrast adjustment cycles between 0% and 100%, reflected in the bar graph of the display.
- Push the ENTER key to continue.
10. Complete Display Test:
- A Display Test Beginning message appears momentarily, followed by a sequence through white, light gray, dark gray, and black on the screen.
- After completion, a Display Test Completed message is displayed, followed by the Power-On Status display listing self-test results as Pass or Fail.
11. Acknowledge Results:
- If the Power-On Status remains displayed and does not time out, push the ENTER key to acknowledge.
12. Return to Normal Operation Mode:
- The SV Sky View page is displayed. Push the MENU key.
- Highlight Select Op Mode, then push the ENTER key.
- Highlight Continuous, then push the ENTER key to enable the DAGR to acquire a current position fix.
13. Verify Satellite Acquisition:
- After satellites are acquired and a current position fix is obtained, the display stops blinking and shows Tracking SVs on the SV Sky View page.
- The display automatically switches to the Present Position page.

Notes:
• If the DAGR does not acquire satellites, the display blinks between black and gray text and goes into Standby mode (both handheld and host platform operation).
• If the display continues to blink, verify a clear view of the sky and perform the manual initialization procedure.
DAGR self-test procedure, navigation device testing, satellite unit diagnostics

Desert Navigation (2) -

Identify Desert Terrain Characteristics
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Recognize desert terrain types: Identify the three main types of deserts:
- Mountain deserts: Characterized by scattered ranges or areas of barren hills or mountains.
- Rocky plateau deserts: Feature flat to uneven...
Procedure:

1. Recognize desert terrain types: Identify the three main types of deserts:
- Mountain deserts: Characterized by scattered ranges or areas of barren hills or mountains.
- Rocky plateau deserts: Feature flat to uneven terrain with minimal vegetation and sparse rainfall.
- Sandy or dune deserts: Composed primarily of sand dunes, often in vast, open expanses.

2. Understand environmental conditions: Note that deserts are defined by low annual rainfall and extreme temperatures, both hot during the day and cold at night.

3. Assess terrain variability: Be aware that desert regions can vary from nearly flat to lava beds or salt marshes, which may impact movement and navigation.

4. Use maps for reference: Consult maps such as Figure 13-1 in FM 3-25.26 to identify major desert regions and their locations globally.
desert terrain, navigation, map reading, desert regions, military operations
Identify Major Desert Regions and Locations
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Procedure:

1. Refer to Table 13-1: Use the table provided in FM 3-25.26 (page 13-2) to identify major desert regions and their locations.

2. List of major desert regions: Note the following:
- Sahara: Located in North Africa.
...
Procedure:

1. Refer to Table 13-1: Use the table provided in FM 3-25.26 (page 13-2) to identify major desert regions and their locations.

2. List of major desert regions: Note the following:
- Sahara: Located in North Africa.
- Kalahari: Found in Southwest Africa.
- Arabian: Situated in Southwest Asia.
- Gobi: Located in Mongolia and Northern China.
- Rub’al Khali: Found in South Arabia.
- Great Basin, Colorado, Chichuahua, Yuma Sonoran, and Mohave: Spanning Northern Mexico and the Western United States.
- Takla Makan: Located in Northern China.
- Kyzyl Kum: Situated in Southwest USSR (now Central Asia).
- Kara Kum: Found in Southwest USSR (now Central Asia).
- Syrian: Located in Saudi Arabia, Jordan, and Iraq.
- Great Victoria: Found in Western and South Australia.
- Great Sandy: Located in Northwestern Australia.
- Patagonia: Situated in Southern Argentina and Chile.
- Atacama: Found in Northern Chile.

3. Use for planning: Use this information to plan operations or travel routes that may involve desert environments.
major deserts, desert locations, map reading, military navigation

Land Navigation Training (2) -

Set Up a Sustainment Program for Land Navigation Training
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
### Purpose
The purpose of setting up a sustainment program is to provide Soldiers with recurring training that reinforces and builds on the land navigation skills they have received in institutional training. All Soldiers should receive this...
### Purpose
The purpose of setting up a sustainment program is to provide Soldiers with recurring training that reinforces and builds on the land navigation skills they have received in institutional training. All Soldiers should receive this training at least twice a year. The program also helps identify areas where additional training may be needed.

### Training Guidance
1. Determine Proficiency Levels: The unit commander must assess the proficiency levels and problems within the unit through:
- After-action reports from rotations to NTC/JRTC
- ARTEP final reports
- Feedback from subordinates
- Personal observation
- Annual training results
2. Once the commander identifies areas needing improvement, he should issue training guidance to his subordinate leaders.
3. The unit staff must provide:
- Training sites
- Resources
- Time for land navigation training
4. It is recommended that land navigation be trained separately and not just as a subtask in tactical training.

### Certification
1. The commander must establish a means of certifying training for subordinate commanders.
2. Subject matter experts should be provided by the unit staff to ensure training meets established standards.
3. Instructors should be certified to teach, and courses should be certified before being used by the unit.

### Program Development
1. The sustainment program must meet the needs of all Soldiers in the unit, covering:
- Diagnostic examination
- Map reading instruction/review
- Land navigation skills training
- Dead reckoning training/practice
- Terrain association training/practice
- Land navigation written/field examination
- Leaders’ training and testing
2. The program should be developed in training modules that can be used as a whole or individually.
3. Modules should be designed to allow the commander flexibility based on unit proficiency levels.
4. The sustainment program must be documented and maintained in the unit’s training files.
sustainment,program,training,land navigation,map reading,certification,module,diagnostic examination
Set Up a Train-the-Trainer Program for Land Navigation
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
### Purpose
The purpose of the train-the-trainer program is to develop trainers who can provide Soldiers with the confidence and skills needed to accomplish all assigned land navigation tasks.

### Development of the Program
1. The unit commander...
### Purpose
The purpose of the train-the-trainer program is to develop trainers who can provide Soldiers with the confidence and skills needed to accomplish all assigned land navigation tasks.

### Development of the Program
1. The unit commander should appoint a cadre of officers and NCOs as primary and alternate instructors for land navigation training.
2. Use the unit’s existing training modules to train these individuals until they demonstrate expertise in each module.
3. Determine which instructors will conduct specific training modules and ensure they are fully prepared to deliver instruction.
4. These instructors act as a training cadre for the entire unit, responsible for:
- Training their peers
- Instructing subordinate units
- Certifying the quality of training within each unit

### Conduct of Training
1. Conduct land navigation training at the lowest possible level, ensuring that all leaders are included in training to maintain unit integrity.
2. The cadre should ensure that training is consistent with established standards and that all Soldiers receive instruction from certified instructors.
train-the-trainer,program,leadership,cadre,instructor certification,module training

Weather Navigation (2) -

Adjust Route Based on Weather Conditions for Vehicle Movement
Standard Source: STP 21 1 Army Warrior Skills Level 1 Soldiers Manual of Common Tasks
Procedure:

1. Assess weather impact on vehicle movement:
- Snow and ice can halt mounted movement.
- Rain and snow reduce soil load-bearing ability.
- Heavy rain may restrict cross-country vehicles to road movement only.

2....
Procedure:

1. Assess weather impact on vehicle movement:
- Snow and ice can halt mounted movement.
- Rain and snow reduce soil load-bearing ability.
- Heavy rain may restrict cross-country vehicles to road movement only.

2. Adjust the route accordingly:
- Avoid flooded or muddy areas to prevent the vehicle from getting stuck, which hinders combat capability.

3. Select alternate routes if necessary:
- If weather conditions make a planned route impassable, choose an alternative that minimizes risk and maintains mobility.
weather effects on vehicles, route adjustment, vehicle movement, navigation in bad weather
Interpret Isobars on Weather Maps
Reference Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Procedure for Interpreting Isobars on Weather Maps:

1. Understand isobar definition: Isobars are lines on a weather map that connect points of equal atmospheric pressure.
2. Recognize contour-like patterns: Isobars resemble **contour...
Procedure for Interpreting Isobars on Weather Maps:

1. Understand isobar definition: Isobars are lines on a weather map that connect points of equal atmospheric pressure.
2. Recognize contour-like patterns: Isobars resemble contour lines, similar to those found on topographic maps.
3. Identify high-pressure areas: High-pressure regions on a weather map are referred to as ridges and are associated with stable, calm conditions.
4. Identify low-pressure areas: Low-pressure regions on a weather map are referred to as troughs and are associated with unstable, stormy conditions.
5. Measure pressure units: Isobars can be measured in either millibars (mb) or inches of mercury (hg).
isobars, weather maps, ridges, troughs, pressure units

Stream Crossing (2) -

Assess and Plan for Mountain Stream Crossings
High Priority Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
Objective: Evaluate the feasibility of crossing a mountain stream or river safely.

Procedure:
1. Treat all rivers and streams as danger areas due to their potential strength and unpredictability.
2. Avoid crossing whenever possible...
Objective: Evaluate the feasibility of crossing a mountain stream or river safely.

Procedure:
1. Treat all rivers and streams as danger areas due to their potential strength and unpredictability.
2. Avoid crossing whenever possible unless absolutely necessary for mission success.
3. If a crossing is required, consider the following factors:
- Type of stream: Determine whether it is shallow or deep, fast or slow flowing.
- Width of the stream: Assess if the width allows safe crossing without additional equipment.
- Speed and depth of water: Ensure that the current is not too strong and the depth does not exceed waist level (as covered in this chapter).
4. Conduct a thorough reconnaissance of potential crossing sites to identify hazards such as submerged obstacles, deep pockets, or unstable banks.
5. If the stream is too wide, swift, or deep for safe crossing using standard techniques, consider an alternate route or request engineering support for bridging operations (e.g., use of rafts or boats).
stream crossing, river crossing, mountain terrain, reconnaissance
Team Crossing Technique for Stream Navigation
Standard Source: FM 3 97 61 Military Mountaineering
When the water level begins to reach thigh deep, or anytime the current is too swift for personnel to safely perform an individual crossing, a team crossing may be used.

### Chain Crossing Method:
1. Forming the Line:
- Two or more...
When the water level begins to reach thigh deep, or anytime the current is too swift for personnel to safely perform an individual crossing, a team crossing may be used.

### Chain Crossing Method:
1. Forming the Line:
- Two or more individuals cross arms with each other and lock their hands in front of themselves (see Figure 9-3).
- The line formed faces the far bank.
- The largest individual should be on the upstream end of the line to break the current for the group.

2. Crossing Technique:
- The line will move across the stream using the same principles as for individual crossings, but with the added support of each other.
- The line should cross parallel to the direction of the current.
- The team still moves at a slight downstream angle, stepping off with the downstream foot in the lead.

### Notes:
• This method is particularly useful for crossing swift or deep streams where individual crossings may be unsafe.
• Ensure all members are coordinated and maintain balance throughout the crossing.
team crossing, chain method, stream navigation, military mountaineering, group crossing technique

Training Setup (2) -

Navigation Course Setup for Evaluation
Reference Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Procedure:

1. Select an area with varied terrain and vegetation that is large enough to accommodate three to five points spaced between 1,000 and 5,000 meters apart.

2. Ensure each point is on or near an **identifiable terrain...
Procedure:

1. Select an area with varied terrain and vegetation that is large enough to accommodate three to five points spaced between 1,000 and 5,000 meters apart.

2. Ensure each point is on or near an identifiable terrain feature.

3. Mark each correct point on the ground with a sign containing a letter or number.

4. Place dummy signs between 100 and 200 meters to the right and left of the correct points to test accuracy.

5. Clearly mark all correct points on the map for reference.

6. Prepare a sheet of paper with the azimuth and distance for each leg of the course.

7. Provide pencils for the Soldier to use during the evaluation.
navigation course setup, evaluation preparation, terrain feature marking, training materials
Set Up Navigation Training Area
Reference Source: STP 21 1 Warrior Skills Level 1
Steps:
1. Select an area with varying terrain and vegetation that is large enough to have two points 1,000 to 2,000 meters apart.
2. Ensure each point is on or near an identifiable terrain feature and mark them on the ground with a sign...
Steps:
1. Select an area with varying terrain and vegetation that is large enough to have two points 1,000 to 2,000 meters apart.
2. Ensure each point is on or near an identifiable terrain feature and mark them on the ground with a sign containing a letter or number.
3. Place dummy signs not less than 100 meters nor more than 200 meters to the right and left of the correct point.
4. Clearly mark correct points on the map.
5. Prepare a sheet of paper giving the azimuth and distance for each leg of the course to be covered.
6. Have pencils available for the tested Soldier.
navigation training setup, terrain feature marking, dummy signs, map preparation

Tracking And Navigation (2) -

Determine Direction of Tracked Vehicle Tracks
Standard Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
To determine the direction of travel for tracked vehicles:

• Examine the tracks left by snowmobiles or tracked vehicles.
• Look for plates formed inside the track where the snow has been compressed.
• At ground level, the majority of these...
To determine the direction of travel for tracked vehicles:

• Examine the tracks left by snowmobiles or tracked vehicles.
• Look for plates formed inside the track where the snow has been compressed.
• At ground level, the majority of these plates will face the direction of travel, although a small number may face in the opposite direction.
tracked vehicle,direction determination,snowmobile tracks,navigation,plates
Use Tracking Teams for Sign Cutting
Standard Source: USMC Winter Survival Course Handbook
To use tracking teams effectively for sign cutting:

1. Document and sketch the initial track with all tracking teams (minimum of two).
2. The first team continues to track until another team has positively found the same track further ahead on...
To use tracking teams effectively for sign cutting:

1. Document and sketch the initial track with all tracking teams (minimum of two).
2. The first team continues to track until another team has positively found the same track further ahead on the trail.
3. The last track is marked for future reference.
4. The second team assumes responsibility for locating each track until they are radioed by the first team that "they have found the track".
5. This process is called sign cutting, and it involves making large sweeping arcs ahead of the primary tracking team until the track is located.
6. Once the track is located, teams change roles by leap frogging.
7. If the track is lost or misidentified, the teams will move back to the last marked track.
tracking teams,sign cutting,navigation,leap frogging,track identification

Surveying Layout (2) -

Lay Out a Simple Rectangle Using an Engineer's Transit or Leveling Instrument
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
a. Establish baseline AB from a reference monument or bench mark, or a fixed line such as a street curb, and then locate point C and D on the baseline by measurement.

b. With the transit at point C, backsight on point B, turn a 90° angle,...
a. Establish baseline AB from a reference monument or bench mark, or a fixed line such as a street curb, and then locate point C and D on the baseline by measurement.

b. With the transit at point C, backsight on point B, turn a 90° angle, and locate and place corner stakes E and F by measurement and determine their elevation.

c. Move transit to point D, backsight on point A, turn a 90° angle, and locate and place corner stakes G and H by measurement and determine their elevation.

d. Install batter boards and wire to retain the layout during excavation and construction.

e. Check the squareness of the layout by measuring the length of the diagonals, EH and FG and comparing it with the length computed by where c is the computed diagonal length and a and b are the lengths of the two sides (see calculation of diagonal, fig. 1-6). The squareness can also be checked by using a 3 - 4 - 5 right triangle or carpenter's square to check the 90° angle at the corners.
lay out, rectangle, engineer's transit, leveling instrument, surveying, construction layout
Lay Out an Irregularly Shaped Project Using Surveying Techniques
Standard Source: US Army Frame Structures
When the outline of the building is not a simple rectangle, the procedure in establishing each point is the same as described above, except that more points have to be located and the checking of the work is more likely to reveal a small error....
When the outline of the building is not a simple rectangle, the procedure in establishing each point is the same as described above, except that more points have to be located and the checking of the work is more likely to reveal a small error. After having established the baseline, it is usually advisable with an irregularly shaped building to lay out first a large rectangle which will comprise the entire building or a greater part of it. This is shown in figure 1-37 as rectangle HQRS. Having once established this accurately, the remaining portion of the layout will consist of small rectangles, each of which can be laid out and checked separately.
irregular shape, surveying, construction layout, building outline, large rectangle

Weather Signs (2) -

Determine Wind Direction Using Natural Methods
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Use dry grass or light dust:
- Throw up small bits of dry grass or hold a handful of light dust and let it fall.
- The direction the grass or dust moves indicates the wind's direction.

2. Use your thumb:
- Wet...
Procedure:

1. Use dry grass or light dust:
- Throw up small bits of dry grass or hold a handful of light dust and let it fall.
- The direction the grass or dust moves indicates the wind's direction.

2. Use your thumb:
- Wet your thumb all around with water.
- Let the wind blow over it; the side that feels colder is the direction from which the wind is coming.

Notes:
• This method works best on calm days when wind movement is subtle.
wind direction,determine wind,survival navigation
Interpret Clouds for Weather Forecasting
Standard Source: Boy Scout Handbook 1911
Procedure:

1. Observe cloud formations to predict weather:
- Low clouds moving swiftly: Indicate coolness and rain.
- Hard-edged clouds: Signal wind.
- Rolled or jagged clouds: Suggest strong wind.
- **Mackerel sky...
Procedure:

1. Observe cloud formations to predict weather:
- Low clouds moving swiftly: Indicate coolness and rain.
- Hard-edged clouds: Signal wind.
- Rolled or jagged clouds: Suggest strong wind.
- Mackerel sky (clouds resembling fish scales): Predict rain within 12 hours.

2. Look for other weather signs:
- A slack rope tightening indicates rain is coming.
- Smoke falling downward suggests rain.
- A red sun in the morning or a pale yellow/greenish sunset means rain is likely.
- A circle around the moon signals an approaching storm.

3. Use traditional sayings:
- *"The evening red, the morning gray sets the traveler on his way; the evening gray, the morning red brings down showers upon his head."*
- *"When the grass is dry at night, look for rain before the light."*
- *"When the grass is dry in the morning, look for rain before the night."*
- *"When the dew is on the grass, rain will never come to pass."*

Notes:
• East wind brings lengthy rain.
• A sudden shower passes quickly.
• Slow rain lasts long.
• Rain before 7 AM means clear weather by 11 AM.
• Heavy morning fog generally indicates a clear day.
cloud observation,weather signs,survival navigation,forecasting

Map Reading Techniques (2) -

Determine the Aspect of Slope for Navigation in Mountainous Terrain
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Purpose: To determine the aspect of slope using a compass to aid navigation when visibility is poor.

Procedure:
1. Take a compass reading along an imaginary line that runs straight down the slope, ensuring it cuts through each contour...
Purpose: To determine the aspect of slope using a compass to aid navigation when visibility is poor.

Procedure:
1. Take a compass reading along an imaginary line that runs straight down the slope, ensuring it cuts through each contour line at approximately a 90-degree angle.
2. Use this compass reading to determine the direction of the slope (aspect) based on your current location and the map.
3. This information helps you keep track of your position and guide cross-country movement, even in poor visibility conditions.
4. Combine this technique with other navigation methods for improved accuracy.
slope aspect, compass reading, navigation, map reading
Use an Altimeter for Navigation in Mountainous Terrain
Standard Source: FM 3 25 26 Map Reading Land Navigation
Purpose: To use an altimeter to determine elevation and pinpoint location on a map during navigation.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the altimeter is calibrated correctly, with markings down to 10 or 20 meters of elevation.
2. Use the altimeter...
Purpose: To use an altimeter to determine elevation and pinpoint location on a map during navigation.

Procedure:
1. Ensure the altimeter is calibrated correctly, with markings down to 10 or 20 meters of elevation.
2. Use the altimeter to measure your current elevation by reading the scale, which measures air pressure and converts it into an elevation value in feet or meters.
3. Combine this elevation data with a directional reading from the map (such as a bearing) to perform ressection and pinpoint your location on the map.
4. This technique is especially useful when navigating through areas with rapid changes in elevation, where traditional resection methods may be less effective.
5. Regularly check and recalibrate the altimeter for accuracy, especially during prolonged use or in rapidly changing weather conditions.
altimeter use, elevation measurement, navigation, map reading

Air Assault Formations (2) -

Air Assault Formations - Staggered Trail Left/Right
High Priority Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Purpose: Deploy forces in a formation that allows rapid deployment for all-round security with simplified pre-positioning.

Procedure:
1. Use Staggered Trail Left or Right formation when:
- A relatively long, wide landing area is...
Purpose: Deploy forces in a formation that allows rapid deployment for all-round security with simplified pre-positioning.

Procedure:
1. Use Staggered Trail Left or Right formation when:
- A relatively long, wide landing area is available.
- Rapid deployment of forces to the flank and front is required.
- Simplified pre-positioning of loads is needed.
2. This formation allows for all-round security but restricts suppressive fire somewhat by gunners.

Notes: Use this formation when perimeter defense or multi-directional operations are needed.
staggered trail left/right formation, air assault tactics, landing zone deployment
Air Assault Formations - Trail
High Priority Source: SH 21 76 Ranger Handbook
Purpose: Deploy forces in a formation that allows rapid deployment to the flank with simplified pre-positioning.

Procedure:
1. Use Trail formation when:
- A relatively small landing area is available.
- Rapid deployment of forces...
Purpose: Deploy forces in a formation that allows rapid deployment to the flank with simplified pre-positioning.

Procedure:
1. Use Trail formation when:
- A relatively small landing area is available.
- Rapid deployment of forces to the flank is required.
- Simplified pre-positioning of loads is needed.
2. This formation allows for unrestricted suppressive fire by gunners and simplifies load management.

Notes: Use this formation when speed and efficiency are critical.
trail formation, air assault tactics, landing zone deployment

Map Reading And Compass Use (1) -

Use a Map and Compass to Avoid Getting Lost
High Priority Source: How To Avoid Getting Lost
To avoid getting lost in combat or during outdoor activities, follow these steps:

1. Use a map: A map is a drawing of a section of the earth's surface as seen from above. It shows man-made objects such as roads, buildings, and bridges.
2....
To avoid getting lost in combat or during outdoor activities, follow these steps:

1. Use a map: A map is a drawing of a section of the earth's surface as seen from above. It shows man-made objects such as roads, buildings, and bridges.
2. Understand the legend: The legend, located in the lower left-hand corner of every map, explains symbols used on the map. This includes:
- Symbols for man-made objects
- Color coding used on the map
- Other symbols that help you understand what the ground looks like.
3. Use a compass: A compass is an essential tool for finding directions and navigating without getting lost.
4. Apply common sense: Use your judgment to avoid situations where you might become disoriented or separated from your group.

Additional Information:
• On maps, a lane is generally considered as being a minimum of 2.5 meters (8 feet) in width.
• In developed areas, only through roads are classified.
map,compass,navigation,legend,symbol,color coding,common sense

Alternative Navigation (1) -

Use Stereoscopic Vision for Navigation Without Equipment
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. Understand the Principle of Stereoscopic Vision:
- The human eye can perceive three-dimensional images from pairs of photographs without optical equipment.
- This ability may be useful in unknown terrain where maps or...
Procedure:

1. Understand the Principle of Stereoscopic Vision:
- The human eye can perceive three-dimensional images from pairs of photographs without optical equipment.
- This ability may be useful in unknown terrain where maps or compasses are unavailable.

2. Practice with Aerial Photographs:
- Use aerial or other pairs of photographs to train your eyes to view the subject in true three dimensions.
- This skill can help you interpret landscapes and navigate effectively.

3. Apply Stereoscopic Vision in Real-World Navigation:
- In survival situations, use this technique to understand terrain features and plan a route.
- This method is especially useful when traditional navigation tools are not available.
stereoscopic vision, navigation without equipment, aerial photography, survival navigation

Stereoscopic Navigation (1) -

Identify Left Eye Image in Stereoscopic Pictures
Reference Source: the ten bushcraft books
To determine which image is for the left eye:

1. Select two identifiable points that are similar in each photo of the pair.
- One point should be a black spot on line YY crossline 1, located at the end of a mountain range.
- The other...
To determine which image is for the left eye:

1. Select two identifiable points that are similar in each photo of the pair.
- One point should be a black spot on line YY crossline 1, located at the end of a mountain range.
- The other point should be a white spot to the right where a little river joins the main stream on line XX crossline 2.

2. Ensure one point is near the highest elevation and the other is near the lowest elevation.
3. Align the points as close to a vertical line as possible for accurate comparison.
4. Observe parallax: In the left eye image, Y and X are closer together than in the right eye image.

Additional Notes:
• The two pictures must be equal in print density and placed side by side for viewing.
• For comfortable stereoscopic viewing with unaided eyes, the maximum distance between two points should not exceed 3 inches (7.5 cm). With strain, separations up to 4 inches (10 cm) may be fused.

Creating 3D Pictures: You can make three-dimensional pictures using a single lens camera by taking two photos from different positions with the same exposure and aperture. The distance between these positions should be:
• Not less than 9 inches (22.5 cm)
• Not more than 12 inches (30 cm)
for every 30 feet (9 meters) of distance to the nearest object in the foreground.

Explanation: The parallax is the angle between the point closest to and farthest from the camera. Compare this with reversed photos to observe differences in spacing, such as the space between A and B.
stereoscopic images,left eye image,right eye image,parallax,aerial photography,navigation

Route Logging (1) -

Logging Your Route and Making a Chart
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
A log is a record of essential information during your journey, including distances, bearings, and any other helpful notes. Here's how to create one:

### Calculating Distances
• Distance is calculated based on rate of travel and time.
• Rate of...
A log is a record of essential information during your journey, including distances, bearings, and any other helpful notes. Here's how to create one:

### Calculating Distances
• Distance is calculated based on rate of travel and time.
• Rate of travel varies depending on terrain:
- Open country: 15–20 minutes per mile (1.6 km)
- Scrubby or rocky terrain: 24–30 minutes per mile
- Jungle or steep terrain: 30–40 minutes per mile
- Very steep slopes: 60–90 minutes per mile
• These times are for an active person with a 30 to 40 lb. (15 to 20 kg) pack.
• With heavier loads, the maximum time applies.

### Measuring Rate of Travel
• On level ground: 110 paces = 100 yards (metres)
• Multiply the time to walk 100 yards by 17.5 to get your speed per mile (or multiply time for 100 metres by 10 for km).
• On steep or rocky ground: 150–170 paces = 100 yards (metres), and up to 200+ paces on very steep slopes.

### Recording Information
Use a log with the following columns:

|Time|Rate of Travel|Distance Miles|True Bearing|Observation|
|---|---|---|---|---|
|08.30|3½|3|84|Open grassland very good walking. High ranges to east about 4 miles. River 1 mile south.|
|||||Climbed steep saddle to range crest. Very rough and|
|09.30|2|1|197|stony. Grade on top 1-8. River ½ mile south at foot of|
|||||range.|
|||||Range crest stony underfoot. Many small crests to be|
|10.00|2½|3¾|110|climbed. Sides too steep to detour. River going south-|
|||||east along foot of range. Range rising to east.|

### Plotting the Chart
• After recording, plot this information on a map or chart.
• This chart helps you always know your location in relation to where you started.
• Early explorers used this method to navigate unknown lands.
logging route, navigation chart, distance calculation, rate of travel, map plotting

Route Choice (1) -

Choosing a Route in Cross-Country Travel
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
When choosing a route during cross-country travel, follow these guidelines to avoid getting lost:

### Preferred Route
• Choose routes that go up spurs and ranges, and down streams.
• This reduces the chance of becoming lost because all spurs...
When choosing a route during cross-country travel, follow these guidelines to avoid getting lost:

### Preferred Route
• Choose routes that go up spurs and ranges, and down streams.
• This reduces the chance of becoming lost because all spurs lead to main range crests, and all streams lead to main river courses.
• Avoid going down spurs or up rivers, as this can easily lead you off course and into dense bush.

### Planning Your Route
• Study your map carefully before setting out.
• Plan a route that follows the general rule: go up spurs and down rivers if possible.

### Avoiding Common Mistakes
• Traveling down a series of spurs or following an incorrect watercourse can lead to being hopelessly bushed (lost in dense vegetation).
• Use a sketch map to visualize your route and avoid these pitfalls.

### Example
• If traveling from point A to B, choose the route C rather than D on the sketch map provided. Route C follows spurs and rivers, while route D leads into potential confusion.
cross-country travel, route selection, avoiding getting lost, navigation principles

Sun Time Measurement (1) -

Measure Time Using the Sun and Compass
High Priority Source: the ten bushcraft books
To measure time using the sun and compass:

1. Determine your true North-South line using a compass.
2. Account for magnetic variation from True North (if known).
3. Observe the position of the sun relative to the North-South line.
4....
To measure time using the sun and compass:

1. Determine your true North-South line using a compass.
2. Account for magnetic variation from True North (if known).
3. Observe the position of the sun relative to the North-South line.
4. Use the following method to estimate degrees:
- Hand at full arm's length, fingers widely spread: 22 degrees
- Thumb turned in: 15 degrees
- Closed fist: 8 degrees
- From second knuckle to edge of fist: 3 degrees
- Between two centre knuckles: 2 degrees
5. Measure the number of degrees the sun is from the North-South line.
6. Multiply the number of degrees by four to obtain the number of minutes before or after noon.
time measurement,sun navigation,compass use,degrees calculation

Sun Movement (1) -

Understand Sun Movement for Navigation
High Priority Source: the ten bushcraft books
Understanding sun movement:

1. The sun crosses the North-South line (Meridian) daily at its highest point (Zenith).
2. When the sun is at its highest point in the sky, it is North or South of you, depending on your location and the sun's...
Understanding sun movement:

1. The sun crosses the North-South line (Meridian) daily at its highest point (Zenith).
2. When the sun is at its highest point in the sky, it is North or South of you, depending on your location and the sun's position relative to the equator.
3. There are 24 hours between each sun crossing of the North-South line.
4. The Earth rotates 360 degrees in 24 hours, which means:
- It moves 15 degrees per hour
- Or one degree every four minutes
5. This knowledge is useful for measuring time and direction accurately.
sun movement,navigation,earth rotation,time measurement

Definitions (1) -

Define Time and Direction for Navigation
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Definitions:

• Time: A method of measuring the intervals between events. The most regular event is the movement of the sun, used for everyday purposes.
• Direction: The line or course to be taken, typically measured from North or...
Definitions:

• Time: A method of measuring the intervals between events. The most regular event is the movement of the sun, used for everyday purposes.
• Direction: The line or course to be taken, typically measured from North or one of the cardinal points (East, South, West).
• Stars provide a more accurate method of measurement and are used by navigators and astronomers.
time definition,direction definition,navigation basics

Bearing Measurement (1) -

Determine Bearings Using True North
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. Face East and stretch your left arm toward true North.
2. Raise your right arm along your side until both arms form a straight line.
3. Your right arm now points to South (or 180° True).
4. Bring the two arms together evenly, and...
Procedure:

1. Face East and stretch your left arm toward true North.
2. Raise your right arm along your side until both arms form a straight line.
3. Your right arm now points to South (or 180° True).
4. Bring the two arms together evenly, and you will be pointing to East (or 90° True).
5. Use these cardinal points as references to measure other bearings within five degrees of error.

Notes:
• For bearings greater than 180°, face West and point your right arm toward North and left arm toward South.
bearings, cardinal points, true north, navigation

Time Correction (1) -

Calculate Longitude Correction for Solar Time
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
Procedure:

1. Determine your location's longitude relative to the standard time meridian for your region.
2. Identify whether you are East or West of the standard time meridian:
- If you are East, your solar time is ahead of...
Procedure:

1. Determine your location's longitude relative to the standard time meridian for your region.
2. Identify whether you are East or West of the standard time meridian:
- If you are East, your solar time is ahead of standard time.
- If you are West, your solar time is behind standard time.
3. Apply the following corrections based on your position relative to the standard time meridian:
- For each degree East, deduct 4 minutes from solar time.
- For each degree West, add 4 minutes to solar time.

Example:
• If you are located at 15°E and the standard time is based on 0° (Greenwich Mean Time), your correction would be:
- You are East by 15 degrees → Deduct 4 minutes × 15 = 60 minutes from solar time.

Notes:
• This correction is essential for converting solar time to standard time accurately.
• These corrections are also necessary when using a sun compass to determine directions and local standard time without equipment.
• Accuracy can be achieved within two minutes of local standard time and a bearing error of half a degree.
longitude correction, solar time, standard time, navigation, sun compass

Star Navigation (1) -

Using Star Maps to Locate Stars in the Sky
Standard Source: the ten bushcraft books
To locate a star using a star map and determine its position in the sky:

Step 1: Identify the overhead point
• Stand upright with your head thrown well back.
• Rotate your body through half-circles until you observe stars moving in arcs.
• The...
To locate a star using a star map and determine its position in the sky:

Step 1: Identify the overhead point
• Stand upright with your head thrown well back.
• Rotate your body through half-circles until you observe stars moving in arcs.
• The center of these arcs is the point directly overhead.

Step 2: Use a star map to identify the star
• Match the star's position on the map with its apparent location in the sky.
• Ensure the star is not significantly East or West, but rather North or South of your position.

Step 3: Determine the star’s Right Ascension (RA)
• Locate the RA number on the star map. For example, if a star is on radial line 16 hours 25 minutes, this is its RA at Greenwich on September 21st.

Step 4: Apply date corrections to RA
• Add two hours per calendar month or half an hour per week and four minutes per day from the last September 21st until your current date.

Step 5: Calculate local time
• Add the correction factor (from Step 4) to the RA. If the result exceeds 24 hours, subtract 24 hours.
• Finally, adjust for your longitude to determine the local time at which the star is overhead.
star map,right ascension,declination,navigation,star identification
